details.ground_truth: A former Boston TV news anchor says that last year, when her son was 18, he was at a bar in Natucket, Mass., when he met Kevin Spacey. The actor "bought him drink after drink after drink and when my son was drunk, Spacey made his move and sexually assaulted him," said Heather Unruh at a press conference Wednesday. She says Spacey stuck his hand down her son's pants and onto his genitals, and tried to convince him to go to a party where they could continue to drink, USA Today reports. A bystander asked the teen if he was OK and the teen said no, at which point the woman urged the teen to flee and he took her advice, Unruh said. Unruh, who appeared with an attorney, says her son filed a police report last week and handed over evidence; she says a criminal investigation has been opened but the Nantucket Police Department has not confirmed that. The attorney with Unruh noted that her son's accusations are "well within" both the civil and criminal statutes of limitations. USA Today has a list of the 14 people so far accusing Spacey of sexual assault or harassment. Meanwhile Jon Bernthal, who worked with Spacey on Baby Driver, told a SiriusXM radio show Tuesday that he lost respect for Spacey while working on the film. "I thought he was a bit of a bully," Bernthal said, per People. "I didn’t really care for the way he was behaving toward some of the other people on set."

**Mali Crisis: A Threat to West Africa and the World**

A recent coup in Mali has sparked a crisis that threatens not only the stability of the West African country but also the entire region. The coup, led by a group of disgruntled soldiers, overthrew the democratically elected government of President Amadou Toumani Touré on March 22. The power vacuum created by the coup has allowed a Tuareg rebellion to gain control of the northern part of the country, including the key towns of Gao, Kidal, and Timbuktu.

**The Tuareg Rebellion**

The Tuareg rebellion, led by the National Movement for the Liberation of Azawad (MNLA), is seeking to establish an independent independent state called Azawad. However, another group, Ansar Dine, linked to the North African branch of al-Qaeda, wants to impose Islamic law in the region. The rebels have taken advantage of the political situation and made rapid advances in the past few days, with residents in Gao and Kidal reporting that Islamist fighters have ransacked bars serving alcohol and banned Western music on local airwaves.

**International Response**

The international community has been quick to respond to the crisis, with the African Union and West African nations imposing sanctions on Mali. The sanctions include a ban on the importation of fuel, which is trucked in overland from neighboring Ivory Coast and Senegal. The country's electricity grid is also expected to falter in coming weeks, with April being one of the hottest months of the year. The Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) has given the junta a 72-hour deadline to restore civilian rule, which expired on Monday.

**Humanitarian Crisis**

The crisis in Mali has exacerbated a humanitarian crisis in the region, with aid agencies warning that 13 million people need food aid following a drought in the region. Since January, the violence has uprooted more than 200,000 people, including around 100,000 who have fled the country. The UN Security Council is due to hold an emergency meeting on the crisis in Mali later on Tuesday.

**UNESCO Warning**

The World Heritage Site of Timbuktu, a city on the edge of the Sahara desert, is at risk of being damaged by the fighting in the region. The UN's cultural agency, UNESCO, has warned that the city's "outstanding architectural wonders" must be safeguarded. Timbuktu is home to about 700,000 ancient
details.ground_truth: Since we last checked in, the situation in Mali following a soldiers' coup has spiraled, with rising concerns over food and gas shortages, violence, and indeed the country's "very existence," according to Le Monde. An update: The coup has driven more than 200,000 people from their homes to other parts of the region, the AP reports. The UN worries that major food shortages could be imminent, and "mayhem in these towns and cities is increasing," says a rep. Neighboring countries have established an embargo against the rebels who ousted the president; they've closed off their borders and frozen the country's regional bank account. That has prompted residents of Mali, which imports all its fuel, to rush to gas stations to collect fuel. The country's electricity grid may also be headed for failure. UNESCO is citing a threat to Timbuktu, a World Heritage Site full of "architectural wonders," notes the BBC. Mosques there are "essential to the preservation of the identity of the people of Mali," says the group. Yet "nothing seems to be able to stop" the Tuareg coup, Le Monde reports. West African leaders need to help Mali's military fight back before the instability spreads beyond the country's borders.

**Global Efforts to Eradicate Child Institutionalization by 2050**

A recent campaign launched by author and philanthropist JK Rowling, #LetstalkLumos, aims to raise awareness about the global issue of child institutionalization. According to Lumos, a charity dedicated to closing child institutions and orphanages, there are approximately 8 million children worldwide who are separated from their families and locked away in often atrocious conditions.

The practice of institutionalizing children has been shown to cause measurable and sometimes irreparable harm, including malnutrition, developmental delays, and psychological trauma. Children who are institutionalized are also more likely to be abused or trafficked, and are often unable to find employment or reintegrate into their communities as adults.

However, Lumos estimates that the institutionalization of children can be eradicated globally by 2050, based on the successes already achieved in several countries. In Moldova, for example, there has been a 70% reduction in the number of children in institutions since Lumos began working there in 2007. In the Czech Republic, Lumos's demonstration area has seen a 75% fall in admissions, and in Bulgaria, the number of children in institutions has reduced by 54% since Lumos began working there.

International donors play a vital role in supporting the efforts to eradicate child institutionalization. The issues they choose to fund and the principles they promote greatly influence what support is available to children and families. Ending the practice of keeping children in institutions not only makes excellent economic sense, but is also a moral imperative.

JK Rowling, who has committed to becoming president of Lumos for life, has stated that it is her dream that, within her lifetime, the very concept of taking a child away from its family and locking it away will seem to belong to a cruel, fictional world. The #LetstalkLumos campaign is an important step towards making this dream a reality, and raising awareness about the global issue of child institutionalization.

**Key Statistics:**

* 8 million children worldwide are separated from their families and locked away in institutions
* Children who are institutionalized are more likely to be abused or trafficked, and are often unable to find employment or reintegrate into their communities as adults
* Lumos estimates that the institutionalization of children can be eradicated globally by 2050
* In Moldova, there has been a 70% reduction in the number of children in institutions since Lumos began working there in 2007
* In the Czech Republic,
details.ground_truth: JK Rowling makes the case today that millions of kids around the world are living in appalling conditions—the stuff of Grimms' fairytales—in institutions that are supposed to be caring for them. Rowling wants to change that, not by improving these orphanages with "pretty murals" or teddy bears, but by eliminating them altogether, she writes in the Guardian. The big challenge is providing support for often-poor families who think they have no choice but to place their child in such an institution. If a child's biological family isn't up to the task, then foster families can step in. But Rowling argues that institutions are absolutely the wrong answer. Rowling has started a charity called Lumos (yes, after one of her Harry Potter spells), and writes that it has made genuine progress in reducing new placements in countries such as Bulgaria, Moldova, and the Czech Republic. She wants to raise awareness and donations with campaigns like this one. "I recently committed to becoming president of Lumos for life," writes Rowling. "It is my dream that, within my lifetime, the very concept of taking a child away from its family and locking it away will seem to belong to a cruel, fictional world." Click for her full column.

**US and Pakistan Agree to Cooperate on Future Operations Against "High-Value Targets"**

In a bid to ease tensions and strengthen their alliance, the United States and Pakistan have agreed to work together on future operations against "high-value targets" in Pakistan. The announcement was made in a joint statement following a visit by US Senator John Kerry to Pakistan.

**Key Developments:**

1. **Return of US Helicopter Tail:**** Pakistan has agreed to return the tail of a US military helicopter that was damaged during the raid that killed Osama bin Laden. The handover is seen as a significant step towards improving relations between the two countries.
2. **Increased Intelligence Sharing and Joint Operations:**** The two countries have committed to increase intelligence sharing and joint operations to defeat their common enemies. This includes cooperation on operations against "high-value targets" in Pakistan.
3. **US Economic Assistance Package:**** US Senator John Kerry, who chairs the US Senate's Foreign Relations Committee, has conveyed to Pakistani leaders that they must demonstrate a commitment to fighting Islamist militancy to address the concerns of members of Congress who have called for an end to US aid.
4. **No Designs on Nuclear Arsenal:**** The US has assured Pakistan that it has "no designs" on its nuclear arsenal, which is a key concern for Pakistan.
5. **Drone Strikes:**** The issue of drone strikes remains a contentious one, with Pakistan tacitly allowing but publicly protesting them.

**Context:**

The US-Pakistan relationship has been strained since the US raid that killed Osama bin Laden in May. Pakistan has been angry about not being informed in advance, while US officials have questioned whether Pakistani officials colluded with bin Laden. The relationship has reached one of its worst points, with Pakistan's assistance being crucial to a potential negotiated end to the war in Afghanistan.

**Conclusion:**

The agreement to cooperate on future operations against "high-value targets" is a significant step towards improving the US-Pakistan relationship. However, the issues of drone strikes, intelligence sharing, and joint operations remain contentious ones that need to be addressed. The US and Pakistan must work together to defeat their common enemies and strengthen their alliance.
details.ground_truth: The US and Pakistan have agreed to work together in the future against "high value targets" in Pakistan, the countries announced in a joint statement today. The news comes after Sen. John Kerry arrived in Pakistan last night, intending to send a strong message following the death of Osama bin Laden: Cooperate more when it comes to rooting out terrorists, or face "profound" changes in the US-Pakistan relationship—which could include kissing billions in aid goodbye. Kerry is the most senior US official to go to Pakistan since bin Laden's death, but since issuing the joint pledge, he said Secretary of State Hillary Clinton will soon announce her own plans to visit, the AP reports. Kerry's meetings with Pakistani leaders began last night, the Washington Post reports. Many in Washington think Pakistan is harboring Islamist militants, and some members of Congress are calling for the US to sever the billions of dollars in aid it provides to Pakistan. Kerry is in Pakistan to lay out the new stakes following bin Laden's death, and question officials about how bin Laden was able to hide in Pakistan for years. In Kabul, Kerry said there is "some evidence" that the Pakistan government has knowledge of insurgent activities, calling it "very disturbing."

**North Korea's VX Nerve Agent Attack: A Diplomatic Crisis Unfolds**

In a shocking and deadly attack, North Korea's half-brother, Kim Jong-nam, was killed at the Kuala Lumpur airport in Malaysia using the banned chemical weapon VX nerve agent. The incident has sparked a diplomatic crisis between Malaysia and North Korea, with each country accusing the other of wrongdoing.

**The Attack**

According to Malaysian police, two women, one Indonesian and one Vietnamese, coated their hands with chemicals and wiped them on Kim's face on February 13. He sought help from airport staff but fell into convulsions and died on the way to the hospital within two hours of the attack.

**The VX Nerve Agent**

VX is a highly toxic and potent nerve agent that can be administered through the skin. It is banned under the Chemical Weapons Convention, which North Korea never signed. The country is believed to have the capacity to produce up to 4,500 metric tons of chemical weapons during a typical year, which it could increase to 12,000 tons per year during a period of crisis.

**North Korea's Chemical Capabilities**

North Korea is suspected of being particularly focused on mustard, phosgene, sarin, and V-type chemical agents, including VX. The country's development of such agents has been of special concern because of fears it might try to put them in artillery shells for an attack on South Korea's capital, potentially threatening the lives of millions.

**The Diplomatic Crisis**

North Korea has denounced Malaysia's investigation as full of "holes and contradictions" and accused the authorities here of being in cahoots with Pyongyang's enemies. Malaysian Foreign Minister Anifah Aman warned North Korean envoy Kang Chol on Friday that he would be expelled unless he stopped "spewing lies" about the attack.

**The Investigation**

Malaysian police are still investigating how the lethal agent entered Malaysia. The airport had not been decontaminated, and it is feared that the nerve agent could have contaminated not only the airport but anyplace else Kim had been, including medical facilities and the ambulance he was transported in.

**The Aftermath**

The death of Kim Jong-nam has marked a serious turnaround in relations between Malaysia and North Korea. While Malaysia isn't one of Pyongyang's key diplomatic partners, it is one of the few places in the world where North Koreans can travel without a visa. As a result, for years, it's been a quiet destination for Northerners looking for jobs, schools,
details.ground_truth: Kim Jong Nam's assassins killed him with a banned chemical weapon, Malaysian police revealed Friday. The country's police chief said toxicology reports on swabs from the face and eyes of the exiled North Korean found VX nerve agent, which the BBC notes is classed as a weapon of mass destruction by the United Nations. He said that one of two women believed to have rubbed the extremely toxic substance on Kim's face with their hands suffered from vomiting after the attack. The New York Times reports that VX agent can be created by mixing two compounds—and police suspect the two women put the substances on Kim's face, one after the other, to create a deadly dose. The police chief said the airport where Kim was attacked is now being decontaminated. North Korea—which is widely suspected to have been behind the killing of leader Kim Jong Un's half brother—never signed the Chemical Weapons Convention that banned VX, the AP notes. Pyongyang denies involvement and says Malaysia's investigation is full of "holes and contradictions." The father of one of the two women being held, Vietnamese citizen Doan Thi Huong, tells the Times his daughter trained as a pharmacist and he has seen little of her in recent years. (Police say that after Malaysia refused to give Kim's body to North Korean diplomats, somebody tried to break into the morgue.)

**RICHMOND HIGH SCHOOL RAPE AND BEATING CASE: A SUMMARIZED ACCOUNT**

On October 24, 2009, a 15-year-old sophomore girl was brutally gang-raped and beaten on the grounds of Richmond High School in Richmond, California, after attending the school's homecoming dance. The incident has sent shockwaves throughout the community and raised questions about campus security and the bystander effect.

**The Attack**

According to police, the girl had left the dance and was walking to meet her father for a ride home when a classmate invited her to join a group drinking in the courtyard. She quickly became drunk and was then attacked by a group of 7-10 people, ranging in age from 15 to their early 20s, for over two hours at a dimly lit area near benches. As many as two dozen people witnessed the attack without reporting it to the police.

**Arrests and Suspects**

Two suspects have been arrested and charged in connection with the attack. Manuel Ortega, a 19-year-old former student, was arrested as he ran from the scene and is being held on $800,000 bail for investigation of rape and robbery. A 15-year-old student, who knew the victim, was also booked on one count of sexual assault at Contra Costa County Juvenile Hall. A 21-year-old Richmond man was arrested on Tuesday night on suspicion of sexual assault and rape.

**Investigation and Response**

Police are offering a $20,000 reward for information leading to the conviction of any of the assailants. Officers have passed out flyers in the area of the campus and served search warrants at undisclosed locations. The school's surveillance cameras, which were installed after a series of violent crimes, are not working, and a new system is expected to be in place in the next few months. Four police officers and several school security staffers were on hand at the homecoming dance, but two of the staffers left early due to a prior commitment.

**Community Reaction**

The attack has left the community in shock, with many students and parents expressing their outrage and fear. Some students have already left the school in response to the attack, with one student saying, "It's not safe there at all. I'm not going back." The incident has also raised questions about the bystander effect and the need for greater community involvement in reporting crimes.

**Conclusion**

The Richmond High School rape and beating case is a disturbing and tragic reminder of the need for greater community involvement in reporting crimes

**FBI and DOJ Under Scrutiny for Alleged Surveillance Abuses**

The House Intelligence Committee has released a memo, known as the Nunes memo, alleging that the FBI and the Justice Department abused their surveillance authority when they sought a secret court order to monitor a former Trump campaign adviser, Carter Page. The memo claims that the government failed to disclose important information about the source of the information used to obtain the warrant, Christopher Steele, a former FBI source who was paid by the Democratic National Committee and the Hillary Clinton campaign to gather derogatory information on Donald Trump.

**Steele's Bias and Lack of Reliability**

The memo also highlights Steele's bias against Trump and his lack of reliability as a source. Steele had become a less-than-reliable source for the FBI due to his numerous encounters with the media and his failure to maintain confidentiality. Additionally, Steele's wife was employed by Fusion GPS, the company that hired Steele, and his relationship with the company was concealed from the FISC.

**FBI's Handling of the Investigation**

The memo also criticizes the FBI's handling of the investigation, including the decision to brief President-elect Trump on a summary of the Steele dossier, despite its lack of corroboration. Deputy Director McCabe testified that no surveillance warrant would have been sought from the FISC without the Steele dossier information.

**Twitter Location Information**

In a separate development, Twitter has announced that users can now add location information to their tweets, such as their city or precise location. Users also have the option to delete their tweet location history.

**Key Takeaways**

* The FBI and the Justice Department are under scrutiny for alleged surveillance abuses.
* The Nunes memo highlights Steele's bias and lack of reliability as a source.
* The FBI's handling of the investigation is criticized, including the decision to brief President-elect Trump on a summary of the Steele dossier.
* Twitter users can now add location information to their tweets and delete their tweet location history.

**Gerry Adams Released After Four Days of Questioning in Connection with 1972 IRA Murder**

Sinn Fein leader Gerry Adams was released on Sunday evening after four days of questioning by police in Northern Ireland in connection with the 1972 IRA murder of Jean McConville, a 37-year-old widow and mother of 10. Adams, who has consistently denied any involvement in the murder, was arrested under the terrorism act and taken to Antrim police station for questioning.

**Background to the Case**

Jean McConville was abducted from her home in Belfast in December 1972 and shot and secretly buried. Her body was not found until 2003. The case is one of the most high-profile in the "Disappeared" series of cases, in which republicans abducted, murdered, and buried in secret those they suspected of being informers to the British Army.

**Adams' Response to the Arrest**

Adams has criticized the timing of his arrest, which came in the middle of an election campaign. He claimed that police could have used discretion rather than arresting him and questioned the use of "pernicious, coercive legislation" to deal with a legacy issue. Adams also stated that he had contacted police two months ago about the McConville case and had gone voluntarily to the police station for questioning.

**Reaction from Other Parties**

Other political parties have rejected claims that the timing of the arrest was political. The Democratic Unionist Party's Jeffrey Donaldson said that there was nothing within the law of Northern Ireland or the UK that said the police had to suspend the investigation of murder during an election campaign. The Ulster Unionist Party's Mike Nesbitt said that the McConville family's 42-year wait for truth and justice made Adams' complaint over the delay seem churlish.

**Next Steps**

The decision to release Adams means that prosecution lawyers will now decide if charges will be brought. The file sent by the police to the Public Prosecution Service will detail the evidence gathered, and it will be up to PPS lawyers to decide if there is enough evidence to bring any charges and what those charges would be. It could take some time for police to prepare the file for the PPS, with prosecutors then taking a further period to assess the evidence.

The case against Adams is not the only one to be based on the Boston College tapes, a series of candid, confessional interviews with former loyalist and republican paramilitaries designed to be an oral history of the Trou

**Two Daycare Workers Arrested for Allegedly Burning Five Toddlers with Hot Glue Gun**

In a disturbing and shocking incident, two daycare workers, Lizandra Cosme, 32, and Susana Gonzalez, 27, have been arrested and charged in connection with an alleged incident at the Children's Learning Place in Chicago, where five 2-year-old toddlers were injured with hot glue.

According to surveillance footage, Cosme allegedly applied hot glue to the hands and arms of the five children, causing them to grimace in pain and whine. Gonzalez, an assistant at the daycare center, was seen laughing and watching the incident unfold. The extent of the children's injuries is unclear, but prosecutors said that Cosme tried to apply Neosporin to one of the children after she saw circular burn marks starting to appear.

Cosme was denied bail and faces five felony counts of aggravated battery to a child. Gonzalez was charged with five misdemeanor counts of causing the circumstances of endangering a child. The daycare center immediately terminated the two employees and notified the Department of Children and Family Services (DCFS) of the alleged incident.

The DCFS is investigating the allegations of abuse, and the state Department of Children and Family Services is also looking into the matter. Cosme's defense attorney said that his client "screwed up" but did not act out of malice, citing her 12 years of working with young children.

The incident has raised concerns about the safety and well-being of children in daycare centers and has sparked a wider conversation about the importance of proper supervision and care in these settings.
details.ground_truth: Two workers at a Chicago day care were fired and arrested after a disturbing incident that left five toddlers injured. Police say surveillance video captured one of the workers burning the 2-year-olds with a hot glue gun while the other woman watched and laughed, People reports. Lizandra Cosme, 32, has been charged with five counts of aggravated battery of a child causing great bodily harm, the Chicago Sun-Times reports. Susana Gonzalez, 27, faces five misdemeanor charges of causing the circumstances of child endangerment over the Dec. 1 incident at the Children's Place day care. It's not clear how seriously the children were injured. Prosecutors say Cosme, who brought the glue gun for a Christmas project, was captured on video applying the hot glue directly to the hands and arms of the three girls and two boys. "Each of the child victims winced and some whined at the hot glue gun application," a prosecutor said during a Monday court appearance. Prosecutors said Cosme tried to cover up the incident, asking a father whether his child had been burned at home, ABC7 reports. They said her actions were discovered when one child's mother, an ER physician, saw the burns and demanded to see surveillance footage. Cosme's lawyer said his client "screwed up" but did not mean to hurt the children.

**Texas Gubernatorial Candidate Wendy Davis Discloses Abortion History in Memoir**

Texas Democratic gubernatorial candidate Wendy Davis has revealed in her upcoming memoir, "Forgetting to Be Afraid," that she had two abortions, one in 1994 and another in 1996, due to severe fetal abnormalities and a medically necessary ectopic pregnancy.

According to the book, Davis's first abortion was in 1994, when she was a mother of two young girls. She writes that she considered revealing the terminated pregnancy during her nearly 13-hour filibuster in the Texas Senate last summer, but decided against it, saying it would overshadow the events of the day.

The second abortion, in 1996, was due to a severe brain abnormality in the fetus. Davis writes that she could feel the fetus's little body tremble violently and knew that it was suffering, leading her to make the difficult decision to end the pregnancy. She describes the loss as leaving her forever changed and an "indescribable blackness" followed.

Davis's memoir also touches on her hardscrabble life after her father left the family, her struggles to pay the bills as a young mother, and her community college education as the first step on an upward path to Harvard Law School and service on the Fort Worth City Council and in the state Senate.

The disclosure of Davis's abortion history has sparked a mixed reaction from anti-abortion and pro-choice groups. While Texans Right to Life expressed sympathy for Davis's difficult decision, they hoped that all decisions would end in choosing to continue a pregnancy. Cecile Richards, president of Planned Parenthood, praised Davis's "unwavering courage" for sharing her story and shining a light on a subject that is often hidden in shame and stigma.

The revelation is unlikely to lose Davis any votes, according to Rice University political scientist Mark Jones, who said that the group that will be most bothered by her having an abortion of a baby with a severe fetal abnormality is a group that wasn't going to vote for her anyway.

Davis is running for governor against Republican Attorney General Greg Abbott, who is heavily favored to replace Republican Gov. Rick Perry next year.
details.ground_truth: Texas gubernatorial candidate Wendy Davis' new memoir is dedicated in part to daughters Amber, Dru, and Tate, and those familiar with Davis' life story might be puzzled by the last name on that list. That's because Davis is revealing for the first time that Tate is the unborn child that she and her husband aborted in the second trimester, after doctors discovered a severe brain abnormality. The San Antonio Express-News and the AP obtained advanced copies, and the news is generating headlines given that Davis shot to national fame while filibustering against an abortion bill in Texas. Davis writes that she decided to abort in 1996 after doctors said the baby would be blind, deaf, and in a vegetative state if she survived the pregnancy. Davis also felt the fetus “tremble violently" in the womb, "as if someone were applying an electric shock to her, and I knew then what I needed to do. She was suffering." Her doctor "quieted" the unborn child's heart and delivered her by C-section. Davis and her husband had Tate baptized. After the abortion, "an indescribable blackness followed," writes Davis in Forgetting to Be Afraid, "and when I finally did come through it, I emerged a different person." Davis also writes of a previously disclosed procedure to terminate an earlier ectopic pregnancy in which the embryo was implanted outside the uterus. She says she opted not to talk about either case during her famous filibuster because she feared it would "overshadow the events of the day."

**Human Trafficking and Prostitution Ring Bust in Massachusetts**

In a recent investigation, two men, Joseph E. Van Wert, 65, and Randy W. Lambach, 45, were arrested and charged with human trafficking and related crimes for allegedly running a citywide prostitution ring in Pittsfield, Massachusetts. The investigation, which began in the spring of 2017, was sparked by reports of increased prostitution activity in the city.

**The Prostitution Ring**

According to police, Lambach, who had been running the prostitution ring for several years, would seek out women with severe drug addictions and advertise their services on social media. He would set up meetings and rates with prospective clients, and Van Wert would allow some of the meetings to take place in his third-floor apartment at a senior living facility in exchange for drugs and cash.

**The Victims**

The investigation revealed that Lambach had recruited and transported about 10 other women in a similar manner, providing narcotics to those who used drugs to get high before having sex. Some of the women Lambach had recruited were formerly his foster children. A witness, whom Lambach approached with photos of the prostitutes, estimated the age of one of the victims to be about 15.

**The Arrests**

Lambach was arrested without incident on November 14, and Van Wert was arrested shortly thereafter. Both men have pleaded not guilty to their charges. Lambach pleaded not guilty to four counts of human trafficking, while Van Wert pleaded not guilty to conspiracy to commit human trafficking, deriving support from prostitution, maintaining a house of prostitution, and sexual conduct for a fee.

**Ongoing Investigation**

The investigation remains ongoing, and additional charges may be coming. The Pittsfield Police Department detective bureau, anti-crime unit, drug unit, and members of the state police detective unit assigned to the District Attorney's Office are assisting with the investigation.

**Contact Information**

For more information, please contact Bob Dunn at bdunn@berkshireeagle.com, at @BobDunn413 on Twitter, or 413-496-6249.
details.ground_truth: Two men have been charged with human trafficking after police arrested them for running a prostitution ring out of a senior living facility in Pittsfield, Mass. Police say Joseph Van Wert, 65, and Randy Lambach, 45, will face a hearing later this month, the New York Daily News reports. Lambach would allegedly seek out women with drug addictions and post their pictures online, then arrange meetings with clients. Some of these meetings took place in Van Wert's apartment in a senior facility. Van Wert offered his apartment in return for cash or drugs. The Berkshire Eagle reports that the police investigation began last spring in response to citizen complaints about increased prostitution across the city. Lambach would allegedly keep up to 90% of the money the women made, sometimes refusing them cash altogether and paying them in heroin or crack cocaine instead. At least one woman said Lambach drove her to an "appointment" across state lines. Police estimated one of the victims was 15 years old. They were also told that some of the women were Lambach's former foster children. Both men have pleaded not guilty, Lambach to four counts of human trafficking and Van Wert to conspiracy to commit human trafficking, deriving support from prostitution, maintaining a house of prostitution, and sexual conduct for a fee.

**Archaeological Discoveries Unveil the Secrets of Britain's Ancient Past**

In recent weeks, archaeologists have made several groundbreaking discoveries in the Vale of Pewsey, Wiltshire, shedding new light on the lives of Britain's ancient inhabitants. The finds, which include beautifully crafted flint tools, decorated pottery, and a stunning Roman brooch, are part of a three-year excavation project aimed at gaining a better understanding of the people who lived in the areas surrounding Stonehenge.

**Marden Henge: A Monument to Status and Power**

One of the most significant discoveries is the excavation of Marden Henge, a massive prehistoric monument that is believed to be one of the largest and most impressive in Britain. The site, which was once surrounded by a three-meter-high rampart and a vast ditch, is thought to have been used for great gatherings and feasts, with the remains of at least 13 pigs and scorch marks from a charcoal firepit suggesting that it was never a permanent residence. Dr. Jim Leary, director of the excavation, believes that the purpose of the monument was to show off wealth and power, with the ability to mobilize a massive workforce being a key factor in its construction.

**A 4,000-Year-Old Bronze Age Skeleton**

In a separate discovery, a 4,000-year-old Bronze Age skeleton, believed to be that of a child, was unearthed by archaeologists at a dig in Wilsford henge. The body was found lying in a foetal position and wearing an amber necklace, and scientific analysis will provide information on the child's gender, diet, pathologies, and date of burial. The discovery is a significant find, as it may shed light on where the young individual had lived and provide a unique insight into the lives of Britain's ancient children.

**A National Festival of Archaeology**

The discoveries at Marden Henge and Wilsford henge are part of a national festival of archaeology, which will see over 1,000 events across the country, including lectures, site tours, and visits to archaeology stores and structures normally closed to the public. The festival is a celebration of the importance of archaeology in understanding our past and the people who lived in Britain.

In summary, the recent archaeological discoveries in the Vale of Pewsey have shed new light on the lives of Britain's ancient inhabitants, with the excavation of Marden Henge and the discovery of a 4,000-year-old Bronze Age skeleton providing a unique insight
details.ground_truth: You think Stonehenge is impressive? Archaeologists in Britain are excavating a monument ten times larger than the iconic structure, though it appears to the naked eye to be little more than farmland. A henge is a circular earthwork, and the one in question is Marden Henge, which sits a few miles north of Stonehenge in Wiltshire. National Geographic reports that roughly 4,500 years ago it featured 10-foot-tall earthen berms that encompassed some 40 acres. The Guardian writes that the henge was "far larger than the Avebury or Stonehenge circles, and too large for any imaginable practical use." But over the ages Marden Henge's berms have "slumped" and farmers have worked the land, and archaeologists have gravitated to the more breathtaking Stonehenge. Until now. Jim Leary of the University of Reading has just kicked off a three-year study of the site; his excavations (he worked at the site in 2010, too) are the only ones to have occurred there in nearly 50 years. He hopes the berms will help explain the "insane, utterly unsustainable" construction boom that led to Marden Henge and four other nearby Neolithic monuments, including Stonehenge. "Not nearly enough attention has been paid to the archaeology of the fertile valley in between these places," says Leary. So far he's found the remains of a 4,000-year-old teen wearing an amber necklace just outside Marden Henge, per the BBC; fancy arrowheads; and a stone building within the henge containing the bones of at least 13 pigs, suggesting a huge feast was held there. "For all the attention that has been lavished on Stonehenge over the years, we may well find out that Marden was where it was really at during the Neolithic," he says. (There's a giant "super henge" under Stonehenge.)

**BP Shareholders Rebel Against CEO Bob Dudley's Pay Package**

In a historic and unprecedented move, a majority of BP PLC's shareholders voted against the company's executive pay policy, including a 20% increase in CEO Bob Dudley's total pay for 2015. The nonbinding vote, which saw 59% of investors reject the pay decisions, is a stinging rebuke to Dudley and his board as they navigate the challenges of low oil prices.

The pay package, which included a $4.2 million bonus for Dudley, was criticized by several shareholder advisory groups, including Glass Lewis, which recommended that shareholders vote against the payment plans. The company's chairman, Carl-Henric Svanberg, defended the pay package, citing the company's "exceptional" performance during a difficult year.

However, the vote is a clear indication that shareholders are increasingly uneasy with executive pay packages, particularly in the energy sector. Several other companies, including Smith & Nephew and US banks, have faced pressure to toughen their bonus "clawback" regimes, which put executives on the hook for future losses.

The vote also highlights a growing trend of institutional investors and advisers taking a more aggressive stance on pay. The Institute of Directors warned that the shareholder rebellion would "determine the future of corporate governance in the UK," and that British boards are now in the "last chance saloon" if they ignore the will of shareholders.

Despite the vote, BP's chairman said that the company wouldn't adjust Dudley's pay, citing the company's remuneration policy, which was approved by shareholders in 2014 with over 96% of the vote. The company's pay policy is subject to a binding shareholder vote every three years, with new proposals due to be put forward for shareholder approval in 2017.

**Key Takeaways:**

* A majority of BP PLC's shareholders voted against the company's executive pay policy, including a 20% increase in CEO Bob Dudley's total pay for 2015.
* The nonbinding vote saw 59% of investors reject the pay decisions, a stinging rebuke to Dudley and his board.
* Several shareholder advisory groups, including Glass Lewis, criticized the pay package, citing the company's $5.2 billion loss in 2015.
* The vote highlights a growing trend of institutional investors and advisers taking a more aggressive stance on pay.
* BP's chairman said that the company wouldn't adjust Dudley's pay, citing the company's remuneration policy, which was approved by
details.ground_truth: Oil giant BP lost $5.2 billion last year, but the company somehow saw fit to propose maximum bonuses for 2015 for its top executives, including a 20% pay increase for CEO Bob Dudley—a proposal that 59% of shareholders roundly rejected by proxy vote at Thursday's annual meeting, MarketWatch reports. The company had indicated earlier in the day that it may also have to reduce its dividend, the Wall Street Journal reports. Dudley is set to receive the full bonus he was eligible for, which comes to $4.2 million (including $1.4 million in cash and a portion in deferred BP shares), per an earlier MarketWatch report. This amount was bumped up from the $3 million ($1 million in cash) he received in 2014. BP's CFO was also on the list to rake in his full bonus. "We think it sends the wrong message," a rep for shareholder Royal London Asset Management tells the BBC. "It shows that the board is out of touch." Not only did BP suffer straight-up monetary losses as the price of oil fell, it also announced it will be getting rid of about 7,000 jobs and taking other belt-tightening measures. And the Financial Times notes that other energy company execs saw their pay slashed in 2015. But a BP spokesman says "executives performed strongly in a difficult environment in 2015, managing the things they could control and for which they were accountable." Andy Critchlow, writing for the Reuters Breakingviews blog, agrees. "Dudley has to work harder than his predecessors," he writes, noting the CEO has helped the company recover from the 2010 Deepwater Horizon disaster and improved safety protocol. "The mild-mannered American has had possibly the toughest job in the oil industry. His rewards look in line with that task." Carl-Henric Svanberg, chairman of BP's board, says the nonbinding shareholder vote won't alter the payouts they've already decided on, but that the board will take investors' concerns into account when coming up with next year's compensation packages.

**Peru's Top Investigator Put on Leave for Misleading Public on Serial Killers**

In a shocking turn of events, Peru's top organized crime investigator, Eusebio Felix, has been put on leave from his job after he misled the country by claiming to have caught a gang of serial killers who acted out an ancient Andean legend and sold their victims' fat. The legend, known as the "Pishtacos," tells the story of mythical killers who roam the Andes mountains extracting fat from travelers.

According to Felix, the gang murdered up to 60 people and exported their fat to Italian cosmetics makers for thousands of dollars a liter. However, police in Lima, the capital, later admitted that the investigation had been botched and that there may have been only one victim. The police also displayed what they said was human fat stored in an empty bottle of Inca Kola, a popular soft drink in the Andes, and a video of police pulling body parts from a shallow grave at a house in the mountainous region of Huanuco.

The police's account has been widely criticized, with anthropologists saying that investigators foolishly believed the Pishtacos legend when searching for a motive for the murder, and then played on people's fears by turning the legend into reality. Doctors have also dismissed the theory that the fat was sold for thousands of dollars, saying that it would be pointless to kill people to harvest their fat when it could be easily collected from plastic surgery clinics that perform tummy tucks.

The police's chief, Miguel Hidalgo, has apologized for the mistake, saying that it affects the image and respectability of the police. The four alleged killers, who are still in custody, may have bottled the victim's fat to intimidate their rivals in an area rife with drug trafficking and violence.

This incident has raised questions about the credibility of the police and the use of myths and legends to explain crimes. It has also sparked concerns about the impact on tourism, with politicians blaming the police for scaring away visitors.
details.ground_truth: The Peruvian cop who claimed to have busted a crime ring that killed dozens of people to harvest their fat has been suspended for lying. Felix Murga, the country's top organized crime investigator, said that a gang had killed 60 people to sell their fat at $15,000 a liter. Investigators now believe there was just one victim and his murder was linked to drug trafficking, the BBC reports. Murga appears to have revived an ancient Andean legend of killers who roam the mountains extracting fat from travelers, say authorities, who blame him for damaging the police force's reputation and scaring tourists away. "This has been a ruse of bad taste," a local politician tells Reuters.

**Pacific Crest Trail (PCT) Sees Increased Interest and Crowds**

The Pacific Crest Trail, a 2,650-mile hiking route from the Mexican border to the Canadian border, has seen a significant increase in interest and crowds in recent years. The release of Cheryl Strayed's memoir "Wild" and the subsequent movie adaptation have been credited as the main factors behind the surge in popularity.

According to the Pacific Crest Trail Association (PCTA), the number of long-distance hiking permits issued has increased, with over 4,000 permits issued in 2016. This is a significant increase from previous years, with only 300 hikers attempting to hike the full trail in 2006. The PCTA has introduced a quota system to mitigate the impact of the increased crowds on the trail and the environment.

The quota system, which was introduced in 2015, limits the number of hikers to 50 per day during the high season. This has helped to spread out the hikers and reduce the impact on the desert environments that dominate the first 700 miles of the trail. However, the PCTA is still concerned about the environmental impact and the financial costs of essential services such as helicopter evacuations and searches.

The increased crowds have also led to a change in the demographics of hikers on the PCT. According to the PCTA, the number of female hikers has increased, with women now making up 30% of the hikers on the trail. This is a significant increase from previous years, when women made up fewer than 10% of the hikers.

In addition to the increased crowds, the PCTA is also concerned about the impact of inexperienced and careless hikers on the trail. The PCTA has launched a campaign, with Strayed's support, using the hashtag #responsiblywild to promote safety and "leave no trace" practices. This includes protecting water quality, burying human waste, packing out trash, and building safe campfires.

Overall, the Pacific Crest Trail is a challenging and rewarding experience for hikers, but the increased crowds and environmental concerns are a significant issue. The PCTA is working to mitigate these issues and ensure that the trail remains a wild and beautiful experience for all hikers.

**Key Statistics:**

* 4,000 long-distance hiking permits issued in 2016
* 300 hikers attempted to hike the full trail in 2006
* 50 hikers per day limited during the high season
* 30
details.ground_truth: Hikers on the Pacific Crest Trail can expect to encounter harsh-but-beautiful landscapes, extreme fatigue, and—these days, at least—traffic. "You can’t talk about the Pacific Crest Trail without mentioning the crowds," Pete Brook writes for Outside. He should know: Brook currently is trekking the 2,650-mile PCT, which runs from the Mexican border through California, Oregon, and Washington to the border with Canada. But don't take his word for it: The Pacific Crest Trail Association so far this year has issued more than 4,000 permits to people planning on tackling at least 500 miles of the trail. To put things in perspective: 300 people attempted to complete the entire PCT in 2006, according to the AP, with about 120 of those making it. In 2014, 1,000 people set off and about half completed the trail. More traffic means more stress on the land, more improperly disposed of poop, and more inexperienced hikers requiring rescue. So what's with the increase in traffic over the past few years? You can thank Cheryl Strayed and her enormously popular memoir-turned-major-motion-picture Wild. After the book came out in 2012, there was a small increase in interest in the trail, the PCTA's Jack Haskel told the AP in 2015. But after the movie, starring Reese Witherspoon, was released in 2014, interest in the PCT exploded. "Millions are hearing about it now and are being inspired," Haskel said. Strayed, more or less unprepared for the journey, hiked 1,100 miles of the trail as a form of therapy as she grappled with personal demons. "However for others that follow her strategy of deliberate ineptitude, things might not turn out so well," writes DontHikeLikeWild.org. "Our advice to would-be hikers: Grow a spine first. Then get out there and hike the PCT." (This female hiker is the "biggest badass you've never heard of.")

**Pete Davidson's Journey to Sobriety and Medical Marijuana Use**

Pete Davidson, a cast member of the popular late-night sketch show Saturday Night Live (SNL), has recently opened up about his struggles with addiction and his reliance on medical marijuana to manage his Crohn's disease.

In an interview with HIGH TIMES, Davidson revealed that he has been sober for the first time in eight years, crediting his great girlfriend, friends, and a sense of good fortune for his success. He also expressed his gratitude to his fans for their love and support, stating that they are a family and that he will always be there for them.

Davidson's struggles with addiction and mental health have been well-documented in the past. He has spoken publicly about his father's death in the 9/11 attacks and has credited Kid Cudi's music with saving his life. In a recent Instagram post, Davidson revealed that he had been missing from social media and the show due to his sobriety, but was happy to be back in action.

Davidson has also been an outspoken advocate for the medicinal use of cannabis, stating that it is the only thing that helps him eat and perform on SNL. He has criticized the New York State medical marijuana program, calling it inadequate and difficult to navigate. He believes that cannabis should be legal beyond just medical purposes and has even won the respect of SNL creator Lorne Michaels, who has praised Davidson's focus and productivity despite his love of marijuana.

Davidson's first hour-long stand-up special, "SMD," will air on Comedy Central on October 29th, and SNL will premiere its new season on October 1st.
details.ground_truth: Noticed Pete Davidson's absence from SNL as of late? Well, there's a good reason behind it: The 23-year-old has been focusing on his sobriety. After telling High Times last fall that he couldn't function without the medical marijuana he's prescribed for Crohn's disease, Davidson now says, "I quit drugs and am happy and sober for the first time in eight years" in an Instagram post. Rapper Kid Cudi might have inspired that sobriety, per the New York Daily News. Last fall, Davidson told a radio station, "I would have killed myself if I didn't have Kid Cudi," who'd checked himself into rehab a few weeks earlier.

**Jeff Herman, Lawyer Behind Bryan Singer and Gary Goddard Suits, Opens Up About His Own Childhood Trauma and Motivation for Taking on Powerful Abusers**

Jeff Herman, a Florida-based lawyer who has filed several high-profile suits against Hollywood executives, including Bryan Singer and Gary Goddard, has opened up about his own childhood trauma and the motivation behind his work.

Herman, 54, revealed that he has some fragmented memories of being abused as a young child, including being held by his arms and legs and having his pants cut open. He also shared a story about being assaulted in a men's room in law school, which he said shook him up and made him realize the importance of his work.

Herman has been a vocal advocate for victims of childhood abuse, and his suits against Singer and Goddard have made headlines in recent weeks. The suits allege that the two men abused a 14-year-old boy at a London hotel suite after the premiere of "Superman Returns" in 2006.

**New Details Emerge in Bryan Singer and Gary Goddard Suits**

In a news conference on Monday, Herman provided new details about the alleged abuse, including photos of the boy and Singer and Goddard. Herman also showed an email allegedly sent by Goddard to the boy, which read "The closest thing that I have to a 'naughty' shot of you."

Herman's suits against Singer and Goddard have been met with strong denials from the two men, who have accused Herman of being a "grandstander" and a "shakedown artist." Herman has responded by saying that the pushback he is getting is similar to the early days of the clergy sex abuse scandal, where people were in denial and unprepared to face the truth.

**More Suits to Come, Herman Says**

Herman has promised that more suits are coming, including one related to another alleged sex ring in Hollywood. He has also been contacted by other possible molestation victims connected to Hollywood, and plans to file more lawsuits in the future.

The kind of pushback I'm seeing reminds me of the early days of the clergy sex abuse scandals in the Catholic Church. People didn't want to believe it. They were unprepared.

**John Doe No. 117 to Testify if Case Goes to Trial**

The 25-year-old man who alleges that he was abused by Singer and Goddard is ready to testify if his case goes to trial. He is seeking financial damages to be decided by
details.ground_truth: Bryan Singer has already been hit with two lawsuits alleging sexual abuse of minors—and the lawyer who filed both of them, plus other suits against Hollywood bigwigs, says more are coming. "I’ve heard from victims about very recent claims of being sexually exploited in Hollywood," Jeff Herman said at a news conference yesterday, according to Variety. He said one coming lawsuit involves another Hollywood "sex ring." Herman also shared photos showing the second alleged victim (with his face redacted) and Singer at the London premiere of Singer's Superman Returns in 2006; John Doe, then 17, says Singer abused him after the premiere, the New York Daily News reports. As for the strenuous denials from all the Hollywood VIPs named in the various suits, Herman said, "The pushback I’m getting reminds me of the early days of the clergy sex abuse scandal." Meanwhile, in an interview with the Daily Beast, Herman says he himself has vague memories of being sexually abused as a child. He says he's worked on some 800 sex abuse cases in his career, notably involving the Catholic Church and the Boy Scouts.

**Double Shooting in Portland, Texas: A Family's Fight for Recovery and Justice**

On June 23, 2012, a senseless and tragic act of violence occurred in Portland, Texas, leaving one young woman dead and another fighting for her life. Mollie Judith Olgin, 19, was shot in the head and died shortly after, while her girlfriend, Mary Kristene Chapa, 18, was also shot in the head and is currently in the intensive care unit (ICU) making an "amazing" recovery, according to her brother Hilario Chapa.

The shooting occurred at Violet Andrews Park in Portland, where the couple had stopped to kill some time before heading to the movies. The Texas Rangers and Portland Police are investigating the incident, but so far, there is no evidence to indicate that it was a hate crime. However, the police are not ruling out the possibility.

The families of both Mollie and Kristene have received an outpouring of support from the community and across the country. Mollie's father, Mario Olgin, is confident that his daughter's killer will be brought to justice and has stated, "Justice will be served." Kristene's family has set up a donation page on WePay to help pay for her medical bills, as they are without insurance due to their father's job situation.

Hilario Chapa has spoken publicly about his sister's progress, saying that she has made physical progress on her right side but is still waiting for a response from the left side of her body. He has also expressed his gratitude for the support from the gay community and has stated, "It makes us all stronger and brings the community together."

A memorial service for Mollie Olgin will be held on Friday at 4:30 p.m. at Limbaugh Funeral Home in Portland, followed by a candlelight vigil for both girls at 6 p.m. at the Violet Andrews Park.

As the investigation continues and Kristene Chapa fights for her recovery, the community remains united in their support and determination to ensure that justice is served.
details.ground_truth: The dad of the teenage lesbian shot dead in a Texas park called for justice yesterday. He immediately had a "bad feeling" when his 19-year-old daughter, Mollie, failed to show up for work, said Mario Olgin. “It was not like Mollie," he told KIII-TV. "If she had some place to be she was going to be there.” Mollie and her 18-year-old girlfriend, Mary Kristene Chapa, had been shot in the head by an unknown assailant. Mollie died at the scene, but Chapa is making an "amazing" recovery, her brother told NBC Latino. Mollie has just finished her first semester of college and dreamed of becoming a psychiatrist. "She was happy," Olgin recalled. Now, "she's my guardian angel. I know she's looking down on us, in a better place." He's confident police will find her killer. "Justice will be served," he declared. Texas Rangers have joined Portland police in continuing to investigate the crime, but haven't yet discussed any progress. They haven't labeled the shootings a hate crime, but aren't ruling it out.

**Scientists Discover Iridescent Dinosaur with Rainbow Feathers**

A team of scientists has made a groundbreaking discovery in northeastern China, uncovering the fossilized remains of a dinosaur with iridescent feathers that shimmered and shifted in the light, similar to those of modern hummingbirds. The dinosaur, named Caihong juji, or "rainbow with a big crest," lived 161 million years ago during the Jurassic Period and was a small, bird-like creature with a crested head and sharp teeth.

**Unique Features of Caihong juji**

Caihong juji had several unique features that set it apart from other dinosaurs. Its feathers were iridescent, with colors that shimmered and shifted in the light, and were likely used for display and social purposes. The dinosaur's body was also covered in feathers, with long, stiff quill-like feathers on its head and neck, and shorter, more flexible feathers on its body. Its tail was frond-like, with asymmetrical feathers that would have borne the aerodynamic load of flight.

**Melanosomes and Iridescence**

The scientists were able to determine the colors of Caihong juji's feathers by analyzing the melanosomes, or pigment sacs, that were preserved in the fossil. They found that the melanosomes on the dinosaur's head, chest, and parts of its tail were long, flat, and organized into sheets, similar to those found in the iridescent throat feathers of hummingbirds. This suggests that Caihong juji's feathers would have glinted and shimmered in the light, creating a rainbow effect.

**Comparison to Other Dinosaur Fossils**

Caihong juji is the oldest known animal to have asymmetrical feathers, a key adaptation for flight. It is also similar to the iconic bird-like dinosaur Archaeopteryx, which lived 10 million years later. However, Caihong juji's feathers are more iridescent and its body is more bird-like, suggesting that it may have been a more advanced ancestor of the birds.

**Implications for the Evolution of Feathers**

The discovery of Caihong juji and its iridescent feathers has significant implications for our understanding of the evolution of feathers. It suggests that feathers may have evolved for display and social purposes before they were used for flight, and that iridescence may have been a common feature of many small raptor dinosaurs before the origin of birds.

Overall, the discovery of Caihong juji is
details.ground_truth: Scientists are now saying there may have been "a more colorful Jurassic World than we previously imagined," thanks to the recent discovery of a fossil in China's Hebei province. Reuters reports that a closer look at the "exquisitely preserved," almost completely intact fossil of a crow-like dinosaur that lived about 161 million years ago revealed that Caihong juji had luminous, brilliantly hued feathers that closely resembled those of hummingbirds. The study in the journal Nature Communications documenting the find explains that "Caihong" means "rainbow" in Mandarin. Scientists think the colorful plumage, which appeared to have covered the bony-crested creature's head, neck, and chest areas, may have kept the dinosaur warm, as well as attracted potential mates; National Geographic compares it to peacock feathers. So how were researchers able to tell what color feathers the creature had from preserved bones? They used high-tech microscopes able to home in on 66 sites on the fossil, detecting tiny cell structures called melanosomes, which underlie pigmentation. Which colors they led to can be found by their shapes, and the melanosomes in Caihong were long and flat like pancakes—similar to those found in hummingbirds, which boast iridescent plumage. Although they can't pick out from the Pantone wheel Caihong's exact colors, researchers say the creature likely sported feathers with a "rainbow glimmer." Study co-author Xing Zu tells National Geographic that Caihong was a predator that spent its days gliding from tree to tree. And "glide" is the operative word, as this dinosaur likely didn't fly: Its feathers were located on its tail, not on its wings like birds, Discover notes. (A study postulated that nearly all dinosaurs had feathers.)

A wave of protests has swept across the Middle East, with demonstrators demanding an end to authoritarian rule and greater freedoms. The protests have been met with a hardening response from governments, with security forces in Libya and Yemen firing on pro-democracy demonstrators, resulting in a number of deaths and injuries.

**Libya:**

In Libya, the government has cracked down on protesters, with security forces firing on thousands of mourners in the city of Benghazi, killing at least 15 people. The protests have been ongoing for several days, with demonstrators demanding an end to the rule of Colonel Muammar Gaddafi, who has been in power for 42 years. The government has also cut off the internet, further isolating the country.

**Yemen:**

In Yemen, security forces have also fired on protesters, killing one anti-government demonstrator and injuring five others. The protests have been ongoing for 10 days, with demonstrators demanding an end to the rule of President Ali Abdullah Saleh, who has been in power for three decades. Saleh has promised not to seek re-election in 2013, but the protests have continued to escalate.

In Bahrain, the government has withdrawn its tanks and troops from the streets, following international pressure. The protests have been ongoing for several days, with demonstrators demanding an end to the rule of the Sunni monarchy and greater freedoms for the Shiite majority. The protesters have taken over the central Pearl Square, but the government has established checkpoints and barbed wire to deter gatherings.

**Algeria:**

In Algeria, police have thwarted a rally by thousands of pro-democracy supporters, breaking up the crowd into isolated groups to keep them from marching. The protests have been ongoing for several weeks, with demonstrators demanding an end to the rule of President Abdelaziz Bouteflika, who has promised to lift the state of emergency, which has been in place since 1992.

**Regional Response:**

The protests have been met with a hardening response from governments, with security forces in Libya and Yemen firing on pro-democracy demonstrators. The international community has called for restraint, with President Barack Obama asking the King of Bahrain to hold those responsible for the violence accountable. The European Union has also called for an end to the violence, and for the governments to engage in a dialogue with the protesters.

**Conclusion:**

details.ground_truth: In direct contrast to the "delirious joy" in Bahrain, the death toll keeps climbing in Libya's protests. Moammar Gadhafi's minions killed another 20 people today, bringing the five-day total to at least 104, says Human Rights Watch. Gadhafi has effectively shut off Internet service and forbid media coverage, but witnesses told AP of attacks by police and government loyalists wielding guns, knives, and even anti-aircraft missiles. While the accounts are impossible to verify—and at least one puts the death toll closer to 200—a grim cycle has emerged, reports the New York Times: "Security forces fire on funeral marches, killing more protesters and creating more funerals." In Yemen, meanwhile, President Ali Abdullah Saleh's forces also opened fire on protesters, killing at least one, reports AP.

**Rare Nirvana Discoveries and Internet Archive Efforts**

In recent news, two distinct stories have captured the attention of music enthusiasts and the online community. The first involves the unearthing of rare photographs from Nirvana's first concert in March 1987. Maggie Poukkula, the 19-year-old daughter of Tony Poukkula, a member of the Seattle band Laytem who hosted the fledgling Nirvana in his basement, shared the photographs on Twitter. The pictures show Nirvana's original lineup, consisting of Kurt Cobain, Krist Novoselic, and Aaron Burckhard, with Poukkula joining in on guitar for Led Zeppelin covers. The discovery comes as part of a larger trend of rare Cobain and Nirvana artifacts being shared, including those featured in the acclaimed documentary Montage of Heck.

The second story involves the efforts of the Internet Archive to preserve and fix broken links on the web. The organization is using crawls to archive pages as they are created and the pages they refer to, allowing for a link to the original version to be preserved even if the referenced page is changed or removed. The goal is to create a more stable and reliable online environment, with the organization hoping that references to archived pages will be used in place of broken links.

**Key Dates:**

- August 7th: Montage of Heck returns to movie theaters
- July 16, 2015: Maggie Poukkula shares the rare Nirvana photographs on Twitter
details.ground_truth: Most teens don't find music history in their dad's stuff, but Maggie Poukkula's dad is Seattle musician Tony Poukkula, who was a friend of Kurt Cobain. Her find? Photos of Nirvana's first concert, performed by then 20-year-old Cobain and his new band in March 1987 in Tony Poukkula's basement. "My dad showed me them awhile back, but he never mentioned that's what was going on in the photos," Maggie Poukkula, 19, tells Rolling Stone. "I found out because of all the articles. I didn't realize it was such a historical thing. I thought they were just cool pictures of my dad and Kurt jamming together." She tweeted the photos last week, and the post quickly went viral. That first concert featured the early Nirvana lineup of Cobain, bassist Krist Novoselic, and drummer Aaron Burckhard playing original songs and Led Zeppelin covers. Similar early-days footage of Cobain and Co. featured in the recent documentary Montage of Heck has renewed interest in the grunge band. The Emmy-nominated project includes home movies, never-before-heard music, and intimate interviews with Cobain's family and friends, notes SPIN. After being critically acclaimed at festivals across the country, the film will return to theaters on Aug. 7, adds Rolling Stone. (Want a little more Cobain history? Here's the mix tape he made at 21.)

Clarissa Dickson Wright, the renowned British chef, food historian, and television personality, has passed away at the age of 66. Born Clarissa Theresa Philomena Aileen Mary Josephine Agnes Elsie Trilby Louise Esmerelda Dickson Wright in 1947, she rose to fame as one half of the popular TV cooking duo "Two Fat Ladies" alongside Jennifer Paterson.

Dickson Wright's culinary journey began in her 40s, after she had overcome her struggles with alcoholism. She started writing a cooking column and running a store called Books for Cooks in London, which eventually caught the attention of a television producer. The show "Two Fat Ladies" made its debut in 1996 on the BBC and was later picked up by the Food Network in the United States.

The show's unique format featured Dickson Wright and Paterson traveling the UK in a motorcycle-sidecar, cooking and sharing their love for good British grub. They were known for their irreverent humor, eccentricity, and indulgent recipes, which often featured high-fat ingredients like lard, bacon, and tongue.

After Paterson's death in 1999, Dickson Wright continued to work in television, fronting several shows, including "Breakfast, Lunch and Dinner" in 2012. She also wrote a well-received book, "A History of English Food," and was an outspoken campaigner for rural life and the Countryside Alliance.

Dickson Wright's personal life was marked by a privileged upbringing, overshadowed by her father's drinking and abuse. She became a barrister at 21 but struggled with addiction and eventually quit law. She never married but had a close relationship with Clive, a fellow alcoholic who died in 1982.

Throughout her life, Dickson Wright remained unapologetic and unafraid to speak her mind, whether it was about her love of hunting and shooting or her disdain for vegetarians and carrots. Her friends, colleagues, and fans will miss her fun, laughter, and intelligence, but her legacy as a pioneering chef, food historian, and television personality will live on.

In a statement, her agent Heather Holden-Brown said, "Clarissa was utterly non-PC and fought for what she believed in, always, with no thought to her own personal cost. Her fun and laughter, extraordinary learning and intelligence, will be missed always, by so many of us."
details.ground_truth: Both of TV's "Fat Ladies" have sung. Clarissa Dickson Wright, one half of the BBC's "Two Fat Ladies" cooking duo, died in Edinburgh Saturday at age 66, the BBC reports. Wright was a former lawyer who filmed four of the "Fat Ladies" series, going on food-related road trips across the UK in a motorbike and sidecar with Jennifer Paterson, before Paterson died in 1999 from cancer. The New York Times describes Wright as a "rebel," both hosts as "irreverent and eccentric," and the recipes as "sometimes confounding." Wright's eclectic working life also included stints as a cook, an author, and a cookbook shop manager; she also ran a catering business, was a guild butcher, and once worked on a yacht in the Caribbean. In fact, she recently said, "I've had a fantastic life and I've done everything I could have wanted to do and more." It wasn't until her 40s, after she'd recovered from alcoholism, that she got into cooking seriously. As for the perhaps-controversial title of the show that brought her fame? "If you're fat you're fat," she once said. "I hate this modern-day political correctness, that you don't call things by their proper name." Her agent remembers Wright similarly in a statement: "Loved dearly by her friends and many fans all over the world, Clarissa was utterly non-PC and fought for what she believed in, always, with no thought to her own personal cost." There's no word on Wright's cause of death, but the Guardian reports that she had been undergoing treatment at a hospital since the beginning of the year.

**Mountain Walker's Fatal Fall: Inquest into Tryfan Incident Reveals Safety Concerns**

A 53-year-old experienced mountain walker, Jane Wilson, fell to her death while attempting to find a safe route off Snowdonia's Tryfan peak in March 2023. An inquest into the incident has revealed that the couple, who were both experienced walkers, used a smartphone app to navigate the mountain, which may have contributed to the tragedy.

According to the inquest, Jane Wilson and her husband, Gary, decided not to go to the summit of Tryfan due to the approaching dusk. They then headed across the mountain's west face, which is known for its loose rocks and steep inclines. Jane Wilson, who was an academic engagement librarian at the University of Manchester, went ahead of her husband to look for a suitable route and was asked if it was safe. However, she fell 20-30 feet down a vertical cliff and then tumbled 150 meters down into an area known as Notch arrete.

The inquest heard that the couple's decision to change their route and use a smartphone app may have been a contributing factor to the tragedy. Detective Constable Tim Bird, the leader of the Ogwen Valley Mountain Rescue Team, said that the app's image was small and not as detailed as a proper map, and that the couple may have "joined up the dots" and misinterpreted the route.

The incident has raised safety concerns about the use of smartphone apps for navigation in the mountains. Chris Lloyd, a member of the Ogwen Rescue team, described the route as "not a straightforward path" and said that Tryfan is responsible for about 30% of the incidents the team attends. Andy Simpson, a spokesman for Mountain Rescue England and Wales, urged walkers to use a map and compass in addition to electronic navigation aids, citing the potential difficulties caused by poor detail on an electronic map and the risk of batteries running out.

The coroner recorded a conclusion of accidental death, and the incident serves as a reminder of the importance of careful planning and navigation when venturing into the mountains.
details.ground_truth: A hiker's fatal fall in March has been blamed on using a smartphone app map instead of an actual map. The BBC reports Jane Wilson and her husband Gary were hiking on Tryfan in Wales when it started to get dark and they decided to skip the summit. While attempting to descend the mountain, Gary was looking at an Ordnance Survey app while Jane went a short way ahead to make sure the path was safe, according to the Telegraph. The Manchester Evening News reports Gary heard his wife fall, got to safety, and called for help. Rescuers found the 53-year-old librarian's body at the bottom of a 30-foot cliff with a fractured skull. At a hearing Friday, Jane's was ruled an accidental death due, in part, to using an app instead of a map. "Apart from potential difficulties caused by poor detail on an electronic map, batteries on mobile phones have a nasty habit of running out just when you need them most," the Telegraph quotes a spokesperson for Mountain Rescue England and Wales as saying. A detective says the app map "would have been small and not as detailed" and took the couple on a route that was not the safest. A director with Ordnance Survey says hikers should always use the app in conjunction with an actual map "due to the nature of mobile devices when navigating the real world." Authorities also say Jane wasn't wearing appropriate footwear for the hike. (Go inside the futile search for "Inchworm" on the trail.)

**Summary of Recent News Developments**

**Politics and Elections**

The 2012 presidential election is heating up, with several key figures weighing in on the candidates and the issues. New York Mayor Michael Bloomberg praised President Barack Obama as a "credible, formidable candidate" for reelection, while also criticizing his use of billionaire investor Warren Buffett as an example for why taxes should be increased on the wealthy. Bloomberg also praised Texas Gov. Rick Perry for his 2007 executive order requiring sixth-grade girls to receive the HPV vaccine and said Mitt Romney's health care plan for Massachusetts worked. Indiana Gov. Mitch Daniels, who considered a White House run himself, says fellow Republicans should forgive Perry's uneven debate performances.

**Republican National Committee**

Republican National Committee Chairman Reince Priebus mocked President Obama's 2008 election slogan, arguing it won't pass the smell test with voters in 2012. He also said that the Democratic National Committee's talking points have already been proved false, referring to the recent GOP victory in New York's special House election.

**Congress and Government Shutdown**

Sen. Mark Warner (D-Va.) called the specter of a government shutdown because Congress can't agree to another short-term spending bill "embarrassing" and blamed tea party-affiliated Republicans for the stalemate. Sen. Lamar Alexander (R-Tenn.) blamed Majority Leader Harry Reid (D-Nev.) for holding up the bill.

**Foreign Policy and National Security**

Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.) says the United States needs to leave "all options on the table" in dealing with Pakistan after U.S. military officials accused the country's intelligence officials of assisting terrorist operations. President Obama's senior adviser, David Plouffe, said he doesn't expect significant changes in top White House staff or the Cabinet ahead of the 2012 reelection campaign. He also said the U.S. may continue reducing aid to Pakistan if it can't do more to impede anti-American terrorism being conducted by the Haqqani terror network.

**Jobs and the Economy**

President Obama's senior adviser, David Plouffe, pushed for Congress to pass Obama's American Jobs Act, saying Obama is "confident in his team, in the direction we've laid out here."
details.ground_truth: Mitch Daniels has some advice for those who are writing Rick Perry's political eulogy: Hold your horses. "It's way too early to know, or to issue, to pronounce last rites over one performance," Daniels said today. "There's still many of these (debates), too many maybe. ... I'd cut him some slack and give him a little time." Politico notes that Daniels also had kind words for Democrat Sen. Mark Warner's work on the Gang of Six, saying, "It's very encouraging, by the way, to hear a guy like Sen. Warner speak to it as clearly as he just did. I wish he were president." More fun on your Sunday dial, as per Politico: Michael Bloomberg on President Obama's 2012 chances: "He would be a credible, formidable candidate. You're going to have a real horse race no matter who the Republican nominee is." Reince Priebus to Debbie Wasserman Schultz on 2012: "It sounds like the new slogan is no longer 'hope and change.' It's, 'Hey, it could've been worse.' Great bumper sticker Debbie, I hope it works for you." David Plouffe on a White House shakeup: "I don't expect that." Obama is "confident in his team, in the direction we've laid out here." Warner on Congress' standoff over a short-term spending bill: "It is embarrassing. The Senate is saying, 'Why should we build schools in Iraq on the credit card but expect that rebuilding schools in Joplin, Mo.,'" will be paid for. Warner blames "a group, more centered in the House, in terms of some of these Tea Party Republicans, who say on every issue, 'We're going to make this a make-or-break.'" Lindsey Graham on Pakistan: "We need to put Pakistan on notice. We're going to have to put all options on the table. including defending our troops. It's now a time of choosing, so I hope they choose wisely."
