**Soda Tax Fails to Deliver on Promises, Sparks Public Outrage**

In recent months, several cities and states have implemented a tax on sugary drinks, aiming to reduce consumption and raise revenue for important projects. However, the results of these efforts have been far from promising, sparking public outrage and calls for repeal.

**Seattle's Soda Tax: A Case Study in Failure**

Seattle's 1.75 cent per ounce tax on sweetened beverages, implemented in 2018, was touted as a model for the nation. Former Mayor Ed Murray called it a "cutting edge piece of legislation" that would reduce sugar consumption, raise revenue, and help low-income families. However, the reality has been far different. The tax has driven up prices, with a case of Gatorade now costing over $26.33, a 64% increase. Retailers like Costco have posted signs directing customers to locations outside the city limits where they can buy the product without the tax. Already, there is evidence that people are voting with their feet and buying drinks in the suburbs, depriving Seattle of the revenue it claims to need.

**Cook County's Soda Tax: A Repeal Victory**

In a similar case, Cook County's penny-per-ounce tax on sugar and artificially sweetened beverages was repealed in December 2018 after a public outcry and a well-funded opposition campaign by the beverage industry. The tax had driven up prices, with a case of soda now costing over $8. Retailers and restaurants had seen a decline in sales, with some even losing business to customers who shopped in neighboring counties to avoid the tax.

**Spokane's Soda Tax: A Potential Threat to Local Businesses**

In a bid to follow Seattle's lead, a bill has been re-introduced to bring a sugary drink tax statewide to Washington. However, local business owners are worried about the potential impact on their sales, with a case of soda now costing over $8 in Seattle. The tax would also include a tax on syrups and powders used to make soda in machines, further increasing costs for businesses.

**The Theory of the Frog in a Boiling Pot of Water**

Critics of the soda tax argue that it is a classic example of the theory of the frog in a boiling pot of water. If the tax is increased slowly over time, people may adapt to it. However, if it is implemented suddenly and dramatically, as in the case of Seattle, people will rebel. The tax has also failed to deliver on
details.ground_truth: "That new soda tax in Seattle is working out about as well as Chicago's," proclaims a headline at Hot Air. It's not a compliment. The tax, which went into effect Jan. 1, slaps an additional 1.75 cents on each fluid ounce of sugar-sweetened drinks, a group that includes soda, sports drinks, and kombucha. That's nearly double the one-cent levy the Chicago-encompassing Cook County tried, a tax it repealed after about two months. The Chicago Tribune reported at the time that Costco's nine affected locations saw their sales of beverages impacted by the tax fall 34%, while sales jumped 38% at locations just beyond the county line. And Costco is again at the fore of what's happening: Because bulk purchases contain significantly more ounces than an individual one, the impact is starker, as KIRO discovered when it spotted an updated Costco sign for Gatorade in Seattle. The sign details Costco's price for the 35-pack of 16-ounce bottles—$15.99—and separately lists the city's $10.34 tax, for a new total of $26.33. But that's not all the sign says: It also directs consumers to nearby locations that are outside city limits and exempt from the tax. The tax is meant to fight obesity and raise funds for worthy expenditures, but the blog for Citizens Against Government Waste sees the ends quite differently: "Let’s be clear. Soda taxes don’t make people healthier. They don't raise revenue—they drive it outside city limits. They don’t help ease inequality—they make the poor poorer." But some in government seemingly remain hopeful: KXLY reports a bill was reintroduced Monday that would push the tax statewide. (Speaking of Seattle and beverages, the world's largest Starbucks is no longer located there.)

**Tragic Birthday Party Incident: Woman Fails to Stop After Hitting Man in Front of Car**

A 34-year-old man, Jonathan Carlyle Merkley, was killed in a tragic incident at a birthday party in San Diego, California. The incident occurred on a Sunday morning, just after midnight, at the Staybridge Suites hotel on Mira Mesa Boulevard.

According to police, Merkley and his friends had been drinking alcohol before the incident. The woman, who was also at the party and had been drinking, decided to leave and get behind the wheel of her BMW. Merkley, in an attempt to prevent her from leaving, walked into the path of the car and laid down in front of it.

The woman failed to stop and ran over Merkley, causing major trauma to his chest. He was taken to a hospital, where he later died. The police know the identity of the BMW driver but have not made any arrests as of late Sunday morning.

The incident has sparked outrage and calls for the woman to turn herself in. Gloria Williams, a hotel guest, said, "I think this is crazy, we don’t value each others lives." The police have released a statement stating that they have interviewed the female friend and have a vehicle in custody pending examination to determine if it is the vehicle in question.

In the following days, the police tracked down the woman and interviewed her. A car that "may have been involved" in the crash was also impounded. The investigation is ongoing, and it is unclear whether the driver knew she hit the man.

The incident serves as a reminder of the importance of responsible drinking and the need to prioritize the safety and well-being of others.
details.ground_truth: A birthday party in California turned tragic after the guest of honor was run over and killed by his friend. Police say Jonathan Carlyle Merkley was celebrating his 34th birthday early Sunday at a hotel near San Diego when a woman decided to leave the party. Merkley wanted her to stay and reportedly walked toward her BMW and laid down in front of what the Orange County Register describes as a moving car. The woman didn’t stop, ran him over, and kept going. Police cite witnesses as saying both had been drinking, Fox 5 reports. Merkley, who suffered major chest trauma, died about 45 minutes later at a hospital. Police tracked the woman down and a car that "may have been involved" was impounded, cops tell the San Diego Union-Tribune. The woman was not arrested but police said on Tuesday that the investigation is continuing. "There's a lot we still need to find out," says San Diego Police Sgt. Tim Underwood.

**Humboldt County's High Rate of Missing Persons: A Complex and Frustrating Issue**

Humboldt County, California, has been identified as having the highest per-capita rate of missing persons reports in the state. A recent investigation by the North Coast Journal found that the county's high rate of missing adults is largely attributed to the cannabis industry, which attracts people from all over the world to work in remote, off-the-grid locations. Many of these individuals do not communicate with their families or friends, leading to reports of them being missing.

However, the investigation also found that not all missing persons reports are legitimate. Some people are listed as missing because they have chosen to cut off contact with their loved ones, while others are mistakenly reported as missing. The North Coast Journal was able to contact several individuals who were listed as missing and found that they were, in fact, safe and sound.

The Humboldt 35, a list of 35 people currently missing in the county, includes a 22-year-old woman named Bekah Martinez, who was reported missing by her mother in November 2017. Martinez was later found to be a contestant on the reality TV show "The Bachelor." Another individual on the list, Mitchell Hernandez, a 47-year-old man, was reported missing in December 2017 after he walked out of a family member's home carrying a.40 caliber pistol and a backpack. His disappearance remains a mystery.

The investigation also highlighted the challenges faced by families of missing individuals, particularly those involved in the cannabis industry. Vikki Joseph, whose brother Jeff Joseph disappeared in June 2014, has been working tirelessly to keep his name in the public eye and communicate with investigators. However, she has been frustrated by the lack of progress in the case and the fact that her brother's name is not listed on the Attorney General's website because the report was filed in Los Angeles.

The National Missing and Unidentified Persons System (NamUs) is a federal database that connects each case with a caseworker who compares it against reports, looks for patterns, and collaborates with families and local law enforcement. The system allows family members to submit DNA samples to its database for comparison against unidentified bodies. However, the patchwork of information available through local and state organizations does not offer the full picture that a national registry might.

In conclusion, the high rate of missing persons in Humboldt County is a complex and frustrating issue that requires a multifaceted approach. While the cannabis industry is a significant contributor to the problem,
details.ground_truth: It turns out missing people are pretty easy to find when they appear every week in your living room. The San Francisco Chronicle reports a 22-year-old woman reported missing in November in California was found this week—because she's a current contestant on The Bachelor. The strange story started when Bekah Martinez's mom reported her missing to the Humboldt County Sheriff's Office on Nov. 18. According to BuzzFeed, Martinez's mom said her daughter had gone to the area to work on a marijuana farm and she hadn't heard from her in a week. The Sheriff's Office was unable to find Martinez or contact anyone who might know her, so Martinez was added to the California Department of Justice's list of missing people. That list was the basis for a story in the North Coast Journal about the high number of missing people in Humboldt County. The newspaper shared the story Thursday on Facebook and asked readers if they recognized any of the missing people. Amy O'Brien did. "I was like wait a minute, she looks so familiar," O'Brien tells the Chronicle. "I instantly thought of The Bachelor." The Journal informed the sheriff's office, which contacted Martinez and removed her from the list. "MOM. how many times do I have to tell you I don’t get cell service on The Bachelor??," Martinez tweeted after learning she was a missing person. The Bachelor started filming in September, and it's unclear if Martinez was still filming when she was reported missing. She tweets "the scariest thing" about being a missing person "is that my efforts to conceal The Worst Drivers License Photo Of All Time have been thwarted"

Nicole "Snooki" Polizzi, a reality star from the show "Jersey Shore," is reportedly pregnant with her first child. Despite her initial denials, sources close to the situation confirm that she is indeed expecting, with her boyfriend Jionni LaValle being the father. This news comes as a surprise to many, as Snooki has built her career on her partying and reckless behavior, which may now be put to the test as she navigates motherhood.

The news of Snooki's pregnancy has sparked concerns about the potential risks of fetal alcohol spectrum disorders (FASD), particularly given her history of heavy drinking. FASD is a group of conditions that can occur in a person whose mother drank alcohol during pregnancy, and can result in a range of physical and neurodevelopmental problems, including growth deficiency, facial abnormalities, and central nervous system damage.

According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), FASD is estimated to affect between 2% and 5% of people in the United States and Western Europe, with the lifetime cost of an individual with FAS estimated to be two million USD in 2002. The risk of FAS is higher in indigenous populations, with a rate of 2.76 per 1000 births in Western Australia.

In a recent interview with Ellen DeGeneres, Snooki opened up about her history of heavy drinking and admitted that she may occasionally overdo it. She confessed that episodes of over-imbibing used to happen as often as once a month and that she doesn't want to be a role model. However, she also expressed that living with reckless abandon is not always as fun as it may seem, saying, "It sucks. You're like, 'What did I do? Why did I wake up in a garbage can?'"

The diagnosis of FASD requires a multi-disciplinary evaluation to assess each of the four key features: growth deficiency, FAS facial features, central nervous system damage, and prenatal alcohol exposure. The "4-Digit Diagnostic Code" ranking system distinguishes between levels of prenatal alcohol exposure as high risk and some risk, with a BAC level of 100 mg/dL or greater delivered at least weekly in early pregnancy being considered high risk.

Prevention of FASD is the only certain way, and the Surgeon General recommends that women abstain from alcohol use while pregnant or planning a pregnancy. However, there is some controversy surrounding the "zero-tolerance" approach

Why did I wake up in a garbage can?'"

**Employee Tracking and Privacy Rights: A Central California Case**

A Central California woman, Myrna Arias, has filed a lawsuit against her former employer, Intermex Wire Transfer LLC, alleging that she was fired after refusing to install an app that tracked her location 24/7, even when she was off the clock. The app, developed by Xora Inc. (now ClickSoftware Solution), was designed to enable employees to create electronic timecards and track their shifts, but Arias claims that it was used to monitor her movements and invade her privacy.

According to the lawsuit, Arias's boss, John Stubits, admitted that employees would be monitored while off duty and bragged about knowing her driving speed and routes. Arias likened the app to a prisoner's ankle bracelet and informed Stubits that his actions were illegal. However, Stubits scolded her for uninstalling the app and told her that she had to keep her phone on 24/7 to assist clients.

Arias claims that she was fired a few weeks later and that Intermex's actions were a violation of California and federal laws against invasion of privacy. Her attorney, Gail Glick, stated that an employer can legally monitor employees at work if there is a legitimate business interest, but that Intermex's actions went "above and beyond a normal wrongful termination and interfered with her ability to earn a livelihood."

The case highlights the importance of employee tracking and privacy rights in the modern workplace. While some companies use employee tracking apps to improve productivity and efficiency, others may use them to monitor and control employees. The lawsuit also raises questions about the use of technology to track and monitor employees, particularly when it is used outside of work hours.

**Background on Employee Tracking Apps**

The StreetSmart web-based management application, developed by Xora Inc. (now ClickSoftware Solution), is designed to enable office staff to track employee locations and monitor their productivity. The app allows employees to record their daily work and job-specific hours, receive their daily job assignments on their phone, and capture customer or job information on their phone. The app is available on over 140 different types of feature and rugged phones, smart phones, and tablets, and is offered on a monthly basis for roughly $1 a day per user.

The app's features and functionality are similar to those of other employee tracking apps, which are designed to improve productivity and efficiency in the workplace. However, the lawsuit highlights the potential risks and consequences of using these apps, particularly when they are used to
details.ground_truth: Myrna Arias didn't like the GPS app on her phone that constantly tracked her, so she uninstalled it. The problem: Arias' iPhone was issued by her employer, which required her to run the app constantly, and after she removed it, the California woman was fired. Now she's suing her former employer, money transfer service Intermex, for invasion of privacy, unfair business practices, and retaliation, among other things, Ars Technica reports. Arias started researching the app, then called Xora—its website touts the app's ability to let employers "instantly see where their employees are and where they have been"—and eventually she and some of her colleagues asked a trainer from the app company to clarify. Boss John Stubits then, per the suit, "admitted that employees would be monitored while off duty and bragged that he knew how fast she was driving at specific moments." Arias says she has no problem being monitored while at work, but she complained to Stubits about being tracked in her free time; he was unsympathetic and said she should have no problem because of how much she was being paid by Intermex. "Her manager made it clear that he was using the program to continuously monitor her, during company as well as personal time," the complaint states, noting that he told Arias she needed to keep her phone on at all times in case a customer called. Arias' attorney tells Courthouse News that there was no way to turn the app off, as it would constantly run in the background. "She found it very offensive that they were treating her like a felon," the attorney says. Arias says her boss "scolded" her for uninstalling the program last year, and fired her soon after. (Elsewhere, a woman got fired over a text mistakenly sent to the boss.)

**Freeway Shooting Cases: Recent Developments and a Fatal Shooting in Alabama**

In recent news, there have been significant developments in the freeway shooting cases that plagued the Phoenix area last year. Leslie Allen Merritt Jr., the man charged with four of the freeway shootings, was granted a reduced bond and allowed to return home under electronic monitoring. The defense lawyers argued that ballistic tests cast doubt on the claim that Merritt was behind the shootings, and the judge reduced the bond from $1 million to zero. Merritt has pleaded not guilty to the charges and has been in jail in solitary confinement for seven months.

Meanwhile, in a separate incident, a driver suspected of firing a gun at motorists on an Alabama highway and exchanging gunfire with officers was killed. The incident occurred in Blount County, about 45 miles northeast of Birmingham, and began around 7 a.m. Tuesday. The driver fled from officers and crashed, leading to an exchange of gunfire. The suspect's name and other details were not released.

The two incidents highlight the ongoing concerns about freeway safety and the need for law enforcement to address these issues. In the Phoenix case, the defense lawyers argued that the evidence against Merritt was weak, and the judge's decision to reduce the bond suggests that there may be more to the story than initially meets the eye. In the Alabama case, the fatal shooting raises questions about the use of force by law enforcement and the need for de-escalation techniques.

The Phoenix case has also sparked a debate about the use of social media in the pursuit of justice. Gov. Doug Ducey's tweet "We got him!" after Merritt's arrest was seen as premature and insensitive, and highlights the need for law enforcement to be cautious in their public statements.

Overall, these two incidents highlight the complexities and challenges of freeway shooting cases and the need for law enforcement to address these issues in a thoughtful and nuanced manner.
details.ground_truth: A judge on Tuesday allowed a man charged in freeway shootings that rattled Phoenix last year to be released from jail amid questions about evidence authorities say links him to the crimes. The judge overseeing the case of Leslie Merritt Jr. reduced his bond to zero and said he can return to his home under electronic monitoring, the AP reports. He was expected to be released later in the day. The reduction of the bond—once $1 million—was a major victory as defense lawyers contend that ballistic tests cast doubt on the claim by authorities that Merritt was behind four of the freeway shootings. "With all due respect your honor, there's no evidence against him to show he's responsible for this," defense lawyer Jason Lamm said. "He is no more the I-10 shooter than, respectfully, you are." Merritt lifted up his shackled arms in celebration as he walked from the courtroom. After the hearing, family members hugged and shook the hands of defense lawyers. Merritt has pleaded not guilty to drive-by shooting, aggravated assault, and other charges. The shootings caused panic on Phoenix-area freeways, where 11 vehicles were hit in August and September. Detectives took Merritt into custody on Sept. 18; in court the next day, Merritt adamantly denied shooting any cars, telling the judge, "I'm the wrong guy." His lawyers immediately began raising questions about the evidence, citing ballistics information and phone records they say provided an alibi for their client. (Also Tuesday, a motorist suspected of shooting at other vehicles on an Alabama highway died after a shootout with officers.)

**Multiple Shootings in the US: A Tragic and Unsettling Trend**

In the past 24 hours, the United States has witnessed a disturbing and tragic series of shootings, leaving a trail of death and injury in its wake. The most recent incident occurred at a Rite Aid distribution center in Harford County, Maryland, where a 26-year-old temporary employee, identified as Snochia Moseley, shot and killed three people and injured three others before taking her own life.

According to reports, Moseley, who had no prior criminal record, used a 9mm Glock pistol and brought several magazines for the gun, which was registered to her. The shooting began outside the building and moved inside, with law enforcement officers responding quickly and arriving on the scene in just over five minutes. Four gunshot victims were taken to Johns Hopkins Bayview Medical Center, with two in stable condition and two "very seriously injured." One other victim is in serious condition at Christiana Hospital in Newark, Delaware.

This incident is the third high-profile shooting in the US in the past two days, following a shooting at an office complex in Middleton, Wisconsin, which left three people hurt, and a gunman who wounded four people at the Masontown Borough Municipal Center in Pennsylvania.

The Rite Aid shooting is also the latest in a series of gun violence incidents to beset Maryland and other parts of the country. In June, a gunman stormed the Capital Gazette newsroom in Annapolis, killing five staff members. In March, a student at Great Mills High School in Southern Maryland shot and killed a 16-year-old classmate and injured a 14-year-old boy. And just 11 months ago, three co-workers were killed at a kitchen countertop company in Edgewood, with two fellow employees also injured in the attack.

The frequency and severity of these incidents have sparked outrage and calls for action from lawmakers and anti-gun violence activists. Former US Rep. Gabrielle Giffords, who was shot in the head at a "Congress on Your Corner" event in 2011, said that the three workplace active shooting attacks in the past 24 hours "should spark outrage in every American." She added that gun violence has become an "everyday occurrence" and that it's essential to remind ourselves that this level of violence is not normal.

Governor Larry Hogan has offered his condolences and support to the families of the victims and the first responders who responded to the scene. The Harford County Emergency Operations Center has been partially activated,
details.ground_truth: News that a shooting left multiple people dead may sound depressingly familiar, but the one outside Baltimore on Thursday is unique in at least one way: The shooter was female. Police say the 26-year-old woman, who has not been identified, died of a self-inflicted gunshot wound to the head. Harford County Sheriff Jeff Gahler said she was a temporary employee at the Rite Aid distribution center where the shooting took place, reports the Baltimore Sun. Police say the suspect killed three people, and three others remain hospitalized with wounds not believed to be life threatening. Details and developments: When, where: The Rite Aid distribution center where the shooting took place is at a business complex in Perryman, about 30 miles from Baltimore, reports WBAL. Deputies got there at 9:06am, within 5 minutes of the first call, said the sheriff. He said that deputies fired no shots and that the shooter used a 9mm Glock semiautomatic handgun. Why: Police aren't speculating about a motive. “Normally, she was a nice person, but she came in in a bad mood,” Mike Carre, an employee at a nearby business, tells the Washington Post, recounting what Rite Aid workers told him. "She wanted to pick a fight," he said. "And then she started shooting.” CNN quotes a law enforcement official who describes her as a disgruntled employee; the site says she worked there as a security guard. The woman lived in Baltimore County, and the gun was legally registered to her.

**New Study Suggests Acetaminophen May Increase Risk of ADHD in Children**

A recent study published in the Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA) Pediatrics has found that pregnant women who frequently take acetaminophen, commonly found in over-the-counter pain relievers such as Tylenol, may be at a higher risk of having children diagnosed with attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) and similar disorders.

The study, which analyzed data from over 64,000 children in the Danish National Birth Cohort study, found that women who took acetaminophen for longer periods and later in pregnancy were associated with higher risks. Specifically, the study found that:

* Women who took acetaminophen for 20 weeks or more had a 50% increased risk of their children receiving ADHD medication.
* Women who took acetaminophen were more likely to have children later diagnosed with ADHD and similar issues, with a 29% higher risk of ADHD and a 37% higher risk of a rarer diagnosis called hyperkinetic syndrome.

The study's authors suggest that acetaminophen may increase the risk of ADHD by interfering with maternal hormones that are critical for fetal brain development. However, they emphasize that the study does not show a cause-and-effect relationship and that more research is needed to confirm the findings.

**Experts Weigh in on the Study**

Dr. Beate Ritz, a professor and chair of the epidemiology department at the University of California, Los Angeles Fielding School of Public Health, says that pregnant women shouldn't worry at this point, but that they should try to avoid taking painkillers as much as they can until more is known about the association.

Dr. Max Wiznitzer, a pediatric neurologist and associate professor at Case Western Reserve University School of Medicine, says that the study highlights the importance of not taking a drug's safety during pregnancy for granted and that there are nonpharmacological ways to deal with pain, such as massages, baths, and acupuncture.

**What to Do if You're Pregnant and Need to Take Acetaminophen**

If you're pregnant and need to take acetaminophen, the American Academy of Pediatrics recommends that you:

* Take the lowest dose possible for the shortest amount of time necessary.
* Avoid taking acetaminophen for chronic or long-term use.
* Consider talking to your doctor about alternative pain relief options, such as nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) or other over-the-counter pain
details.ground_truth: The bad news about acetaminophen just keeps on coming: A new study finds that use of the drug during pregnancy is linked to "ADHD-like behavioral problems" in children, CNN reports. The Danish study looked at data from more than 64,000 children and found that those who had prenatal exposure to acetaminophen had a 13% higher risk of exhibiting ADHD-like behaviors, a 29% higher risk of being prescribed ADHD medication, and a 37% higher risk of receiving a diagnosis of hyperkinetic disorder, a severe form of ADHD, USA Today reports. The risk increased the later in pregnancy the drug was taken and the longer it was taken. Women who used acetaminophen for 20 weeks or longer had a 50% higher chance of their children being prescribed ADHD medication. But the authors did not find a cause-and-effect relationship, and they note that "exhibiting ADHD-like behaviors" is not the same as actually having ADHD. Right now, doctors consider acetaminophen the "safest" pain reliever for pregnant women, NBC News reports; the study author says pregnant women "shouldn't worry at this point." What could account for the link? The authors think acetaminophen may interfere with key maternal hormones that impact fetal brain development. Another recent study also found that frequent acetaminophen use during pregnancy was linked with a 70% higher risk of behavioral problems in children.

**Bill Cosby Sentenced to Prison, Accuses Racism and Sexism in the Justice System**

On September 25, 2018, Bill Cosby, the 81-year-old actor and comedian, was sentenced to three to 10 years in state prison for drugging and sexually assaulting a woman, Andrea Constand, at his home outside Philadelphia in 2004. The conviction marked a significant milestone in the #MeToo era, which has seen numerous high-profile figures accused of sexual misconduct.

In a bizarre and fiery press conference outside the courthouse, Cosby's publicist, Andrew Wyatt, accused the judge and his wife of being part of a "sex war" that is currently unfolding in Washington, D.C. with the Supreme Court nominee Brett Kavanaugh. Wyatt also called Cosby's trial the "most racist and sexist trial in the history of the United States," and claimed that Cosby was a "great educator of men and boys" and one of the "greatest civil rights leaders."

Wyatt's comments sparked a wave of criticism and outrage, with many accusing him of trying to deflect and downplay the seriousness of Cosby's crimes. Cosby's team has repeatedly invoked the persecution of Jesus and Emmett Till, a black boy murdered after a white woman falsely accused him of whistling at her, to try to garner sympathy and support.

**Cosby's First Night in Prison**

After his sentencing, Cosby was taken away in handcuffs and spent his first night behind bars at SCI Phoenix, a new 3,830-bed state prison in suburban Philadelphia. The Department of Corrections says he will serve his three-to-10-year sentence at SCI Phoenix, where he will be allowed phone calls and visitation in accordance with policy. Cosby is being housed in a single cell in a unit adjacent to the infirmary, and will be assessed by correction officials to determine where it would be best for him to complete his sentence.

**Kavanaugh's Nomination in Jeopardy**

The controversy surrounding Cosby's trial and sentencing has sparked a renewed debate about the justice system and its treatment of black men. Meanwhile, the Supreme Court nominee Brett Kavanaugh is facing a similar crisis, with two women accusing him of sexual assault and misconduct in incidents dating back to the 1980s. Christine Blasey Ford is set to testify before the Senate Judiciary Committee on Thursday, and a second accuser, Deborah Ramirez, has come forward with her own story of sexual misconduct.
details.ground_truth: Bill Cosby was labeled a "violent sexual predator" by the judge who sentenced him to three to 10 years in prison on Tuesday, but his publicist had a different description: one of the "greatest civil rights leaders" in history and one of the "greatest educators of men and boys." Outside the courtroom in Norristown, Pa., publicist Andrew Wyatt claimed the 81-year-old Cosby had faced the "most racist and sexist trial" in American history, the New York Times reports. Wyatt claimed that three psychologists who testified against Cosby in the sexual assault trial were trying to "make money off of accusing black men of being sexual predators." He also read a statement from Camille Cosby, the disgraced entertainer's wife, alleging that an audio recording played in the trial had been doctored. Wyatt, who previously called Cosby's trial a "public lynching," accused Judge Steven O'Neill of being part of a "sex war" and linked the sentencing to the treatment of Supreme Court nominee Brett Kavanaugh. Vox reports that the strangest moment came when Wyatt likened his client to Jesus. "They persecuted Jesus, and look what happened," he said. "Not saying Mr. Cosby is Jesus, but we know what this country has done to black men for centuries." Cosby, who left the Montgomery County Courthouse in handcuffs, spent his first night behind bars at SCI Phoenix, a state prison that opened two months ago in suburban Philadelphia, reports WPVI. (Cosby's mug shot was released Tuesday afternoon.)

**U.S. Secretary of State Hillary Clinton Rules Out Presidential Run in 2012 and 2016**

In a series of interviews during her Asia-Pacific tour, U.S. Secretary of State Hillary Clinton has made it clear that she has no plans to run for the top U.S. office in either 2012 or 2016. Clinton, who was the Democratic presidential nominee in 2008, stated that she is "very pleased to be doing what I'm doing as Secretary of State" and that she does not intend to pursue a presidential run.

Clinton's comments were made in response to speculation that she might run for president again, particularly in the wake of the Democrats' poor showing in the mid-term elections. However, Clinton emphasized that she hopes the United States is ready for a female president, adding "it should be" and that it is nice to visit countries that have already proven that they can elect women to the highest governing positions.

Clinton's tour of the Asia-Pacific region has taken her to New Zealand, where she was met with a slight gaffe by the country's Prime Minister John Phillip Key, who accidentally introduced her as "President Clinton" before correcting himself. Clinton has also sought to defend President Barack Obama and put the Democrats' losses in the mid-term elections in perspective, stating that they are "big" but not out of the pattern of historical political elections.

Overall, Clinton's comments have put an end to speculation about her potential presidential run, and she will continue to serve as Secretary of State until her term is up.

Asked in another interview whether she could be the first female president, she answered, "Well, not me. But it will be someone ..." The significance? On another front, Clinton said the midterm losses were "big—but not out of the pattern of historical political elections." She added, "I think the president made very clear was he made decisions which were essential for the well-being of the American people."

Schumer has been a vocal supporter of Colin Kaepernick's peaceful protest against racism and police brutality in the NFL. She recently urged Maroon 5 to pull out of the Super Bowl Halftime Show as Rihanna reportedly did, in solidarity with Kaepernick's cause.

Schumer has also been supporting the #NewsNotNoise campaign, which aims to educate people about what they need to know versus what noise they can tune out. In a recent development, Schumer was reportedly detained and arrested for protesting Brett Kavanaugh's nomination to the Supreme Court. This is just the latest example of Schumer's commitment to social activism and her willingness to take a stand on the issues she cares about.

Schumer has been a vocal advocate for gun control, the transgender community, and other causes she cares about. Her commitment to politics and social activism has made her a respected and influential voice in the entertainment industry.

Overall, Schumer's recent announcements and statements reflect her commitment to politics, social activism, and using her platform to make a difference.

Yellin, of the site NewsNotNoise.org, showed at the end of a list of Schumer's recommended congressional and gubernatorial candidates the line: "I'm pregnant-Amy Schumer." Schumer had teased the announcement on her own Instagram page, captioning a photo of hers and Fischer's heads atop Prince Harry's and Meghan Markle's bodies, "About to announce some exciting news on @jessicayellin insta page. Please follow her for up to the minute #newsnotnoise she breaks down what’s really going on. She agreed to post a lil noise today for me! Follow her and VOTE!!" **Tyson Foods Faces Animal Abuse Allegations, Promises to Retrain and Improve Animal Welfare**

Tyson Foods, one of the world's largest meat producers, is facing new allegations of animal abuse after an undercover investigation by the animal rights group Compassion Over Killing. The video, released in August 2016, shows Tyson workers throwing, punching, and kicking chickens, as well as sticking plastic rods through their beaks in a process known as "boning." The video also depicts workers wringing birds' necks, running over them with forklifts, and leaving injured birds to die in "dead piles."

In response to the allegations, Tyson Foods fired 10 workers who were members of the crew depicted in the video and discontinued the process of "boning" at two of the facilities in the video. The company's vice president of sustainable food production, Christine Daugherty, described the employees' actions as "disgusting" and "inexcusable" and stated that animals in the company's care deserve to be treated humanely.

However, this is not the first time that Tyson Foods has faced animal abuse allegations. In January 2006, a similar undercover investigation by Compassion Over Killing showed workers at a Tyson facility stomping, kicking, and suffocating breeder chickens. The company responded to the allegations by saying that the abusers were not reflective of Tyson's institutional commitment to animal welfare.

In light of the latest video, Tyson Foods has announced that it will retrain all of its live poultry workers on the company's animal welfare policies. The company is also evaluating further steps it can take to ensure animal well-being procedures are being followed throughout its operations. The new video has also spurred Tyson to discontinue the practice of inserting plastic tubes in rooster's beaks, a process known as beak modification.

Compassion Over Killing has made formal complaints about the abuse to prosecutors and animal control officers in Mecklenburg, Lunenburg, and Buckingham counties, and no charges have been filed as of Wednesday. The group's executive director, Erica Meier, stated that Tyson Foods needs to be held accountable for the abuse and that it is a systemic problem.

Tyson Foods has also been in touch with local authorities about the video, but declined to say whether the company wants to see the employees charged. **Donald Trump's Campaign Shake-up and Michael Cohen's "Says Who?" Moment**

In a bid to regain favor in the polls, Donald Trump's campaign has undergone a major shake-up, with the appointment of new leaders, including Stephen Bannon, the head of Breitbart News, as campaign chief executive, and Kellyanne Conway as campaign manager. However, Trump's special counsel and lawyer, Michael Cohen, has been at the center of controversy, with an awkward exchange on CNN's Brianna Keilar, where he repeatedly asked "Says who?" when questioned about Trump's low ratings in the polls.

The exchange, which has gone viral on social media, saw Keilar respond with "Polls. Most of them. All of them?" to which Cohen asked again, "Says who?" The exchange has been mocked on Twitter, with users spamming Cohen with "Says who?" in response to all his tweets.

The shake-up comes just a few months before the election, and has been seen as a desperate attempt by Trump to regain ground in the polls. According to recent polls, Hillary Clinton is leading in most states, including Virginia, Colorado, and Iowa, while Trump is winning in Indiana, where his running mate, Mike Pence, is the governor.

The controversy surrounding Cohen's "Says who?" moment has also raised questions about his role as Trump's special counsel and lawyer, and his aggressive defense of Trump against the media. Interestingly, Cohen was a registered Democrat who voted for Barack Obama in 2008, and has a history of threatening to sue multiple organizations over stories.

Overall, the shake-up and Cohen's "Says who?" moment have dominated the headlines, and have raised questions about Trump's campaign strategy and the role of his advisors and lawyers.

In an interview with CNN Wednesday, notoriously combative Trump Organization attorney Michael Cohen interrupted host Brianna Keilar when she said that the campaign was "down," Politico reports. "Says who?" he asked. "Polls. Most of them. All of them?" she replied. "Says who?" Cohen asked again. When she repeated it was polls, he responded, "OK, which polls?” and Keilar said: “All of them." USA Today reports that Twitter users were quick to mock Cohen and are now replying "Says who?" to all his tweets. Cohen, in what Law Newz calls "typical lawyer fashion," also took issue with Trump's overhaul of campaign staff being called a "shake-up," insisting it was actually a "change-up." Cohen also raised eyebrows when he told Kielar that Trump knows about "the African-American problem in this country," the Independent reports. "What I meant to say is the problem that exists in the African-American community," he later said. (Michael Moore says he "knows for a fact" that Trump never really wanted to be president.)

**South Carolina Deputy Fired After Viral Video of Student Confrontation**

A South Carolina sheriff's deputy, Senior Deputy Ben Fields, has been fired after a viral video showed him forcefully removing a student from a classroom at Spring Valley High School in Columbia. The incident, which occurred on Monday, has sparked widespread outrage and calls for the deputy to be charged with assault.

According to the sheriff, Leon Lott, the internal investigation into the incident found that Fields' use of force was unacceptable and that he did not follow proper procedure. Lott said that Fields was sorry for how the confrontation played out, but that his actions were not justified.

The student, who has not been identified, suffered several injuries during the arrest, including a cast on her arm, neck and back injuries, and a rug burn on her forehead. Her attorney, Todd Rutherford, said that she was not a threat to anyone and that the deputy's actions were excessive and unreasonable.

The incident has sparked a national conversation about the use of police force in schools and the need for more training and certification for school resource officers. Many districts across the country have put officers in schools after the Columbine High School massacre in 1999, but experts say that schools are now relying too heavily on police to discipline students.

The FBI, the Justice Department, and state law enforcement have opened separate investigations into the incident, and the president of the South Carolina chapter of the NAACP has called for Fields to be charged with assault. School district officials have also blasted Fields' actions as "outrageous" and "reprehensible," and the video itself "shamefully shocking."

**Key Facts:**

* Senior Deputy Ben Fields was fired after a viral video showed him forcefully removing a student from a classroom at Spring Valley High School in Columbia.
* The internal investigation into the incident found that Fields' use of force was unacceptable and that he did not follow proper procedure.
* The student suffered several injuries during the arrest, including a cast on her arm, neck and back injuries, and a rug burn on her forehead.
* The FBI, the Justice Department, and state law enforcement have opened separate investigations into the incident.
* The president of the South Carolina chapter of the NAACP has called for Fields to be charged with assault.
* School district officials have blasted Fields' actions as "outrageous" and "reprehensible," and the video itself "shamefully shocking."

**Headline:** Mark David Chapman's Wife Hopes for His Release After 38 Years in Prison, While Internet Archive Aims to Fix Broken Links on the Web

**Subheading:** Gloria Hiroko Chapman, wife of the man who murdered John Lennon, expresses her devotion to her husband and hopes for his release, while the Internet Archive works to preserve and fix broken links on the web.

**Mark David Chapman's Wife Hopes for His Release**

Gloria Hiroko Chapman, the wife of Mark David Chapman, who murdered John Lennon in 1980, is hoping that her husband may soon be free after 38 years behind bars. Chapman was given 20 years to life for the crime and has had to wait nearly four decades for a parole hearing. Gloria, 67, has expressed her devotion to her husband and has refused to divorce him, citing her Christian faith and the vows they exchanged when they married in 1979. Despite admitting that she was violently abused during their marriage, Gloria has said that she will wait for Mark, who is due to have a parole hearing in August.

**The Internet Archive Aims to Fix Broken Links on the Web**

The Internet Archive, a non-profit organization, is working to preserve and fix broken links on the web. The organization uses crawlers to archive pages as they are created and to archive the pages that they refer to. This way, as the pages that are referenced are changed or taken from the web, a link to the version that was live when the page was written will be preserved. The goal is to fix all broken links on the web, and the organization is working with "No More 404" sites to achieve this goal.

**In Related News**

John Lennon's widow, Yoko Ono, has objected to Chapman's parole, citing the severity of the crime. However, the New York State parole board has been more willing to place emphasis on an inmate's behavior behind bars than focus on the severity of the crime, which may bode well for Chapman's chances of release.

**Conclusion**

Gloria Hiroko Chapman's devotion to her husband and her hopes for his release are a complex and nuanced issue, while the Internet Archive's efforts to preserve and fix broken links on the web are a vital and important task. As the world continues to evolve and change, it is more and more important to preserve and fix the links that connect us to our past and to each other.

Mark David Chapman, now 63, was turned down again this week. Chapman, who shot Lennon in New York City in 1980, is up again for parole in about two years. Chapman's wife recently told the UK Mirror that he talked of killing Lennon two months before actually doing so.

The Pentagon's official Twitter account, @DeptofDefense, recently made a mistake that sparked a controversy. On November 16, 2017, the account retweeted a tweet from @proudresister, calling for President Donald Trump to resign from the presidency due to allegations of sexual misconduct. The operator caught the error and immediately deleted it.

Pentagon press secretary Dana White also tweeted a clarification, which was shared by the Pentagon's official Twitter account. Many Twitter profiles contain a disclaimer saying that retweets do not constitute an endorsement, but the Pentagon account does not have this disclaimer.

"The Lovely Bones" is a film adaptation of the bestselling novel by Alice Sebold, directed by Peter Jackson and starring Saoirse Ronan, Stanley Tucci, Mark Wahlberg, and Rachel Weisz. The story follows the life of Susie Salmon, a 14-year-old girl who is murdered by her neighbor George Harvey in 1973. Susie's spirit is trapped in a limbo between heaven and earth, where she watches her family and friends go on with their lives.

The film has received mixed reviews from critics, with some praising Ronan's performance as Susie and the film's visuals, while others have criticized the film's tone and its departure from the original novel. The film's portrayal of the afterlife is also seen as cheesy and unoriginal, breaking no ground in depicting a way station between heaven and Earth.

The film's thriller scenes are well-executed, with a tense sequence in which a detective and Harvey eye each other through the windows of a dollhouse, and a suspenseful episode in which Susie's sister Lindsey sneaks into Harvey's house to find evidence. However, the conceit of Susie trapped in a DayGlo world between the one she left and her final resting place, imparting lessons on coping with death, feels preachy and is more moving when she simply utters: "I was here for a moment, and then I was gone."

The film's rating is PG-13 for mature thematic material involving disturbing violent content and images, and some language. It runs for 2 hours and 15 minutes and opens nationwide nationwide on a specific date.
details.ground_truth: Peter Jackson's crafts a fantastic vision of the afterlife in The Lovely Bones, say critics, but some feel it came at the expense of the human side of the story of a murdered teen, adapted from the book by Alice Sebold. "By turns warmly sentimental, serial-killer sinister, and science-fiction fantastical, The Lovely Bones was an unlikely book to achieve worldwide success," Kenneth Turan writes in the Los Angeles Times. "Those mismatched elements come back to haunt" the film version, "making the final product more hit-and-miss than unblemished triumph." "Sebold's Lovely Bones is fleshed out with the perilous, irresistible power of sex—a real world of extramarital sex and sex between young lovers in addition to the heinous rape from which moviegoers are shielded." But Jackson "shies from the challenge, shortchanging a story that isn't only about the lightness of souls in heaven but also about the urges of bodies on earth," writes Lisa Schwarzbaum at Entertainment Weekly. "Through Jackson's art" and the magic of star Saoirse Rogan, Richard Corliss writes at Time, "the obscenity of child murder has been invested with immense gravity and grace." "Some books are not meant to be adapted to the big screen," and The Lovely Bones is one of them, writes Claudia Puig at USA Today. What works in the book, she writes, comes off as "artificial and emotionless on-screen."

details.ground_truth: A video released online appears to show a Mexican soldier executing a suspected oil thief at point-blank range as he lays defenseless on the ground. The heavily edited video, taken from a surveillance camera, shows a car coming under fire during what the military describes as a series of ambushes that left four soldiers and six suspects dead in Palmarito on May 3. As soldiers surround the vehicle, its occupants exit and are forced to lie down in front of the car. Soldiers are then seen dragging a suspect to the same spot. He appears injured but rolls from his back onto his front. Six minutes later, a soldier appears at the side of the frame and appears to shoot the man in the back of the head, per the AP. A dark stain spreads around him, reports Reuters. "There was already concern about the use of excessive force by the military. Now this video seems to give us the proof," says the chairwoman of Mexico's Senate Commission on Human Rights, per the Washington Post, which identifies the man shot as Raul Jimenez Martinez, 46. Mexico's defense ministry, however, says it will cooperate fully with an investigation launched by the attorney general's office on May 4. "Under no circumstances can conduct contrary to the law and human rights be justified," the ministry says. Residents of Palmarito have already taken to the streets to protest the deaths of the suspects, placing the blame on the army, though the defense ministry previously claimed the suspects used residents as human shields.

**Tragic Death of 20-Year-Old Photography Enthusiast at Four Seasons Hotel in Manhattan**

A 20-year-old man from Rockaway, New Jersey, Connor Cummings, fell to his death on Wednesday while attempting to take pictures from the scaffolding of the 52-story Four Seasons Hotel in Manhattan. Cummings, a sophomore psychology major at the University of Massachusetts at Amherst and an avid photography enthusiast, was with his 18-year-old friend Dimitri Olivares when the incident occurred.

According to police, Cummings and Olivares had taken the hotel elevator to the top floor and then climbed up two flights of stairs to the rooftop exit. They then accessed a fenced-off area with mechanical equipment and climbed a ladder to get a panoramic shot of the city. Cummings lost his footing and fell 25 feet, landing on the hotel's roof, where he was pronounced dead at the scene.

Olivares, who was left shaken and traumatized by the incident, initially fled the scene in a taxi cab but later returned to the hotel to report what had happened to hotel security and police. He was questioned by detectives and released without charges.

The incident has sparked an investigation, with hotel officials working with police to determine the circumstances surrounding Cummings' death. The hotel has expressed its condolences to Cummings' family and emphasized the importance of safety and security for its guests, patrons, and employees.

Cummings' friends and family have remembered him as a kind, passionate, and dedicated individual who loved photography and was committed to helping others. His fraternity fraternity has posted a message on Facebook mourning his death and describing his "fiery passion" for photography.

The incident serves as a reminder of the risks and dangers associated with climbing and accessing restricted areas, and the importance of prioritizing safety and security in all endeavors.
details.ground_truth: Connor Cummins' family describes him to WABC-TV as a photography devotee—and that enthusiasm may have cost him his life. The New Jersey man, said to be either 20 or 24 years old, and an 18-year-old pal ascended the scaffolding outside NYC's Four Seasons hotel Wednesday night in search of the perfect panoramic picture—but Cummins may have slipped on railings made slippery by the rain and fell nine stories to his death, cops tell NJ.com. An NYPD spokesman tells the New York Daily News that the initial investigation suggests the men weren't guests of the hotel. Cummins, who originally hails from Rockaway and whose Facebook page indicates he's now a student at the University of Massachusetts Amherst, per ABC, landed on one of the hotel's lower-level rooftops around 11:15pm and was pronounced dead at the scene, reports the New York Post. His friend wasn't injured and was questioned by authorities to fill in the blanks; he was released Thursday morning without being charged. "He was a loving boy, that's all I can say," one of Cummins' relatives tells the Post. "I don't know what happened." (There's more suspicion surrounding a Muslim teen's fatal fall off a Seattle building.)

**Truckers' Protest in Washington, D.C. - A Summary of the News**

A group of truck drivers, organized by the "Truckers for the Constitution" and "Truckers to Shut Down America" (T2SDA), have planned to protest the federal government by backing up the Capital Beltway around Washington, D.C. The protest, which is scheduled to take place on October 11, aims to draw attention to the truckers' grievances, including low wages, high fuel costs, and excessive regulations from the Obama administration.

The truckers have framed their decision to protest as a necessity to get the attention of lawmakers, who they believe are more concerned with their own wealth and power than with serving the people. They have also expressed their disapproval of national political leaders, including President Obama, Secretary of State John Kerry, and Senators John McCain and Lindsey Graham, whom they have labeled as "domestic enemies."

However, in a recent update, it has been reported that the truckers are no longer planning to arrest members of Congress, as previously stated. Instead, they will circle the beltway "three lanes deep" and keep the left lane open for emergency vehicles. D.C. commuters who wish to be allowed past the convoy must have "T2SDA" written on their vehicle.

The truckers' protest is not the first of its kind. A similar three-day protest in 2007 against illegal immigration and competition from Mexican truckers did not spoil commutes as feared. The Facebook page advertising the upcoming ride has close to 50,000 likes, indicating a significant level of support and interest.

The truckers' grievances are not limited to their industry alone. They have also expressed their concerns about the government's surveillance of American citizens, the national debt, and the perceived erosion of the U.S. Constitution. They believe that the only way to get their legislators to operate lawfully and constitutionally is to penalize them with a credible threat of a shutdown.

In summary, the truckers' protest in Washington, D.C. is a complex and multifaceted event that reflects the deep-seated frustrations and concerns of a group of workers who feel that their rights and interests are being ignored and disregarded by the government.
details.ground_truth: Protesting truckers are planning a shutdown of their own—on Washington's beltway. Starting on Friday morning, they intend to fill the road "three lanes deep" with tractor-trailers, an organizer of "Truckers Ride for the Constitution" tells US News and World Report. In a demonstration against what they view as violations of the Constitution, planners like Earl Conlon are calling for "the arrest of everyone in government who has violated their oath of office." Their grievances include truckers' wages, industry regulations, and NSA spying, the Hill reports. They're also accusing President Obama of treason for allegedly putting weapons in the hands of rebels with ties to al-Qaeda. Organizers have received some 3,000 RSVPs, says one. Moving at the 55mph speed limit, they'll leave a lane open for emergency vehicles; vehicles with "T2SDA," or "Truckers to Shut Down America," displayed will also be allowed to pass. "Everybody that doesn't have a supporter sticker on their window, good luck: Nobody in, nobody out," says Conlon. A Facebook page for the event has more than 55,000 "likes."

A growing scandal has engulfed the Secret Service, with multiple agents and military personnel accused of participating in a prostitution ring during a trip to Colombia ahead of the Summit of the Americas. * David Chaney, a 48-year-old supervisor and former member of the presidential protection detail, was forced out of the agency after making light of his official protective work on his Facebook page, joking about a picture of himself standing watch behind Sarah Palin.
* Greg Stokes, another senior supervisor, was notified by agency officials that he will be fired, although he will be given an opportunity to contest the charges.
* 11 Secret Service agents and 10 military personnel are suspected of bringing as many as 21 prostitutes to their rooms.
* Three of the men were being dismissed from the agency for their involvement, with a junior member of the team voluntarily electing to resign.

**Investigation and Fallout**

* The Secret Service recalled its 11 employees and replaced them with another team before Obama arrived on April 13.
* All of the recalled members were placed on administrative leave, and their top-secret security clearances were revoked.
* Capitol Hill lawmakers who oversee the Department of Homeland Security expected more resignations and firings in the case.
* Rep. Peter T. King (R-N.Y.) said the 11 agents involved in the scandal underwent drug tests and polygraph exams.
* Agency investigators in Colombia have visited all of the hotels where Secret Service personnel stayed and have interviewed each of the maids who cleaned rooms in the Hotel Caribe.

**Reaction from Lawmakers and the Public**

* Rep. Nancy Pelosi (D-Calif.) called the incident "disgusting" and said those responsible should have to pay a price.
* Rep. Randy Forbes (R-Va.) called for leadership changes at the Secret Service, saying "you get three strikes" and the agency has had them.
* Sarah Palin responded to the Post's revelations about Chaney's comments by saying she was disgusted that a Secret Service agent would make jokes about checking out her "backside" and called his behavior "pretty embarrassing."
* Fox News host Greta Van Susteren told Palin she agreed Obama should be held accountable for how he reacted to the incident
details.ground_truth: One of the two Secret Service supervisors to lose their jobs so far over the Colombia sex scandal joked that he was checking out Sarah Palin as he guarded her during the 2008 campaign, the Washington Post finds. Senior agent David Chaney posted at least two pictures of himself with Palin on Facebook, one with the comment, "I was really checking her out, if you know what I mean." Chaney has been forced to resign, and another supervisor has been informed that he will be fired. In an interview with Fox News, Palin said the joke is on Chaney. "Well check this out, buddy—you're fired!" she said, calling the scandal "a symptom of government run amok," reports AP. "It's like, who's minding the store around here?" she added. "The president, for one, he better be wary, there, of when Secret Service is accompanying his family on vacation. They may be checking out the first lady instead of guarding her."

**Falling from the Top: 5 Notable Individuals Who Lost Their Stature**

In a culture that often glorifies fame, fortune, and power, it's not uncommon for individuals to experience a precipitous loss in stature. 24/7 Wall St. recently compiled a list of 50 well-known individuals who have fallen from the top, and we take a closer look at 5 of the most notable cases.

**Anthony Scaramucci: The Short-Lived White House Communications Director**

Anthony Scaramucci's tenure as White House Communications Director was the shortest in the position's nearly 50-year history, lasting just 11 days. His tumultuous time in the Trump administration was marked by protocol breaches, conflicting statements, and a profanity-laced interview with The New Yorker. His wife's divorce filing during his brief stint added to the chaos.

**Chris Christie: The Governor with the Lowest Approval Ratings**

Chris Christie, the outgoing Governor of New Jersey, has the lowest approval ratings of any governor in the state's history. His reputation was sullied by the Bridgegate scandal, and his decision to fawn over Donald Trump after the presidential election didn't help. His recent decision to lounge on a state-owned beach while the public was denied access due to a government shutdown only added to the criticism.

**James Comey: The FBI Director Who Lost His Job**

James Comey's time as FBI Director was marked by controversy, particularly his decision to publicly attack Hillary Clinton's judgment and announce an investigation into her just days before the election. His firing by Trump in May 2017 was met with widespread criticism, and the White House's conflicting justifications for the decision only added to the confusion.

**Mark Fields: The CEO Who Failed to Inspire Confidence**

Mark Fields, the former CEO of Ford Motor Company, was shown the door in 2017 after his failure to inspire confidence on Wall Street. His decision to invest in electric and autonomous vehicle technology, despite the company's declining profits, was seen as a misstep. His eventual departure was met with a 10% reduction in the global workforce, an attempt to boost profits.

**Tiger Woods: The Golfer Who Lost His Way**

Tiger Woods, once the top-ranked golfer in the world, has been embroiled in controversy on and off the course since 2009. His latest scandal, a DUI arrest, was just the latest in a string of incidents that have seen him fall from the top. His current ranking of 899 is a far
details.ground_truth: Power: One day you have it, the next you don't. No one likely knows that better than the world's 50 "least powerful" people, described by 24/7 Wall St. as "well-known individuals around the world who have recently experienced a precipitous loss in stature." The top 10: Anthony Scaramucci: Where to begin? Try here, here, and here. Chris Christie: The former head of Donald Trump's transition team has the lowest approval rating of any governor in New Jersey's history. James Comey: The former FBI director lasted not even five months under Trump. Mark Fields: The Ford CEO was canned after shares fell almost 40% during his tenure. Tiger Woods: Blame his DUI arrest and 899th ranking in men's pro golf. Kendall Jenner: Remember that Pepsi commercial? Montgomery Moran: After a wave of issues at Chipotle, the co-CEO stepped down last year. Tomi Lahren: The conservative media personality was suspended from the Blaze after announcing she was pro-choice. Park Geun-hye: The former president of South Korea was impeached and jailed after a corruption scandal involving Samsung. Mitch McConnell: As Senate majority leader, McConnell failed to repeal and replace the Affordable Care Act. Click for the full list, which also includes Sean Spicer, R. Kelly, and Theresa May. **Government Shutdown Looms as Congress Fails to Reach a Deal**

The United States is on the brink of a partial government shutdown as Congress fails to reach a deal on a spending bill. The House of Representatives voted to approve a plan to fund federal agencies through December 15, but the Senate is expected to reject any changes to the Affordable Care Act (ACA), also known as Obamacare.

**House Republicans' Strategy**

House Speaker John Boehner (R-OH) has a powerful incentive to continue to press his party's fight against the ACA, as conservatives in the House Republican conference are clamoring for a confrontation over it. Boehner does not want to stand down from a fight too soon, and Republicans believe there is some chance that the Senate will agree to repeal the medical device tax, giving the House GOP a victory.

**Senate Democrats' Strategy**

Senate Majority Leader Harry Reid (D-NV) has been the most ardent proponent of President Barack Obama taking a hard line with House Republicans in the latest fiscal crisis. Reid has been getting his way, and the party's leadership, senior White House officials, and the president have been on the same page, refusing to let Republicans gut or delay the ACA. Democrats believe that the burden will be squarely on Boehner to pass a temporary stopgap measure to keep the government funded, or risk the wrath from voters tired of political brinksmanship.

**Economic Impact of a Shutdown**

The Washington region, home to the largest concentration of federal workers and contractors in the nation, could lose an estimated $200 million a day and see more than 700,000 jobs take a financial hit if the federal government shuts down. The region's economy is weak, and a shutdown will have real costs, including cuts in federal services, such as no new applications for benefits like Medicare, Social Security, and child-care subsidies.

**Local Governments Prepare for a Shutdown**

Local governments in the D.C. region have been setting aside pots of money in anticipation of a shutdown, with Montgomery County accounting for a possible $60 million loss in revenue in its current budget. Maryland and Virginia officials are also looking at the possibility of tapping reserve funds to mitigate the effects of a shutdown.

**Timeline**

* Monday night: The deadline for Congress to pass a spending bill to avoid a government shutdown
* Tuesday: The first day of a potential government shutdown
* Wednesday: The Senate is expected to reconvene and quickly move to kill the House's anti-ACA provisions
* Thursday: The House is expected to pass a clean
details.ground_truth: Washington is braced for the first government shutdown in 17 years, which will happen at midnight tonight unless lawmakers manage to break the deadlock over funding and hammer out a deal. Here's what to expect from a day of drama in DC: The House has passed legislation to delay ObamaCare for a year and repeal a tax on medical devices, so the measure will return to the Senate this afternoon, just hours before the shutdown is due to kick in. Harry Reid is expected to table the anti-ObamaCare amendments with a simple Democratic majority and quickly send it back to the House—but if any opportunity arises for Senate Republicans to use delaying tactics, expect Ted Cruz to lead the way, the Wall Street Journal predicts. There's a chance of a straight up-or-down vote in the House, but if it becomes clear that it will be impossible to break the deadlock by midnight—which appears to be the likeliest scenario—the partial shutdown could still be averted if lawmakers pass a short-term funding bill to buy another week or so of debating time. So who will blink first? Neither Reid nor John Boehner appears likely to give way. Reid is determined to play hardball and not give an inch on ObamaCare, Politico reports. Reid and his allies believe that giving any concessions now will only lead to bigger concessions to avoid a debt default next month—and they think the backlash from a government shutdown will hit the GOP hardest. Boehner, meanwhile, will face the wrath of his party's conservatives if he gives way too soon—but polls show the public will blame his House Republicans if there is a shutdown. If there is a shutdown, it won't be an abrupt halt to government, but more "like a spending freeze that will gradually spread through the government like ice forming in water," Quartz explains. Federal employees, 800,000 of whom will be sent home without pay, will be the first affected, and the shutdown will also affect scientific research, trade negotiations, and the federal programs that guarantee home loans and provide capital to business. Air traffic controllers will still be at work, however, and agencies that deal with national security and law enforcement are exempt from the shutdown. A shutdown of a few days might not do too much damage, but experts warn that the national economy will take a serious hit if it is prolonged. In Washington, DC, however, even a brief shutdown will cost the district an estimated $200 million a day—and affect basic services like trash collection. "This is serious," the director of George Mason University’s Center for Regional Analysis tells the Washington Post. "The national economy may not notice a shutdown much unless it lasts three or four weeks. But for the Washington area, this is a tsunami."

**NASA's Parker Solar Probe Mission: Unveiling the Secrets of the Sun**

NASA's Parker Solar Probe, previously known as the Solar Probe Plus, is set to embark on a historic mission to explore the sun's outer atmosphere, known as the corona. The spacecraft will be named in honor of astrophysicist Eugene Parker, who predicted the existence of the solar wind in 1958. Parker, now 89 years old, is the first living person to have a spacecraft named after them.

The Parker Solar Probe will be equipped with a 4.5-inch thick carbon-composite solar shield to protect it from temperatures up to 2,550 degrees Fahrenheit while keeping the science instruments at room temperature. The spacecraft will rely on incredible feats of engineering to allow it to touch the sun, including a thermal protection system and a solar array cooling system.

The mission's objectives include tracing the flow of energy that heats and accelerates the sun's corona and solar wind, determining the structure and dynamics of the plasma and magnetic fields at the sources of the solar wind, and exploring mechanisms that accelerate and transport energetic particles. The findings will help answer questions about how stars work and improve space weather forecasting.

The Parker Solar Probe will make a Venus flyby before reaching its first perihelion or the closest point to the sun. It will repeat that flyby of the perihelion routine 23 more times over its lifetime until 2025. On the final orbits, the spacecraft will fly within 9 solar radii, or nine times the radius of the sun, of the sun's "surface," seven times closer than any other spacecraft has been to the sun before.

The 20-day launch window opens July 31, 2018, and the spacecraft will arrive at NASA's Kennedy Space Center to prep for launch. The mission is a testament to the importance of Parker's body of work and his founding of a new field of science that has inspired many important science questions NASA continues to study and further understand every day.

**The Importance of the Parker Solar Probe Mission**

The Parker Solar Probe mission is crucial in understanding the sun's behavior and its impact on the Earth. Space weather events, caused by the solar wind, can disrupt our planet's magnetic field, causing power outages, communication disruptions, and even radiation exposure on airline flights. The mission's findings will help improve space weather forecasting, allowing us to better prepare for and mitigate the effects of these events.

The mission will also shed light on the mysterious corona,
details.ground_truth: NASA is going to the sun. More specifically, it's launching an unmanned probe next year that will travel closer to the star than any spacecraft has done previously. "It's a spacecraft loaded with technological breakthroughs that will solve many of the largest mysteries about our star," says Nicola Fox of the Johns Hopkins University Applied Physics Laboratory. In less scientific terms, per the Telegraph: "We will finally touch the sun." The nuts and bolts: The mission: The 10-foot probe will launch in July or August of 2018 and eventually get to within 3.7 million miles of the sun, about seven times closer than any previous spacecraft. Eventually, it will be whizzing around the sun at a speed of 450,000 miles per hour, reports CNN. The mission ends in 2025. Corona: The probe will actually fly into the outermost part of the sun's atmosphere, called the corona, per a mission overview at NASA that touts "humanity's first visit to a star." The heat: The probe will rely on a 4.5-inch-thick carbon-composite solar shield to help it withstand temperatures up to 2,550 degrees Fahrenheit, reports Orlando's WKMG. Instruments will remain at room temperature. The name: The probe's name has been changed to the Parker Solar Probe to honor astrophysicist Eugene Parker, who's credited with discovering solar wind. Parker, who turns 90 in June, is a professor emeritus at the University of Chicago, and the Guardian recounts that the theory he put forward in 1958 about a stream of charged particles flowing from the sun was once thought to be "crazy." A first: This is the first time NASA has named a mission after a living scientist. "I'm certainly greatly honored," said the man himself, per Space.com. Two puzzles: Scientists hope to better understand two things in particular: "How is the solar wind accelerated, and why is the ... corona so much hotter than the solar surface?" (It's 3 million degrees Fahrenheit vs. 10,000 degrees Fahrenheit.) Why it matters: Generally, solar storms are relatively harmless when they reach Earth, but these particle bursts occasionally wreak havoc on satellites and here on Earth, and they have the potential to be devastating. "The more we know about how these processes work, the better we can get at predicting when they will happen," writes Loren Grush at the Verge.
