**Jamaica Decriminalizes Possession of Small Amounts of Marijuana, Paving the Way for Medical Cannabis Industry**

In a historic move, Jamaica's parliament has passed a law decriminalizing the possession of small amounts of marijuana, also known as "ganja" or "weed." The new law makes possession of up to 2 ounces (56.6g) of marijuana a petty offense that can result in a fine, rather than a criminal record. This change is the culmination of decades-long efforts to relax Jamaica's strict laws on the drug.

The law also paves the way for the development of a lawful medical cannabis industry in Jamaica. A licensing authority will be established to regulate the cultivation and distribution of marijuana for medical, scientific, and therapeutic purposes. Tourists who are prescribed medical marijuana abroad will be able to apply for permits to buy small amounts of Jamaican weed.

Rastafarians, who have long used marijuana as a sacrament, will also be able to use the drug for religious purposes without fear of persecution. The law is seen as a significant step forward for the Rastafarian community, which has faced victimization and persecution for its practices since colonial times.

Jamaica's national security minister, Peter Bunting, emphasized that the law does not mean that the country will soften its stance on transnational drug trafficking or cultivation of illegal plots. The security forces will continue to rigorously enforce Jamaican law consistent with international treaty obligations.

The move by Jamaican lawmakers is part of an international trend of easing restrictions on marijuana for medical or personal use. More than 20 US states allow some form of medical marijuana, and several countries, including Uruguay and Argentina, have decriminalized the possession of small amounts of the drug.

The development of a medical cannabis industry in Jamaica is seen as a potential economic boon for the country, which is laboring under its latest loan program with the International Monetary Fund. Local scientists have already made a name for themselves in the field of marijuana-derived products, such as "Canasol," which helps relieve pressure in the eyes of glaucoma patients.

While the law is seen as a significant step forward, there is still much work to be done. Delano Seiveright, director of the Cannabis Commercial and Medicinal Taskforce, said that the amendments are a "big step in the right direction, but there's still a lot of work to do."
details.ground_truth: Alaska yesterday became the third state to legalize marijuana for recreational use, and now a country where you would have thought that was the case already is moving closer to that goal itself. Jamaica's parliament last night approved a law decriminalizing small amounts of pot, the AP reports. People found with 2 ounces or less of marijuana will now simply receive a ticket—not a crime on their record—and cultivation of five plants or fewer is now allowed. A licensing agency was also established to oversee pot cultivation and distribution for medical and scientific efforts. Rastafarians are rejoicing, because the bill also grants them the legal right to use cannabis for sacramental purposes, while tourists who have medical marijuana prescriptions elsewhere can pay for permits to buy a bit of ganja on the island. Jamaica has shied away from decriminalization because it didn't want to risk violating international treaties and provoking US sanctions, the Guardian reports. The process for the new law was described by National Security Minister Peter Bunting as an "elephantine," nearly 40-year effort, ABC Australia reports. "[The law] eliminates an unnecessary source of friction between police and citizens, and ensures that our young people are not gratuitously shackled with criminal records," he said in a statement. Jamaica also hopes to boost its health tourism and medical marijuana industries and make it a major player in the pot product market, the Guardian notes. International exporting, however, remains a no-no. A US counternarcotics official told the AP in an email that "Jamaican law is of course Jamaica's own business," but warned that drug trafficking into the US is still illegal.

**AirAsia Flight 8501 Crash: Search Teams Find Wreckage, Investigation into Cause Continues**

On December 28, 2014, AirAsia Flight 8501, an Airbus A320-200, crashed into the Java Sea with 162 people on board, resulting in no survivors. The search and rescue efforts have been ongoing for several days, with multiple countries and organizations involved in the operation.

**Search and Rescue Efforts**

On January 3, 2015, Indonesian officials announced that they were confident that the wreckage of AirAsia Flight 8501 had been located after sonar equipment detected four massive objects on the ocean floor. The objects, measuring up to 18 meters long and 5.4 meters wide, appeared to be part of the jet's body. However, strong currents and big surf have prevented divers from entering the waters to get a visual of the suspected fuselage.

**Investigation into Cause**

The cause of the crash remains unknown, but bad weather appears to have been a factor. A 14-page report released by Indonesia's Meteorology, Climatology and Geophysics Agency stated that the plane was trapped in bad weather that would have been difficult to avoid. The report also suggested that icing, caused by bad weather, may have damaged the Airbus's engines.

**AirAsia's License and Suspension**

Indonesia's transport ministry announced that AirAsia did not have a permit to fly on Sundays, which was the day of the crash. However, Singapore's Civil Aviation Authority stated that from its end, the airline had been approved to fly the route daily. As a result, AirAsia's flights from Surabaya to Singapore have been suspended.

**Humanitarian Efforts**

The search and rescue efforts have also focused on finding the bodies of the victims. So far, 30 bodies have been found floating in the choppy waters. Indonesian authorities have announced that autopsies will be carried out on some of the bodies, including the pilot and co-pilot, whose remains have not yet been recovered.

**Family Members' Reaction**

For family members, the wait has been agonizing, with local media covering every development and theory, many of which have proved to be untrue. Some relatives said they were simply worn out, but they were encouraged by reports that parts of the plane had been detected and hoped that everyone on board would be retrieved.

**International Cooperation**

The search and rescue efforts have involved multiple countries and organizations, including the US, Singapore, Malaysia, and Russia. The hope is
details.ground_truth: Search teams in Indonesia may have found the main fuselage of the AirAsia plane where most of the remaining victims and the plane's black boxes are expected to be. Authorities found "four big parts of the plane we're looking for," says the official in charge of the search in the Java Sea, reports Reuters. The biggest of the pieces is about 59 feet long and 18 feet wide, reports AP. The pieces have only been detected, not recovered, and divers hope conditions will permit them to reach the objects tomorrow. So far, only 30 of 162 bodies have been recovered, and most of the victims are believed to be still strapped in their seats. Authorities also said today that AirAsia was in violation of its license by flying to Singapore on a Sunday, the day of the crash, reports the Telegraph. As a result, the airline might have its license revoked in Indonesia. There's still no conclusions yet in what caused the crash, but theories continue to center on stormy conditions. "Flight 8501 appears to have been trapped in bad weather that would have been difficult to avoid," says a report by Indonesia's Meteorology, Climatology and Geophysics Agency.

**Internet Archive's Effort to Fix Broken Links on the Web**

The Internet Archive, a non-profit organization dedicated to preserving the web's history, has launched an initiative to address the issue of broken links on the web. The project involves creating crawls of supported "No More 404" sites, which will archive pages as they are created and the pages they refer to. This will ensure that even if the referenced pages are changed or removed from the web, a link to the original version will be preserved.

The goal of the Internet Archive's effort is to fix all broken links on the web, making it easier for users to access the content they need. By replacing broken links with references to archived pages, users will be able to see what was originally intended by a page's authors. This will not only improve the user experience but also provide a valuable resource for researchers, historians, and anyone interested in the web's evolution.

The Internet Archive's initiative is a significant step towards making the web a more reliable and accessible place. By preserving the web's history and fixing broken links, the organization is helping to ensure that the internet remains a valuable resource for generations to come.
details.ground_truth: The Telegraph reported yesterday on a crazy court case in the UK: After a pregnant Italian woman, in town for business, had a panic attack, social service workers in Essex got a court order allowing the woman to be forcibly sedated and undergo a C-section so they could take her baby. Fifteen months later, the little girl is still with social service workers, who won't return her to her mother. The case is now "an international legal row," the Telegraph says, and the anonymous woman's lawyers call it "unprecedented." The woman was in Britain in July 2012 for an airline training course, and called police when she suffered the panic attack. They arrived while she was on the phone with her mother, who told police the woman suffered from bipolar disorder and was off her medication, according to a Telegraph columnist. Police took her to a psychiatric facility, and restrained her under the Mental Health Act when she said she wanted to go back to her hotel. She underwent the C-section after having been there five weeks. The case is ongoing; the mother says she has made a full recovery, but a judge nonetheless ruled that her daughter should be put up for adoption. More on the case here and here.

**Habeas Corpus: A Fundamental Right Under Threat**

Habeas corpus, also known as the Great Writ, is a legal procedure that ensures the government cannot hold an individual indefinitely without a valid reason. This fundamental right has been a cornerstone of Western law since the signing of the Magna Carta in 1215 and was enshrined in the US Constitution. However, in recent years, this right has been compromised by the Military Commissions Act (MCA) of 2006, which revoked the right to habeas corpus for individuals detained at Guantanamo Bay and those labeled as "enemy combatants."

**Guantanamo Bay and the Erosion of Habeas Corpus**

The majority of individuals detained at Guantanamo Bay have little or no evidence against them, and many were sold into captivity by warlords for bounties. Despite numerous legal victories affirming the detainees' right to challenge their detention, the Bush administration has prevented them from getting a fair hearing in the courts. The Supreme Court has agreed to hear the detainees' cases for the third time, and it is hoped that they will uphold habeas corpus and hold the administration accountable.

**Nonhuman Rights and the Writ of Habeas Corpus**

In a groundbreaking decision, a New York judge granted a pair of research chimpanzees, Hercules and Leo, the right to have their day in court through a writ of habeas corpus. This is the first time in US history that an animal has been covered by a writ of habeas corpus, and it could force the university to release the primates and sway other judges to do the same with other research animals. The Nonhuman Rights Project, which filed the case, sees this as a major step forward in their goal to secure the right to bodily liberty for cognitively complex animals.

**The Implications of these Decisions**

The erosion of habeas corpus and the granting of a writ to nonhuman animals have significant implications for our society. If we do not defend the right to habeas corpus, we all lose. The compromise of this core legal value and necessary protection against the executive branch has eroded the very foundation of our legal and constitutional framework. The recognition of nonhuman animals as legal persons could also lead to a re-evaluation of our treatment of animals and the environment, and could have far-reaching consequences for our society and the world at large.

**Conclusion**

In conclusion, the decisions on habeas corpus and nonhuman rights are a reminder that our fundamental rights and freedoms are under threat. We
details.ground_truth: The writ of habeas corpus lets prisoners appear in court to make their captors justify why they're being held. Until now in the US, those who've used this legal tactic have been human. But a New York judge yesterday ruled that the writ may be used by two lab chimpanzees at Stony Brook University, Science magazine reports. NY Supreme Court Justice Barbara Jaffe ordered a university rep to address a suit filed by the Nonhuman Rights Project by appearing in court May 6 to argue why the college is holding Hercules and Leo. The group, which says the animals are being detained for biomedical experiments, says its ultimate goal is to have the animals released to the Save the Chimps sanctuary in Fort Pierce, Fla., per the NhRP's website. "This is a big step forward to getting what we are ultimately seeking: the right to bodily liberty for chimpanzees and other cognitively complex animals," Natalie Prosin, the project's executive director, tells Science. The case kicked off in December 2013 with lawsuits that also included two chimps held on private property; each case was struck down, sending the NhRP down the appeals path. Not everyone's convinced the upcoming hearing will mean liberation for the chimps. A Pepperdine University law professor tells Science that the judge likely just wants to hear both sides of the story: "It would be quite surprising if the judge intended to make a momentous substantive finding that chimpanzees are legal persons" without doing so, he notes. Prosin, meanwhile, says her group isn't stopping with the chimps and will also target the release of other animals. (Click to read about what chimps talk to each other about.)

**Deadly Violence on the Las Vegas Strip: Multiple Shootings and a Fiery Crash Leave Three Dead and Several Injured**

A series of violent incidents on the Las Vegas Strip has left the city in a state of shock and concern for visitor safety. In the early hours of Thursday, February 21, 2013, a black Range Rover SUV opened fire on a Maserati, causing it to crash into a taxi cab that burst into flames. The incident resulted in the deaths of three people, including the driver of the Maserati, Kenneth Wayne Cherry Jr., 27, and the taxi cab driver, Michael Boldon. The passenger in the Maserati was also injured and taken to the hospital.

The shooting was reportedly sparked by an altercation at the valet area of the Aria resort, which spilled onto the streets and led to sporadic gunshots being fired from the Range Rover. The SUV, which had tinted windows and paper dealer plates from out of state, fled the scene and was being sought by officials who contacted police authorities in three neighboring states.

This incident was the latest in a series of violent incidents on the Strip since the beginning of the year, including a shooting at a parking garage on February 6 that left two people critically wounded, and a stabbing in an elevator at The Hotel at Mandalay Bay on Saturday.

Las Vegas, which prides itself on being a safe destination for visitors, has faced criticism and concern over the recent surge in violence. However, police officials have assured that they have made arrests or identified suspects in each case, and that the city remains a safe place to visit.

The incident has also highlighted the need for improved security measures on the Strip, with many tourists and locals expressing their shock and concern over the violence. The Professional Drivers Association, a taxi driver organization, has donated $5,000 to the Boldon family and has raised money through memberships and fund-raisers to assist drivers who are victims of crimes and acts of violence.

As the city continues to investigate and respond to the recent surge in violence, it remains to be seen whether the Las Vegas Strip will be able to regain its reputation as a safe and welcoming destination for visitors.
details.ground_truth: Police in three states are still hunting for a Range Rover whose occupants opened fire on another car on the Vegas Strip this morning and set off a deadly chain reaction. Cops don't know most of the details, but they say it started with a dispute of some kind in one of the nearby hotels, then spilled into the street, reports the Las Vegas Sun. When the Range Rover caught up to a Maserati at a stop light, shots were fired from the SUV that sent the Maserati crashing into a taxi, which exploded upon impact. Three people—the Maserati driver, the taxi driver, and a taxi passenger—were killed, and three others were injured, reports the LA Times. Police are hoping to piece together more details by interviewing a passenger in the Maserati, who was injured in the melee. Earlier reports that gunfire came from both vehicles as part of a moving shootout were incorrect. The AP collects this quote from a tourist visiting from Manchester, England: "This doesn't happen where we come from, not on this scale. We get stabbings, and gang violence, but this is like something out of a movie. Like Die Hard or something."

**Teenage Girl Survives Small-Plane Crash in North-Central Washington State**

A 16-year-old girl, Autumn Veatch, has survived a small-plane crash in the rugged North Cascades mountains of north-central Washington state. The plane, a Beech A-35, was traveling from Kalispell, Montana, to Lynden, Washington, when it disappeared from radar on Saturday afternoon. Family members alerted authorities after the plane failed to arrive at its destination.

**Rescue Efforts and Autumn's Survival**

Rescuers narrowed down the search area based on cellphone data and typical flight patterns. Autumn Veatch, who was on board the plane with her two step-grandparents, Leland and Sharon Bowman, of Marion, Montana, emerged from the wilderness after hiking for a couple of days. A motorist picked her up and drove her 30 miles east to a general store in Mazama, where employees called 911. Autumn was taken to a hospital in Brewster for treatment of minor injuries, including dehydration and exposure to the elements.

**Condition of the Other Two Passengers Unknown**

The fate of Autumn's two step-grandparents remains unclear. Autumn's father, David Veatch, said his daughter told him the plane crashed and caught fire after flying into a bank of clouds. She remained at the crash site for a day before deciding to hike down, eventually finding a trail and following it to the trailhead on Highway 20.

**Search Efforts Continue**

Five aircraft equipped with special radios for detecting the missing plane's emergency-locator transmitter searched the mountains on Monday, while ground crews focused on areas between Mazama and Rainy Pass. The planes halted their search for the night by 8 p.m., while a couple of Navy helicopters from the Whidbey Island Naval Air Station continued to sweep the area.

**Autumn's Family and Friends React**

Autumn's family and friends were overjoyed at the news of her survival. "We were refusing to accept" that she was gone, said Santina Lampman, a longtime family friend. Autumn's father, David Veatch, said his daughter is an amazing, witty, creative, and loving human being who loves art, music, and hiking.
details.ground_truth: A 16-year-old girl in Washington state is bruised and dehydrated but very much alive after surviving a plane crash and a long trek through what authorities describe as some of the state's most rugged terrain. Autumn Veatch was on a small plane with her step-grandparents that crashed after departing Kalispell, Mont., on Saturday afternoon, the Bellingham Herald reports. There was no sign of the plane or its occupants until a motorist found Veatch on State Route 20 yesterday afternoon and took her to a general store, where employees called authorities, the AP reports. She was hospitalized with minor injuries. Her father tells the Herald that after the plane crashed into a mountain, it caught fire and she was unable to get her step-grandparents out. She spent about a day near the plane waiting for rescue before following a stream and then a trail out of the woods, her father says. Friends say both father and daughter are completely overwhelmed. "She did joke that it was a good thing she'd watched all those Survivor shows that she didn't like, but her dad made her watch anyway," a family friend tells the Seattle Times. Authorities are still searching for the missing Beech A-35. (In Colombia last month, a mother and baby survived four days in the jungle after a plane crash.)

**Summary of Recent News Headlines**

**Gibson Guitar Raids and Environmental Regulation**

In a rare collision of music and environmental regulation, the Gibson Guitar Corporation in Tennessee was raided by federal marshals last week. The government appears to be preparing to charge the company with trafficking in illegally obtained wood, specifically ebony from Madagascar. The Lacey Act, a century-old endangered species law, was amended in 2008 to include plants as well as animals and is being used to enforce the seizure of the wood. Gibson's CEO, Henry Juszkiewicz, claims that the government won't tell him exactly how or if his company has violated the law, and that the business has been injured to millions of dollars.

The Environmental Investigation Agency (EIA) has concluded that Gibson knowingly imported tainted wood, and Andrea Johnson, director of forest programs for the EIA, says that the Lacey Act requires end users of endangered wood to certify the legality of their supply chain all the way to the trees. Gibson denies these allegations, maintaining that all of its purchases from Madagascar have complied with U.S. and Malagasy law.

**The Impact of the Lacey Act on the Guitar Industry**

The Lacey Act's retroactive nature has caused concern among guitar builders, who are worried they might be forced to prove the provenance of wood they acquired decades ago. Attorney Ronald Bienstock has warned clients to be wary of traveling abroad with old guitars, as the law says owners can be asked to account for every wooden part of their guitars when re-entering the U.S. The law also covers the trade in vintage instruments.

Nashville's George Gruhn, one of the world's top dealers of old guitars, banjos, and other rare stringed instruments, has acknowledged that the government has tried to create exemptions to cover vintage instruments, but says they are rife with delays and has nearly eliminated the 40% of his business that used to deal with overseas buyers.

**The Wayback Machine and Internet Archive**

In a separate news story, Alexa Internet has been donating its crawl data to the Internet Archive since 1996, with these data being added to the Wayback Machine after an embargo period. This has provided a valuable resource for researchers and the public, allowing them to access and explore the internet's past.

Overall, these news stories highlight the importance of environmental regulation and the impact it can have on various industries, including the guitar industry. They also showcase the value of preserving and accessing the internet's past through the Wayback Machine and Internet Archive
details.ground_truth: Allegations of illegal wood imports prompted the Justice Department to raid Gibson Guitar facilities in Tennessee last week. NPR aired the complicated story involving the 100-year old Lacey Act, which prohibits imports of endangered species, including plants, into the US. No formal charges have been pressed, but it appears the issue lies with Gibson possibly importing banned Madagascar ebony. Gibson insists the wood confiscated by marshals was legally acquired rosewood from India. Adding some gusto to the story, right-leaning sites assert that Gibson rival CF Martin uses the same wood in some of its guitars, but the company was never investigated. The Landmark Report thinks that's fishy, given that Martin's CEO is a Democratic donor and Gibson's CEO is a GOP backer. Raising the octave even higher, it is possible Michelle Obama ran afoul of the Lacey Act when she presented France's Carla Bruni with a Gibson guitar that may have contained banned wood. However the story plays out, many are frustrated with the retroactive aspect of the law. "It's a nightmare," says a dealer. "I can't help it if they used Brazilian rosewood on almost every guitar made prior to 1970."

**Middle East Conflict Escalates as Israel and Hamas Trade Rocket Fire**

A devastating conflict between Israel and Hamas has escalated in recent days, with both sides trading rocket fire and resulting in a significant number of casualties. The conflict, which began on Wednesday, has seen Israel launch a series of airstrikes and artillery attacks on the Gaza Strip, killing at least 30 Palestinians, including 13 militants and 16 civilians, among them eight children and a pregnant woman.

In response, Hamas has fired a rocket toward Jerusalem for the first time in decades, and Tel Aviv, Israel's commercial center, has come under rocket attack for the second straight day. The Israeli cabinet has authorized the mobilization of up to 75,000 reservists, preparing the ground for a possible Gaza invasion.

Egypt's Prime Minister Hisham Kandil visited Gaza and said Cairo was prepared to mediate a truce, but a three-hour truce that Israel declared for the duration of Kandil's visit never took hold. The Israeli military said 97 rockets fired from Gaza hit Israel on Friday and 99 more were intercepted by its Iron Dome anti-missile system.

The conflict has stoked the flames of a Middle East already ablaze with two years of Arab revolution and a civil war in Syria that threatens to leap across borders. It is the biggest test yet for Egypt's new President Mohamed Mursi, a veteran Islamist politician from the Muslim Brotherhood who was elected this year after last year's protests ousted military autocrat Hosni Mubarak.

**International Response and Diplomatic Efforts**

The international community has condemned the violence, with the 120-nation Nonaligned Movement calling for "decisive action by the U.N. Security Council." The United States has asked countries that have contact with Hamas to urge the Islamist movement to stop its rocket attacks.

Egypt's President Mohammed Morsi has vowed to "not leave Gaza alone" and has ordered his prime minister to lead a delegation to Gaza. The United Nations Secretary General Ban Ki-moon has planned to visit Jerusalem, Cairo, and Ramallah, the West Bank headquarters of the Palestinian Authority, in coming days.

**Humanitarian Crisis and Casualties**

The conflict has resulted in a significant humanitarian crisis, with at least 101 children and 96 women injured in Israeli strikes, according to the Palestinian health minister. A 4-year-old boy, Mahmoud Sadalah, was among the eight children killed in the recent violence, and a 2-year-old boy was killed in the southern town of Khan Yun
details.ground_truth: A brief, planned ceasefire failed to materialize in Gaza today, as death continued to rain down on the strip. Israel had offered to hold its offensive to accommodate a visit from Egyptian Prime Minister Hesham Kandil, provided Hamas held its fire as well. But Hamas said it would keep firing, and one Israeli defense minister complained that 50 rockets had flown in from Gaza during Kandil's visit, CNN reports. A Hamas-run TV station reported that Israel had kept firing as well, but Israel says it took a break for at least two hours. In Palestine the death toll has risen to 21, including 13 civilians, Reuters reports. Among those civilians were seven children and a pregnant teenager. Israel's death toll held steady at 3, despite the hundreds of rockets fired into Israel. But Hamas did up the ante by firing rockets near Tel Aviv for the first time. Air-raid sirens sounded (the last time they did so was during the Gulf War) and civilians ran for cover, though the missiles exploded harmlessly, with one going into the sea. "There will be a price for that escalation," Ehud Barak vowed, according to the New York Times. Israel has now hit 250 Gaza targets, while taking further steps toward a ground invasion, calling up 16,000 reservists, the Wall Street Journal reports. It's also steadily sending troops and armored vehicles to the border, notes CNN—between 1,500 and 2,000 are already there, one official said. Israel claims the attacks have weakened Hamas' military capabilities and caused its attacks to lighten, but Hamas denied that, saying it had hit multiple Israeli targets today. Kandil emerged from his meeting with Hamas expressing solidarity, saying the new Egyptian regime would be more active in helping the Palestinians. "The time has changed," he said. "No longer the Israeli occupation will be able to carry out their attacks against the Palestinians without being held responsible. That time is far bygone." For its part, the Obama administration says it's seeking help from Arab countries in calling on Hamas to relent. "The onus rests squarely on Hamas ... to stop its rocket attacks," says a State Department spokesman. UN Secretary-General Ban Ki-Moon is planning diplomatic visits to Jerusalem, Ramallah, and Cairo within days.

**Maine Woman Fights Off Rabid Raccoon with Her Bare Hands**

A 21-year-old woman from Hope, Maine, has made a daring and desperate move to save her life after being attacked by a rabid raccoon while jogging on a familiar trail near her home. Rachel Borch, who has received six rabies shots and is slated to receive her last injection this weekend, recounted her terrifying experience in an interview.

According to Borch, she was running on a fire road next to her house when she encountered the animal, which was acting strangely and charging at her with its teeth bared. She started dancing around the animal, frantically trying to figure out what to do, but knew it was going to bite her. She put her hands out in front of her, and the raccoon latched onto her thumb.

In a split second, Borch decided to use her hands to defend herself and dragged the raccoon into a nearby puddle, where she held its head underwater until it stopped struggling. The state's Center for Disease Control and Prevention confirmed that the raccoon tested positive for rabies.

This is not the only incident of its kind in Maine this season. Earlier this week, a Wiscasset man was bitten on both hands by what was believed to be a rabid fox in Topsham. As of June 7, there have been 20 animals, including raccoons, red foxes, and skunks, that have tested positive for rabies in 2017.

In 2016, 64 animals in Maine tested positive for rabies, according to CDC data. Hope Animal Control Officer Heidi Blood warned that when there's one infected animal, there's typically another, and that infected animals can start showing signs within two weeks, while humans can exhibit symptoms within a few weeks or months.

Borch's advice for others who find themselves facing a rabid animal? She has none. However, she will never look at raccoons the same way again, having realized that they can be ferocious and deadly.

**Rabies in Maine: A Growing Concern**

Rabies is a virus that affects the brain and spinal cord and can cause death if left untreated. It is rare in people in the United States, but common in animals, especially wildlife, in most parts of the country, including Maine.

According to Heidi Blood, if one animal tests positive for rabies, it's almost 100 percent certain there are other animals in the area also infected. She warned that people and
details.ground_truth: Rachel Borch was out for a jog in the woods near her home in Hope, Maine, when a "ferocious-looking" attacker with beady eyes and tiny teeth made a beeline for her, reports the Camden Herald. Borch, 21, knew immediately that something was wrong with the raccoon that was charging toward her. "Imagine the Tasmanian devil," she tells the Bangor Daily News of the "terrifying" June 3 incident. Yanking out her headphones, she began "dancing" around the animal on the narrow path, but she tells the Herald she knew that, one way or another, the animal was going to bite her. She figured her hands would be the best spot, so she offered them up. The raccoon chomped down on her thumb and stayed there, scratching at her legs and arms as she screamed. Now on her knees, she spotted her phone lying submerged in a mud puddle, and had an idea: drown it. "With my thumb in its mouth, I just pushed its head down into the muck," she tells the News. When the raccoon finally stopped moving, she yanked out her finger and raced the three-quarters of a mile home. Two days later, the state confirmed the animal was infected with rabies. Borch got rabies shots, and an animal control officer adds an unpleasant footnote: The infected raccoon may not be alone. "Not to scare people," Heidi Blood tells the News, but "when there’s one, there’s typically another." (Normally nocturnal like raccoons, a crazed beaver attacked a woman paddle-boarder.)

**Ancient Mars: A Warm and Wet World with Potential for Life**

Recent studies have shed new light on the history of Mars, revealing that the planet was once a warm and wet world with a massive ocean covering nearly half of its northern hemisphere. NASA scientists have used infrared maps to analyze the Martian atmosphere and determine that the planet lost a vast amount of water in the past, equivalent to more than six times that now locked up in the planet's frozen ice caps.

The research, published in the journal Science, suggests that the ancient ocean was at least a mile deep in some areas and held 20 million cubic kilometers of water, more than the Arctic Ocean. The scientists believe that the water pooled into a much deeper ocean in the low-lying northern plains, creating an ocean that covered nearly a fifth of the planet's surface.

The findings have significant implications for the search for life on Mars. The longer water persists on a planetary body in one location, particularly if there is geological turnover, the more likely it is that it would provide a habitable environment for life to either originate or proliferate. An ocean would meet this need, and the researchers believe that the ancient Martian ocean would have been a suitable environment for life to evolve.

The study also reveals that the ordinary hydrogen in the Martian water has been gradually leaking into outer space, resulting in a higher proportion of deuterium in the planet's water than on Earth. By calculating how much water the planet has lost over time, the researchers estimate that an ocean at least a mile deep covered a significant fraction of the Martian surface four billion years ago.

The evidence continues to mount that Mars was once far wetter and warmer than it is today, with increasing signs that the ancient chemical environment was hospitable to biology. The search for life on Mars will ramp up in 2018 with the European Space Agency's Exomars rover, which will look for chemical signatures of life, perhaps emanating from microbes living deep beneath the Martian soil.

**Key Findings:**

* A massive ancient ocean once covered nearly half of the northern hemisphere of Mars.
* The ocean was at least a mile deep in some areas and held 20 million cubic kilometers of water.
* The ordinary hydrogen in the Martian water has been gradually leaking into outer space, resulting in a higher proportion of deuterium.
* The researchers estimate that an ocean at least a mile deep covered a significant fraction of the Martian surface four billion years ago.
* The ancient Martian ocean would have been a suitable environment for life to evolve.

**
details.ground_truth: Imagine all the water in the Arctic Ocean. Now, imagine all that and more on Mars. If you visited the Red Planet roughly 4.3 billion years ago, that's what you likely would have found, say NASA scientists. Their new study arrived at that "solid estimate ... by determining how much water was lost to space," says lead author Geronimo Villanueva. The Guardian reports the scientists used three powerful infrared telescopes to analyze two forms of water in Mars' atmosphere: H2O and HDO, in which deuterium (aka "heavy" hydrogen) has taken the place of one hydrogen atom. As National Geographic explains, Mars' gravity is weaker than our own; that allowed hydrogen to escape from the atmosphere and into space over time, boosting the amount of deuterium in its water. Scientists compared the ratio of HDO to H2O in Martian water today with that in water from a 4.5 billion-year-old Mars meteorite to arrive at their conclusions, a press release states. They ultimately calculated that Mars was once home to 20 million cubic kilometers of ocean. It could have covered every inch of the planet in water 450 feet deep, but the scientists think a different scenario is more likely: that the ocean covered roughly a fifth of the planet, was located in the northern hemisphere, and was as much as a mile deep. While National Geographic observes the findings back up "reams of earlier evidence that water once existed on the surface" of Mars, another study author points out the assessment of the degree of water lost indicates something new. "The planet was very likely wet for a longer period of time than was previously thought, suggesting it might have been habitable for longer," a scientist says. (Mysterious plumes on Mars are stumping scientists.)

**Rescue Mission: Swedish University Saves Student and Family from ISIS**

In a remarkable and unprecedented event, Lund University in Sweden, in collaboration with its security chief and a private security company, successfully rescued a graduate student, Firas Jumaah, and his family from the clutches of the Islamic State (ISIS) in Iraq in 2014.

Jumaah, a member of the Yazidi minority, had initially stayed behind in Iraq to complete his lab work at the university. However, when his family, including his wife and two small children, returned to visit their home country, they found themselves facing a life-and-death situation as ISIS conducted a deadly offensive in northern Iraq.

Jumaah's family had been forced to flee their home and were hiding in a disused bleach factory, with the sounds of gunshots and ISIS warriors roaming the town. In a desperate bid to save his family, Jumaah sent a text message to his supervisor, Charlotta Turner, asking her to remove him from the doctoral program if he wasn't back in Sweden within a week.

Turner, a professor in Analytical Chemistry, was not willing to leave her student to die without trying to do something. She contacted the university's security chief, Per Gustafson, who had a transport and security deal that stretched over the whole world. Over a few days of intense activity, Gustafson hired a security company, which arranged the rescue operation.

Two Landcruisers carrying four heavily-armed mercenaries roared into the area where Jumaah was hiding, and sped him away to Erbil Airport together with his wife and two small children. Jumaah described the experience as feeling both privileged and like a coward, as he left his mother and sisters behind.

The family has almost finished paying the university back for the rescue operation, and Jumaah has completed his PhD and now works for a pharmaceuticals company in Malmö. Gustafson described the event as unique and unprecedented, and Turner said that she was angry and outraged by the situation, but felt a sense of responsibility to help her student.

This remarkable story highlights the dedication and commitment of educators and institutions to their students, and the importance of standing up against injustice and oppression.
details.ground_truth: Four years ago, a chemistry professor got a text from her grad student: If I'm not back in a week, cut me from the doctoral program. Charlotta Turner called him right away: "He was very sad and crying,” the 48-year-old prof at Lund University in Sweden tells NBC News. "I could hear that the situation was hopeless and they had to flee." The student, Firas Jumaah, was visiting his native Iraq to help family members during a brutal 2014 ISIS attack targeting Yazidis—a religious minority that includes his family. The terror group had just enslaved and massacred Yazidis by the thousand in nearby Sinjar. Now Jumaah and family were planning to flee to the mountains. "I had no hope at all," says Jumaah, per the Local. "I was desperate." But Turner took action. She spoke to Lund University's then-security chief, who contacted a company that sent mercenaries into northern Iraq. Only days later, four armed mercs on two Landcruisers blazed into the place where Jumaah was hiding, and rushed him to Erbil Airport with his wife and two young kids. "I have never felt so privileged, so VIP," he says. "But at the same time I felt like a coward as I left my mother and sisters behind me." Seeing his colleagues back in Sweden, he was speechless: "I just cried," he says. Yet Jumaah finished his PhD and found work at a Malmo pharmaceuticals company, and his family survived. The bill: roughly 60,000 kroner ($6,613), which his family has nearly finished paying. “If they told me to pay 200,000 kronor, I would,” says Jumaah. (The UN is finding fresh ISIS horrors.)

Pippa Middleton, the younger sister of the Duchess of Cambridge Kate Middleton, has found herself at the center of a controversy after a photograph was taken of her laughing and smiling as a male companion in a car pointed a gun at a photographer following them in Paris. 

The incident occurred on Saturday when Pippa, 28, was driven to the Gare du Nord to catch the Eurostar home in a black Audi A5 convertible with red leather seats. The driver of the car, who has yet to be identified, pulled out a realistic-looking weapon from the glove box and aimed it directly at the photographer.

It is not clear whether the gun was real or a fake, but the French authorities are investigating the incident after receiving a complaint from a member of the public. If the gun was real, those involved in the incident face up to seven years in prison. If it is a fake, they could be jailed for two years.

Pippa was in Paris to attend a costume birthday party at the Chez Raspoutine as a guest of fashion label owner Arthur de Soultrait, also a passenger in the car when the gun incident occurred. The party was a decadent 18th century-themed bash that saw costume-clad socialites greeted by three dwarves lounging on a sofa for their 'entertainment'. The highlight of the party was a risqué burlesque show which culminated in a stripper bursting out of a birthday cake and showering the audience in sparks.

Arthur de Soultrait, a self-confessed party animal and one of the most successful young entrepreneurs in France, runs the upmarket Vicomte A clothing range, which had a turnover of around £10million in 2010. He is a fanatically keen huntsman whose passion for bloodsports is matched only by his love of the high life. He has been photographed with the Queen and plays polo to a high standard.

A spokesperson for Arthur de Soultrait's company said that the gun was a fake and that it was just a stupid joke. However, the incident has caused anger in London in royal circles that Pippa has been dragged into this when she was just a passenger in the car. At least one witness has made a formal complaint to police, and the judicial police are conducting a preliminary investigation before deciding whether to alert an examining magistrate.

The Palace has refused to comment on the incident, saying that Pippa is a private individual and not a member of the royal family. However, some have argued that the Palace should bring Pipp
details.ground_truth: A "gun joke" involving palace sis-in-law Pippa Middleton is causing a royal stir. The driver of a car that Pippa, 28, was riding in smilingly pointed what looked like a gun at pestering paparazzi in Paris over the weekend. Now he says he was just goofing around, and the gun wasn't real. "It was not a real gun. It was just a stupid joke," said a representative. The French aren't laughing, though, especially in the wake of the shocking shootings in Toulouse last month. Paris authorities warned that everyone in the car (including grinning Pippa) could face arrest and even two years in prison. "Anybody involved in the illegal use of a handgun in public is liable to arrest and interrogation," a source told the Sun. But so far, no one has filed a complaint, and the "targeted" photographer didn't feel threatened, reports ABC. The driver hasn't yet been identified. Her Royal Hotness was in the front passenger's seat, and sitting in the back were two men, including aristocrat fashion designer Viscount Arthur de Soultrait, whose wild birthday party Pippa attended (for photos, check here). Buckingham Palace reps are keeping mum, saying Pippa is a "private individual" and not their concern.

**Jewelry Store Robberies Across the Southeast: FBI Seeks Suspects**

A string of jewelry store robberies has taken place across the Southeast, with the FBI now seeking two suspects believed to be involved in the crimes. The suspects, a white female in her 20s or 30s, 5-foot-8 and 130 pounds, and a black male in his 30s or 40s, 6 feet and 250 pounds, are considered armed and dangerous.

According to authorities, the female suspect has robbed jewelry stores in Florida, Georgia, South Carolina, Tennessee, and North Carolina since April, with the most recent heist taking place on Monday at a Jared Vault in Mebane, North Carolina. In each of the robberies, the suspect has shown a handgun and ordered employees to a back room, where they were zip-tied and left unharmed.

The FBI has released several photographs of the female suspect and a man believed to be working with her, who was caught on surveillance cameras in three of the robberies. The suspect is believed to have stolen more than $450,000 worth of jewelry in the six total robberies, with the largest heist taking place in October at a Reeds Jewelers in Panama City Beach, Florida, where she stole over $400,000 worth of jewels.

The suspect's methods are similar in each of the robberies, with her targeting chain stores at large retail shopping and entertainment plazas. Authorities are investigating similar robberies of jewelry stores in Florida, South Carolina, and two in Georgia.

If you have any information about the suspects or their whereabouts, you are asked to call the Mebane police at 919-563-9031 or Alamance County Crime Stoppers at 336-229-7100.

**Key Details:**

* Suspects: White female female in her 20s or 30s, 5-foot-8 and 130 pounds, and black male male in his 30s or 40s, 6 feet and 250 pounds
* Robberies: Florida, Georgia, South Carolina, Tennessee, and North Carolina since April
* Most recent heist: Monday at a Jared Vault in Mebane, North Carolina
* Stolen goods: More than $450,000 worth of jewelry

details.ground_truth: Will Smith stars as Nicky, an established con man who takes on a new apprentice, Australian newcomer Margot Robbie's Jess, in Focus. They both love the con game, but who's playing who? Here's what critics are saying: Richard Corliss at Time is a fan. "The mix of longtime star and minx on the rise is one tasty element in the success of a movie that approaches the modest goals and effortless allure of a 60-year-old Hitchcock," he writes. Smith "takes a welcome break from glowering sci-fi roles" and writer-directors Glenn Ficarra and John Requa "deserve some credit in letting Will be Will in the star's first charm barrage since 2005's Hitch." Steven Rea at the Philadelphia Inquirer admits Smith "shows some of the movie-star wattage that's been missing from his recent pics. He charms." There's also "combustible chemistry" between Smith and Robbie, but the movie just "disappoints," Rea writes. "Even if you're willing to forgive its sinkhole plotholes and farthest-fetched conceits, the film ... ultimately makes no sense." Betsy Sharkey, on the other hand, says this "rom-com-con" is "an irresistible reminder of all the reasons we first fell for the Fresh Prince so many years ago." Writing at the Los Angeles Times, she notes the romantic role of Nicky fits Smith perfectly, while Robbie is "more than holding her own" as his match. "The scams are Rubik's Cube complicated, but what keeps you guessing is whether the romantic connection between Jess and Nicky is real or just another con." But Mick LaSalle at the San Francisco Chronicle isn't convinced. In a review titled "Will Smith just stole $12 from your pocket," he argues Focus is "ridiculous in every detail. It's a movie with no truth that teaches nothing and shows nothing, that has only its audacity to recommend it." Nicky and Jess lack "impact," partly because "Smith is not a strong leading man," he says. Viewers are left "waiting for the trick, which is not the same as being fooled."

**Mega Millions Jackpot Reaches Record-Breaking Heights**

The Mega Millions lottery jackpot has reached a staggering $636 million, making it the largest U.S. lottery prize in history. Two lucky winners have matched all the winning numbers: 8, 20, 14, 17, and 39, with a Megaball of 7. If they choose to take the lump-sum cash option, the prize would be $341.2 million.

**What to Do if You're a Winner**

If you're one of the lucky winners, here are some essential steps to take:

1. **Sign the ticket**:** Make sure to sign the ticket as soon as possible to prevent anyone else from claiming it.
2. **Take a selfie**:** Capture a photo of yourself with the winning ticket as proof of ownership.
3. **Find a safe place to store the ticket**:** Consider using a safe deposit box or a portable home safe to keep the ticket secure.
4. **Contact experienced professionals**:** Reach out to an attorney and a certified financial planner to help you make informed decisions about your winnings.
5. **Plan a trip**:** Consider taking a break from the public eye and traveling to a secluded location, such as the U.S. Virgin Islands or South America.

**Ticket Buying Frenzy**

The Mega Millions jackpot has sparked a last-minute ticket buying frenzy, with sales expected to surge before the 11 p.m. EST drawing on Tuesday. According to Virginia's lottery director, Paula Otto, up to 70% of tickets are typically bought on the day of the drawing, and this year's sales are expected to be even higher.

**Odds of Winning**

The odds of winning the Mega Millions jackpot are 1 in 259 million, but with the record-breaking jackpot, players have a better chance of winning. By Tuesday's drawing, players will have bought enough tickets to cover 65-75% of the possible number combinations, increasing their chances of striking it rich.

**What's Next**

If no one wins the jackpot on Tuesday, it will rise to at least $950 million for the next drawing on Friday. The Mega Millions jackpot has become a national phenomenon, with many people eagerly awaiting the results of the drawing.

**European Court of Justice Rules Against Nestlé in KitKat Trademark Dispute**

In a long-running and highly publicized case, the European Court of Justice (ECJ) has ruled against Nestlé, the global chocolate giant, in its bid to trademark the shape of its KitKat finger wafers. The decision, which was made on Wednesday, sends the case back to the EU's trademark office, where it is likely that the trademark will be revoked.

The case began in 2002, when Nestlé applied for a trademark in Europe for the KitKat shape, which consists of four trapezoidal bars aligned on a rectangular base. The application was granted in 2006, but Mondelez, the parent company of Cadbury, challenged the decision, arguing that the shape was not distinctive enough to be trademarked.

The ECJ's ruling is a significant blow to Nestlé, which has spent over a decade and millions of dollars fighting to protect its trademark. The court found that Nestlé had failed to show that consumers in enough EU countries recognized the shape as distinctive, and also dismissed an appeal by Mondelez against some of the grounds for a lower EU court ruling in 2016.

The decision has implications for brands operating across the EU single market, where there are varying histories in national markets. It also features in Britain's Brexit debate, with some supporters of cutting ties with the EU suggesting that EU courts have failed to defend a much-loved British treat first marketed by Rowntree's.

**Other Trademark Disputes in the Chocolate Industry**

The KitKat trademark dispute is not the only one in the chocolate industry. Other notable cases include:

* Lindt's trademark dispute over its gold-wrapped Easter bunnies, which were challenged by a German company called Riegelein. Lindt eventually lost the case.
* Toblerone's trademark dispute over its shape, which was registered in 1998. The company's shape was challenged by a British retailer called Poundland, which designed an off-brand version of the chocolate bar. The disagreement was settled out of court.
* Cadbury's trademark dispute over its purple color, which was challenged by Nestlé. The case was heard in the British courts and resulted in a 16,000-word ruling that Kit Kat had "no inherent distinctiveness".

**What's Next for the KitKat Trademark?**

details.ground_truth: "Four trapezoidal bars aligned on a rectangular base" may not be instantly recognizable. Put that setup in chocolate form, however, and many people would be able to distinguish it as a KitKat. Even if that's the case, the European Union's highest court just ruled that the shape of the sweet treat isn't unique enough to merit trademark protection, meaning other confectioners can potentially produce KitKat knockoffs, the Guardian reports. Nestle, which makes KitKats, has been fighting Cadbury (and then Mondelez, which ultimately bought Cadbury) in the courts for more than a decade on this issue. The BBC notes that Nestle first applied for trademark protection for the candy's shape in 2002, and it was granted that protection four years later. Cadbury's pushback began in 2007, and Wednesday's decision by the European Court of Justice essentially tells the EU trademark office it now has to "reconsider" its original decision. What that means, however, is that if the EU Intellectual Property Office finds, based on more recent evidence, that the KitKat bar's shape is recognizable in enough countries, it can retain the trademark protection, per Reuters. The brand name itself of "KitKat" is not being contested. (Atari accused Nestle of ripping off one of its popular video games in an ad for KitKat.)

**The Roar of the T. Rex: Uncovering the Sounds of the Ancient Dinosaurs**

For decades, the Tyrannosaurus Rex has been depicted as a ferocious, roaring creature in movies and popular culture. However, new research suggests that this iconic sound may be nothing more than a product of Hollywood imagination. Scientists have discovered that dinosaurs, including the mighty T. Rex, may have made sounds that are more similar to the cooing of a pigeon or the mumble of an ostrich.

**The Origins of Dino Sounds**

According to a study published in the journal Evolution, closed-mouth vocalizations, which are sounds made through the skin in the neck area while the beak is kept closed, are a common trait in birds and have evolved independently at least 16 times. This behavior is often associated with large-bodied lineages, and researchers believe that it may have been present in some extinct nonavian dinosaurs.

**The Sounds of the Ancient Dinosaurs**

While we may never know for certain what dinosaurs sounded like, researchers are using a statistical approach to analyze the distribution of closed-mouth vocalization among birds and other reptilian groups. They have identified 52 out of 208 investigated bird species that use this behavior, and believe that it may have been used by dinosaurs during mating displays or to defend their territory.

**The Inspiration of Jurassic Park**

In a fascinating look at the making of the iconic movie Jurassic Park, sound designer Gary Rydstrom reveals that he used a variety of animal sounds to create the dinosaurs, including a tortoise mating, a horse, a goose, and even a Jack Russell terrier. He also used his own voice to create the sound of a sick triceratops, and a whale blowhole and a fire hydrant to create its sneezing sound.

**A New World of Dinosaur Sounds**

The study on closed-mouth vocalizations in birds offers a glimpse into a new world of dinosaur sounds, where booming, closed-mouth sounds may have been used by these ancient creatures. As researchers continue to study the evolution of bird vocalizations, they may uncover more clues about the sounds of the ancient dinosaurs, and shed new light on our understanding of these fascinating creatures.

In conclusion, the sounds of the ancient dinosaurs are a mystery that may never be fully solved, but new research and innovative approaches are offering a glimpse into a new world of dino sounds, where the roar of the T. Rex may be just a myth of the past.
details.ground_truth: Dinosaurs may have been much more like modern birds than we knew—and not just because some had feathers. A new study suggests that mighty dinosaurs of yore didn't roar, contrary to every dinosaur movie you've ever seen. Instead, they made a decidedly less scary sound called a "closed-mouth vocalization." Think of a dove cooing or perhaps an ostrich doing this grunting thing. As the scientists explain in the journal Evolution, per UT News, the sound actually comes out of the neck area after air is pushed through an esophageal pouch. In fact, the animal's mouth is shut the whole time. Lots of birds do this today, and because birds descended from dinosaurs, scientists suspect they may have communicated in a similar way. The fossil record can't prove it, though the Washington Post notes that previous research meshes with the idea, including the belief that dinosaurs had air sacs instead of vocal chords. “A cool thing about this work is the demonstration that closed-mouth behavior evolved many times,” says the lead author. “That suggests it can emerge fairly easily and be incorporated into mating displays" among different types of animals. A co-author says the study gives the impression of "a very different Jurassic world. Not only were dinosaurs feathered, but they may have had bulging necks and made booming, closed-mouth sounds." But if so, the makers of Jurassic Park didn't get it all wrong: The hiss of the film's velociraptors came from a goose, per Vulture. (Scientists have grown chickens with dinosaur legs.)

Heath was booked on a $1 million bond and is currently being held at the Fort Worth jail. The police investigation into the double murder, which occurred on the 800 block of Buffalo Springs Drive, is ongoing.

Heath served in the Air Force for 13 years before recently becoming a teacher.

**Vanderbilt University Renames Confederate Memorial Hall, Universities Across the Country Grapple with Racist Symbols and History**

In a significant move, Vanderbilt University has announced that it will pay $1.2 million to remove the word "Confederate" from its Confederate Memorial Hall, a building that has been a source of controversy and debate on campus for years. The decision comes as universities across the country are grappling with the legacy of racism and slavery on their campuses, and the presence of Confederate symbols and monuments.

The building, which was constructed with the help of a $50,000 gift from the United Daughters of the Confederacy in 1933, has been referred to as Memorial Hall since 2002. However, the university was blocked in court from changing the name chiseled on the building because of the gift. Under the new agreement, Vanderbilt will pay the equivalent of the gift made 83 years ago to the organization's Tennessee chapter, and in exchange, the chapter will relinquish its naming rights to the building.

Vanderbilt Chancellor Nicholas S. Zeppos said that the decision to remove the mention of the Confederacy from the building speaks to the courage of students, faculty, and staff who spoke out against it for years. "We are not saying this is not part of Vanderbilt's history," he said. "I think we teach history by how we talk about these events. I don't think that that (name) was really teaching anyone history."

The move is part of a larger effort by Vanderbilt to address diversity and inclusion on campus, including the addition of a chief diversity officer to the university's leadership team. The university has also faced criticism from some who argue that changing the name of the building is a form of rewriting history.

In other news, Middle Tennessee State University is also seeking to remove the name of Confederate Gen. Nathan Bedford Forrest from one of its buildings. The public higher education system overseeing the school endorsed the change last month, but the proposal faces a tougher hurdle when it reaches the Tennessee Historical Commission.

The calls to remove Confederate imagery from public places have multiplied across the South since the slaying of nine black churchgoers in Charleston, South Carolina, last year. Republican Gov. Bill Haslam has said he supported removing the Capitol bust of Forrest, who is famous for his exploits as a Confederate cavalry general and amassed a fortune as a plantation owner and slave trader in Memphis before the Civil War.

The Southern Baptist Convention has also voted to urge its congregations to stop displaying Confederate flags, a move
details.ground_truth: In an attempt to bury a relic of its past, Vanderbilt University announced Monday that it will pay more than $1 million to remove the word "Confederate" from one of its dorms, the Tennessean reports. Chancellor Nicholas S. Zeppos called the inscription on Confederate Memorial Hall "a reminder of racism, slavery and a very, very bloody Civil War." The Nashville university's efforts to change the name in 2002 were halted when the United Daughters of the Confederacy sued. The group’s $50,000 donation in 1933 helped build the dorm. A state appeals court ruled the building could be renamed Memorial Hall only after Vanderbilt gave the United Daughters back their money—$1.2 million in today’s dollars. The university has the cash, thanks to a raft of anonymous contributors who wanted the tie to America’s painful past broken for good. "It's a symbol that is, for many people, deeply offensive and painful," Zeppos told the Tennessean. "And to walk past it or to have to live in that space is really something that I just don't think is acceptable.” Yet tampering with Confederate imagery remains controversial, and a new state law makes it harder to do so, the AP reports. Efforts by Middle Tennessee State University in Murfreesboro to remove the name of Confederate Gen. Nathan Bedford Forrest from a building could face a tougher climb under the Tennessee Heritage Protection Act, which now requires a vote of two-thirds of the state’s Historical Commission rather than a simple majority.

**Florida Police Officer's Selfless Act: Replacing Stolen Engagement Ring for Elderly Woman**

In a heartwarming display of kindness and dedication to duty, a Florida police officer, Laurie Graber, went above and beyond the call of duty to replace a stolen engagement ring for an elderly woman, Betty Wagner, who suffers from Alzheimer's disease.

The story began when Arthur Wagner, Betty's husband, reported to the police that his wife's engagement ring had been stolen while she was being kept sedated in a hospital bed at Westside Regional Medical Center in Plantation. The ring, which had been on Betty's finger for 67 years, was valued at around $4,500 in today's economy.

Officer Graber was sent to investigate the case and learned about the couple's long and loving relationship. She was deeply moved by the story and decided to take matters into her own hands. Graber purchased a small heart-shaped diamond ring from J.C. Penney and returned to the hospital, leaving the ring with the nursing staff and a signed note that read: "It's not much, it's not the same. But 67 years of a promise kept should be recognized."

The new ring has brought some comfort to Arthur Wagner, who was devastated by the loss of his wife's engagement ring. The police are still investigating the theft and are asking anyone with information to come forward.

Officer Graber's selfless act has been widely praised, and her dedication to her duty and her commitment to helping those in need is an inspiration to us all.
details.ground_truth: A classy move by a Florida cop is making headlines: Laurie Graber, an officer in Plantation, got the call for a particularly low-level crime—someone had ripped the engagement ring off the finger of an 87-year-old woman bedridden with Alzheimer's in a hospital, reports Fox News. By the bruises on Betty Wagner's finger, it wasn't an easy job, and no wonder—the ring had been there for 67 years. "I just couldn't imagine what kind of depravity you would have to have to take something off of someone so vulnerable," Graber tells WSVN. Upon leaving the hospital, Graber went to JCPenney and shelled out for a replacement ring with her own money. She brought it back to the nurses' station with a note, reports AOL.com: "It's not much, it's not the same. But 67 years of a promise kept should be recognized." Husband Arthur, who placed the original on his soon-to-be wife's hand in 1946, says the gesture has helped him deal with the theft. (Another Florida officer bought groceries for a mom caught shoplifting.)

**Boston Marathon Bomber Dzhokhar Tsarnaev's Fate: A Long and Winding Road to Execution**

In a recent development, lawyers for Boston Marathon bomber Dzhokhar Tsarnaev have appealed his conviction and death sentence, citing that the trial was held in a city where the bombing occurred, making it impossible for him to receive a fair trial. The lawyers argue that the definition of a "crime of violence" used in the trial is vague and throws many convictions into question. They also claim that the trial was saturated with prejudicial media coverage, events, and environment, including social media posts from friends and relatives of the victims.

Tsarnaev was sentenced to death in June for his role in the April 15, 2013, blasts that killed three people and injured and maimed more than 200 others. The appeal claims that the judge's rejection of the defense's request for a change of venue before the trial began was a mistake. The defense also argues that capital punishment is unconstitutional, citing a recent dissent by U.S. Supreme Court Justice Stephen Breyer.

Despite the dramatic news from the court, Tsarnaev's fate will take years to reach its conclusion. Only three of 340 prisoners sentenced to federal death row have been executed in the last 50 years. Tsarnaev will join the group of men and women awaiting execution on federal death row, and his team of legal defenders has already indicated two strong lines of likely appeal: that the trial should never have been held in Boston and that the defense was given insufficient time to mount a full argument in mitigation.

The involvement of the federal government in the death penalty has become increasingly unusual in modern times, and the application of capital punishment federally has been even more maverick than by individual states. Almost half of Tsarnaev's new federal death row peers were put there by just three states: Texas, Missouri, and Virginia. The majority of the 62 inmates were put there for reasons that seem almost tangential, such as a murder conducted on federal land or a gun used in a shooting carried across state lines.

The prisoner's team of legal defenders, led by Judy Clarke, will likely argue that the trial was unfair and that the death penalty is unconstitutional. The Justice Department could attempt to fast-track executions in the name of public interest, but death penalty experts expect the quickest timeframe from Friday's sentence to Tsarnaev actually being put on a gurney and injected with lethal chemicals would be at least 10 years.
details.ground_truth: Not a huge surprise: Lawyers for Boston marathon bomber Dzhokhar Tsarnaev today appealed his death-penalty conviction, arguing that "continuous and unrelenting publicity" biased jurors and made a fair trial in Boston impossible, NBC News reports. "Put simply, prejudicial media coverage, events, and environment saturated greater Boston, including the social networks of actual trial jurors, and made it an improper venue for the trial of this case," say the papers. The lawyers want another trial at a new location, the AP reports. Earlier this year, the Guardian reminded us that Tsarnaev's appeals will likely delay his punishment for "at least a decade." The April 15, 2013, bomb strike in Boston left three dead and more than 200 maimed or injured.

**$8.2 Billion Takeover: IntercontinentalExchange to Acquire New York Stock Exchange**

In a significant development in the global financial market, IntercontinentalExchange Inc. (ICE) is in talks to acquire the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE) in an $8.2 billion deal. The proposed merger would create a massive entity with 14 stock and futures exchanges and five clearing operations, making it the largest player in the industry.

The deal, which is expected to be announced as soon as Thursday morning, would see ICE offer about $33 a share for the NYSE, representing a premium of 37 percent to the exchange's closing stock price on Wednesday. Two-thirds of the offer would be in stock, with the remaining one-third in cash.

The NYSE, which has been the public face of the stock market for over two centuries, has seen its trading volume decline significantly in recent years. The exchange is now responsible for only about 11 percent of all stock trading, while its electronic Arca platform accounts for another 12 percent. In contrast, ICE has been growing rapidly and has some of the highest profit margins in the industry.

The takeover would mark a significant shift in the way financial markets are traded, with the rise of electronic trading over the traditional "open outcry" floor trading. It would also seal the triumph of exchanges that have been embracing new and innovative technologies to stay ahead of the competition.

The deal is expected to face regulatory and shareholder approval, but it is likely to be cleared given the significant benefits it would bring to both parties. The takeover would link NYSE Euronext to one of the industry's fastest-growing exchanges, while ICE would gain access to a new and lucrative market.

Other potential spoilers, including the CME Group and the Hong Kong and Singaporean exchanges, may run into antitrust concerns and nationalist concerns, respectively. However, it is unlikely that they would be able to block the deal given the significant benefits it would bring to the industry as a whole.

The shares of NYSE Euronext rose more than 21 percent in after-hours trading, to $29.20, after the news of the talks was reported.
details.ground_truth: InterContinentalExchange Inc. has agreed to buy NYSE Euronext, the company that owns the New York Stock Exchange, for $8.2 billion, the two companies announced this morning. ICE, an Atlanta-based commodity exchange, says it intends to leave the NYSE's branding alone, and would explore an IPO for Euronext, spinning it off into a continental European entity, the Wall Street Journal reports. It will pay $33.12 a share for the elder exchange, or about a 37% premium on yesterday's close. You might remember ICE from its last attempt to buy the NYSE, which failed thanks to antitrust concerns from regulators. While the 12-year-old company may lack the name recognition of the iconic NYSE, it's much larger in terms of market capitalization, at $9.3 billion to NYSE Euronext's $5.8 billion—a sign, the New York Times observes, of how completely commodity trading has outpaced stock trading in relevance.

**Academy of Motion Pictures Arts and Sciences Takes Steps to Address Diversity Concerns**

In a significant move to address the ongoing diversity problem in the film industry, the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences (AMPAS) has sent out an unprecedented 683 membership invitations to film industry professionals. The list, which is the largest and most diverse class to date, includes a wide range of individuals from various backgrounds and industries.

The AMPAS has faced criticism in the past for its overwhelmingly older, male, and white membership, and the #OscarsSoWhite movement, created by April Reign, has been a major catalyst for change. In response, the AMPAS has committed to doubling the number of women and people of color in its ranks by 2020.

The list of invitees includes a number of notable directors, including Catherine Breillat, Park Chan-wook, and Lucrecia Martel, as well as actors such as Luis Guzmán, Idris Elba, and Michael B. Jordan. The list also includes a number of international heavyweights and up-and-coming talent, highlighting the AMPAS's efforts to expand its reach and diversity.

The move is seen as a significant step towards addressing the diversity problem in the film industry, and is likely to have a positive impact on the representation and inclusion of underrepresented groups in the industry. The AMPAS's commitment to change and its efforts to diversify its ranks are a welcome development, and are likely to be seen as a model for other industries and organizations.

**Key Statistics:**

* 683 membership invitations sent out by the AMPAS
* 41% of invitees are people of color
* 50% of invitees are women
* List includes a wide range of directors, actors, and industry professionals
* AMPAS has committed to doubling the number of women and people of color in its ranks by 2020

**Notable Invitees:**

* Directors: Catherine Breillat, Park Chan-wook, Lucrecia Martel, and many others
* Actors: Luis Guzmán, Idris Elba, Michael B. Jordan, and many others
* International heavyweights: Souleymane Cissé, Hou Hsiao-Hsien, and many others
* Up-and-coming talent: Ryan Coogler, Ramin Bahrani, and many others
details.ground_truth: Apparently still smarting from some of Chris Rock's Oscars barbs, the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences invited 683 people—many of them women and minorities—to join in an unprecedented move Wednesday, Reuters reports. The voting group behind the Oscars is largely old, white, and male and was lambasted this year with the hashtag #OscarsSoWhite after two years in a row of all-white acting nominees. In response, the academy is attempting to—as the AV Club puts it—" add seemingly goddamn everyone it had, for one reason or another, forgotten to invite into its membership.” The actors, directors, and others invited Wednesday include Idris Elba, Eva Mendes, Oscar Isaac, John Boyega, Ice Cube, Greta Gerqig, Michael B. Jordan, Vivica A. Fox, the Wachowskis, James Wan, Luis Guzmán, Kate Beckinsale, Park Chan-wook, James Hong, Michelle Rodriguez, and not one, not two, but three Wayans brothers. Of the new invitees, 46% are women and 41% are people of color. If all 683 accept their invite, women would account for 27% of the more than 7,000 academy members (up from 25%) and minorities would total 11% (up from 8%). “I'm especially happy to be part of such a diverse group. I actually want to hang out and watch movies with most of the people on this list," Arab-German director Lexi Alexander tells the Los Angeles Times. “To be honest, I cried a few tears when I started to get congratulation tweets in Arabic.” Other invitees took to Twitter to share similar sentiments. “Excited to use my vote to nominate talent that reflects the real world we live in—DIVERSITY," tweets Brie Larson, who won best actress at this year's Oscars.
