**Iran-Iraq Border Dispute: Trial of American Hikers Delayed Amidst Nuclear Talks and Prisoner Swap Proposals**

The trial of two American hikers, Shane Bauer and Josh Fattal, has been delayed by Iranian authorities due to the absence of a third suspect, Sarah Shourd, who was released on bail in September 2010. The hikers were arrested near the Iraqi border in July 2009 and accused of espionage, a charge they deny. The delay comes as Washington and other powers are trying to prod Tehran to return to talks about its nuclear activities, which they fear are aimed at making an atomic bomb.

Iran, which says its nuclear ambitions are peaceful, has said it is ready to restart the talks which stalled last year, but has yet to agree a date and has even suggested the talks should not address the nuclear issue. President Mahmoud Ahmadinejad has suggested that the Americans might be released as part of a prisoner swap for Iranians he says have been abducted or tricked into going to the United States and jailed without due legal process.

Meanwhile, a woman jailed for trafficking defense hardware to Iran has given a telephone interview to Iran's state-run English language television channel, in which she said she had been tortured. Foreign Ministry spokesman Ramin Mehmanparast said he hoped publicity about the case would help secure the release of Iranian prisoners in U.S. jails.

The American hikers' story has been disputed by Iranian authorities, who claim that they were captured in Iraq, not Iran. However, the hikers' lawyer and the U.S. State Department have maintained that they do not know how their arrest happened. The hikers' account of their arrest, which began as a relaxed overnight camping trip in the Kurdish mountains, has been corroborated by a news report that a shot had been fired over their heads was wrong, and that they were instead approached by a guard and blocked from attempting to flee.

The hikers' mother, who sat with them during a news conference in Tehran in May 2010, has said that they were extremely unlucky and that their one mistake was hiking too far. The case has highlighted the tensions between the United States and Iran, and the complexities of the nuclear talks and prisoner swap proposals.
details.ground_truth: The three American hikers detained by Iran weren’t captured in Iraq, says Sarah Shourd: They were beckoned over the border by an armed soldier, she tells the New York Times in an effort to set the WikiLeaks version of the story straight. “We did not actually enter Iran until he gestured to us. We were confused and worried and wanted to go back." In Shourd's fullest account of her ordeal to date, she goes into detail about their fateful overnight camping trip, describing "scores of campfires" near the waterfall they were hiking to—which abutted Iran. “I think we were extremely unlucky,” says Shourd. “I guess I never believed there would be so many hundreds of people close to a border.” Her interview with the Times comes ahead of a court date for her fellow hikers, which was set for Saturday but has been delayed by Iran until Shourd “can return to Iran or,” if she refuses, her case can “be dealt with differently,” said an Iranian official. But her lawyer says the trial should go forward; neither he nor Shourd, he tells Reuters, were notified of the change.

**Mali Coup: Soldiers Seize Control, Oust President**

In a shocking turn of events, soldiers in Mali, a country in West Africa, have stormed the presidential palace and announced that they have taken control of the country. The coup, which occurred on March 22, 2012, marks a major setback for one of the region's few established democracies.

The soldiers, who staged a mutiny on Wednesday, claim that the government is not giving them enough arms to tackle a rebellion by ethnic Tuaregs in the north. The Tuaregs, a traditionally nomadic people, have risen up against the central government in Mali several times since the country's independence from France in 1960. The newest rebellion, which began in mid-January, has been fueled by the return of Tuaregs from Libya who had fought in Gadhafi's army.

The ousted president, Amadou Toumani Toure, was due to step down next month after two, five-year terms. His whereabouts are unknown, and a soldier at the palace said that the president's bodyguards had failed to fight the renegade soldiers, who burst in and searched the grounds looking for him.

The West African regional bloc ECOWAS has condemned the actions of the troops, describing them as "reprehensible" and "misguided." The US and France have also urged the soldiers and government to resolve their dispute through peaceful means.

The coup has sparked widespread unrest in the country, with gunfire and rioting reported in several parts of Bamako, the capital city. The situation is currently unclear and unfolding quickly, with reports of military forces surrounding the presidential palace and movement of vehicles between the palace and the military barracks.

The leader of the mutiny, Capt. Amadou Sanogo, has imposed a national curfew and announced that the constitution has been suspended. A spokesman for the renegade soldiers, Lt. Amadou Konare, condemned the "inability" of the government to "fight terrorism" and said the soldiers would look to hand over to a democratically elected government.

The exact whereabouts of President Toure are not known, and a loyalist military source close to the president told the AFP news agency that he was well and in a safe location. However, it is unclear whether the mutinous soldiers have complete control of the presidential palace or have the support of all the Malian forces.

Mali has had democratic rule for the last 20 years, during which it has come to be seen as a model which
details.ground_truth: A military coup is under way in Mali, as renegade soldiers stormed the presidential palace in the capital of Bamako today, and soon afterward appeared on state TV to announce that they had booted the president and taken over the country, the BBC reports. The soldiers said they were suspending the constitution, dissolving its institutions and instituting a nationwide curfew. They said they were taking power because of President Amadou Toumani Toure's failure to defeat a rebellion by the Taureg tribes in the north, the AP reports. The military “has decided to assume its responsibilities and end the incompetent and disavowed regime of Amadou Toumani Toure,” the troops said, adding that it “does not in any way aim to confiscate power, and we solemnly swear to return power to a democratically elected president as soon as national unity and territorial integrity are established.” The mutiny comes just a month before Toure was set to leave office legally.

**Game of Thrones Actor Dean-Charles Chapman's Hidden Role Revealed**

Dean-Charles Chapman, the actor who played King Tommen Baratheon in Game of Thrones, has a surprising secret: he played another character in the show's universe before taking on the role of the king. Chapman's character, Martyn Lannister, appeared in Season 3, Episodes 3 and 5, and was a squire in the Lannister army who was taken prisoner by Edmure Tully at the Battle of Stone Mill.

Martyn's storyline was short-lived, as he and his brother Willem were killed off by Lord Rickard Karstark in revenge for Jaime Lannister killing his own son. This plot twist is significant because it reveals that Martyn was a son of Kevan Lannister, Tywin's younger brother, making him a cousin of Tommen Baratheon.

The discovery of Chapman's hidden role has sparked a new wave of interest in the show's complex family dynamics and the Lannister family's penchant for incest. While the "Tommen is a faceless man" theory is likely nonsense, it's clear that Chapman's ability to play multiple characters in the show's universe is a testament to his talent as an actor.

The revelation of Chapman's hidden role is a reminder that even the most dedicated fans of the show may have missed important details in the past. With the final season of Game of Thrones now behind us, it's a good time to revisit the show's complex history and appreciate the talented actors who brought it to life.

**Key Takeaways:**

* Dean-Charles Chapman played Martyn Lannister in Season 3, Episodes 3 and 5.
* Martyn was a squire in the Lannister army who was taken prisoner by Edmure Tully at the Battle of Stone Mill.
* Martyn and his brother Willem were killed off by Lord Rickard Karstark in revenge for Jaime Lannister killing his own son.
* Martyn was a son of Kevan Lannister, Tywin's younger brother, making him a cousin of Tommen Baratheon.
* The discovery of Chapman's hidden role has sparked a new wave of interest in the show's complex family dynamics and the Lannister family's penchant for incest.
details.ground_truth: The internet loves obsessing over Game of Thrones, so Hello Giggles wants to know why more people aren't talking about the fact that "Tommen is also his own cousin." Dean-Charles Chapman currently plays King Tommen Baratheon on the show, a role he took over from a younger actor in 2014. But he also played Tommen's cousin, Martyn Lannister, in a few episodes of the show back in the third season, Mashable explains. Hello Giggles thinks the recasting makes complete sense. “Given the Lannister family’s penchant for incest, having several people in that lineage look the same (like exactly the same) would not be a surprise.” Poor Martyn was killed off after being taken hostage by Robb Stark, and Bustle thinks Chapman better hope for a third role as Tommen is "probably doomed just like his look-alike cousin."

**House Intelligence Committee Votes to Release Secret Memo Alleging FBI Misconduct**

The House Intelligence Committee voted along party lines to release a classified memo that alleges misconduct by senior FBI officials involved in the Russia probe. The memo, written by Republicans, claims that the FBI and the Justice Department improperly used government surveillance during the investigation into Russian election interference and contacts with President Donald Trump's campaign.

**White House Favors "Full Transparency" but Has Not Made a Decision**

The White House has stated that President Donald Trump favors "full transparency" for the American people, but has not made a decision on whether to release the memo. White House press secretary Sarah Huckabee Sanders said that "no one" at the White House has seen the memo, so the president was not prepared to make a decision.

**Two Republican Senators Urge Trump to Keep a Public Silence on the Investigation**

Two Republican senators, Lindsey Graham of South Carolina and Susan Collins of Maine, have urged President Trump to keep a public silence on the investigation into his 2016 campaign's contacts with Russia. They also urged special counsel Robert Mueller to review whether Trump tried to fire him last June.

**House Panel Votes to Release Secret Memo Despite DOJ Objections**

The House intelligence committee voted to release the secret memo despite objections from the Department of Justice. The memo has been a source of escalating partisan tension in Congress, with Republicans arguing it shows wrongdoing by FBI officials and Democrats saying it mischaracterizes intelligence and is an attempt to distract from special counsel Robert Mueller's probe.

**FBI Director Christopher Wray Reviewing the Memo but Has Not Been Satisfied**

FBI Director Christopher Wray has reviewed the memo but has not been satisfied with its contents. He has asked for the opportunity to come before the committee and express his concerns, which were not alleviated by his review of the memo.

**Trump Now Has Five Days to Object to the Committee's Request or Clear the Way for the Memo's Release**

The White House has stated that the President will be subjected to appropriate and serious review before a decision is made on the release of the memo. Trump now has five days to either object to the committee's request or clear the way for the memo's release.

**The Memo Accuses Senior FBI Officials of Abusing a Classified Surveillance Program**

The memo accuses senior FBI officials of abusing a classified surveillance program, known as FISA, to spy on a Trump campaign foreign policy adviser. Obtaining a warrant under FISA requires the FBI to present an application to a court with evidence that indicates the target is acting
details.ground_truth: It's all up to President Trump now. The House Intelligence Committee voted along party lines Monday to release a controversial memo that alleges wrongdoing on the part of the Justice Department and the FBI in regard to the Russia investigation, reports Politico. Democrats are fuming over the decision. Trump now has five days to decide whether to allow the memo to be released; his approval is necessary because it's a classified document. The memo alleges that the FBI and the Justice Department improperly used government surveillance during the investigation into Russian election interference and contacts with Trump's campaign, per the AP. The vote to release the memo comes after committee Republicans, led by chairman Rep. Devin Nunes, pushed for its disclosure. The memo addresses a dossier of allegations against Trump compiled by a former British spy, and questions over whether it was improperly used to obtain surveillance warrants. The panel's ranking Democrat, Rep. Adam Schiff, announced the vote results Monday while criticizing the Republicans' decision. Democrats generally say the memo cherry-picks facts and presents a distorted view of things.

**Trade War Escalation: US and China Lock in a High-Stakes Dispute**

The ongoing trade war between the United States and China has reached a boiling point, with both sides imposing and threatening to impose new tariffs on each other's goods. The Trump administration plans to raise the pending tariffs on $200 billion in Chinese goods to 25% from 10%, a move that China's government has vowed to retaliate against with the same amount of tariffs on US products.

The escalation of the trade war has sparked concerns about a global economic downturn, with the International Monetary Fund warning that a full-blown trade war could undermine the strongest economic upswing in years. US stocks have closed higher on the prospects of an easing in trade tensions, with industrial shares such as Boeing, Caterpillar, and 3M Co. adding more than 1%.

Despite the tensions, there are signs that the two sides are trying to restart talks aimed at averting a full-blown trade war. Representatives of US Treasury Secretary Steven Mnuchin and Chinese Vice Premier Liu He are having private conversations as they look for ways to reengage in negotiations. However, the talks have been stalled for weeks, with both sides refusing to budge.

The trade war has also had a significant impact on the global tech industry, with the US government imposing a ban on the Chinese telecom equipment maker ZTE Corp. The ban was later lifted, but the incident has highlighted the tensions between the two countries.

In a sign of the trade standoff's impact on Chinese politics, the Politburo signaled that policy makers will focus more on supporting economic growth amid risks from a campaign to reduce debt and the dispute with Trump. The communique, which followed a meeting of the country's 25 most senior leaders led by Xi, said the nation's campaign to reduce leverage will continue at a measured pace while improving economic policies to make them more forward-looking, flexible, and effective in the second half of 2018.

The trade war has also brought the Trump administration into conflict with its allies, with the US imposing tariffs on steel and aluminum imports from Canada, Mexico, and the European Union. The move has sparked a global backlash, with the EU and other countries imposing their own tariffs on US goods.

As the trade war continues to escalate, it remains to be seen whether the two sides can find a way to resolve their differences and avoid a full-blown trade war.
details.ground_truth: The US in early July hit $34 billion of Chinese goods with an extra 25% tariff; China responded in kind. CNN reports round two could see the US slap tariffs on another $16 billion in goods as soon as this week, but it's a much bigger figure that's attracting attention Wednesday. Bloomberg cites sources who say the White House is thinking about upping the ante and imposing not an additional 10% tariff on $200 billion worth of Chinese goods but an additional 25%. CNN echoes that news via its source. Bloomberg frames the move, which would be revealed in the next couple of weeks, as intended to "force officials back to the negotiating table through threats of even higher tariffs"; the last high-level negotiations took place nearly two months ago. A Chinese foreign ministry rep responded to the news thusly, per the AP: "If the United States takes further measures that escalate the situation, China will definitely fight back. We are determined to safeguard our legitimate and lawful rights and interests."

**PlayStation 4: A Next-Gen Gaming Experience**

Sony's highly-anticipated PlayStation 4 (PS4) has finally been unveiled, marking a significant shift in the gaming industry. The next-gen platform is designed to focus on the gamer, providing an immersive and social experience like never before.

**Key Features and Upgrades**

- The PS4 is built on an x86 PC architecture, making it easier for developers to create games and reducing the risk of hardware-related issues.
- An 8-core combined CPU/GPU and 8 GB of unified GDDR5 memory provide a significant boost in performance and graphics quality.
- A local HDD and the possibility of solid-state storage ensure that games load quickly and smoothly.
- The DualShock 4 controller features a touchpad, a share button, a headphone jack, and a light bar that tracks movement with a PlayStation Move-style controller.
- Background downloading and save states allow for seamless and quick gameplay experiences.
- Social features include the ability to quickly pause and upload gameplay videos, spectator functionality for watching "celebrities" gaming, and real-name-based networking.

**Graphics and Performance**

- Demos of Unreal Engine 4, Killzone, and Driveclub showcased impressive graphics and fluid performance, on par with high-end PCs.
- Realtime facial detail and lighting effects were particularly impressive, with some scenes that could only have been pre-rendered on a PS3 or Xbox 360.
- The PS4's graphics are fantastic, but not a revolutionary leap like the first time you saw Sonic Adventure.

**Backwards Compatibility and Streaming**

- No native PS3 backwards compatibility, but PlayStation Cloud will allow streaming access to old titles without the need for discs.
- A dedicated video-processing chip will enable streaming video from your gaming session without leaving your session.
- UStream integration will allow for public performances and remote gameplay assistance.

**Remote Play and Personalization**

- Remote play will continue where you left off on another screen, with the Vita being the go-to small-screen device.
- Personalization will study your downloading and playing habits to predict the next titles you'll want and download them ahead of time.

**Conclusion**

The PlayStation 4 is a significant step forward in the gaming industry, providing a more immersive, social, and convenient experience for gamers. With its impressive graphics, upgraded features, and innovative social features, the PS4 is set to be a major player in the gaming world.
details.ground_truth: Sony today unveiled its next-generation gaming system, PlayStation 4, saying the console will be part of a new ecosystem focused on hardware, software, and "the fastest, most powerful gaming network." The new console is the Japanese electronics giant's first major game machine since the PlayStation 3 went on sale in 2006. Today's unveiling is Sony's attempt to steal the spotlight, at least until Microsoft unveils its next Xbox in June at the E3 video game expo in Los Angeles. Among the PS4's revisions is an updated controller that adds a touchpad, motion control, and a "share" button. The controller also features a light bar, which means a new PlayStation camera can more easily track the device. Sony has struggled lately to keep up with Microsoft and other rivals such as Apple and Samsung. The company is promising nifty mobile devices, sophisticated digital cameras, and other gadgetry as part of its comeback effort. More details at Gizmodo and TechCrunch.

Researchers from the University of California, Berkeley, have made significant strides in brain-computer interface technology, allowing them to reconstruct and decode visual experiences from brain activity. Two recent studies, published in Current Biology and led by Shinji Nishimoto and Jack Gallant, have demonstrated the potential of this technology to read our minds, decode our dreams, and even predict our intentions.

**Reconstructing Visual Experiences**

In the first study, researchers used functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) to record the brain activity of participants as they watched YouTube videos. They then developed a computer model that matched features of the videos, such as colors, shapes, and movements, with patterns of brain activity. By running the brain activity through the model, they were able to reconstruct a continuous, if abstract, version of the actual videos. This is a significant breakthrough, as it demonstrates the ability to decode dynamic visual experiences, which has long been a challenge in the field.

**Decoding the Mind's Eye**

In the second study, researchers took it a step further by decoding the mind's eye, allowing them to see through another person's eyes. They used a similar approach to the first study, but this time, they were able to reconstruct and decode the visual experiences of the participants in real-time. The resulting videos are blurry and dream-like, but they demonstrate the potential of this technology to read our minds and decode our visual experiences.

**Potential Applications and Implications**

The implications of this technology are far-reaching and have the potential to revolutionize various fields, including medicine, education, and law. For example, people who are paralyzed may be able to control their environment by imagining sequences of movements. Additionally, the technology could be used to decode memories and predict intentions, which may have significant implications for detective work and court cases.

However, the technology also raises significant ethical and privacy concerns. As Gallant noted, an accurate read-out of a faulty memory only provides misleading information, and the potential to watch a person's memories may not be so far off. As we continue to develop and refine this technology, it is essential that we consider the potential implications and ensure that we use it in a responsible and ethical manner.

**Conclusion**

The recent breakthroughs in brain-computer interface technology, led by researchers from the University of California, Berkeley, have significant implications for our understanding of the brain and its functions. The ability to reconstruct and decode visual experiences, as well as decode
details.ground_truth: Scientists at UC Berkeley have made a major advancement in the field of mind reading, reconstructing YouTube videos based on brain scans from people who’d seen them. Researchers would put subjects into an MRI machine and track their brain activity as they viewed videos. Once they’d build a model of how a subject’s mind processed the video, “we could read brain activity for that subject and run it backward … to try to uncover what the viewer saw,” one study coauthor tells ABC News. Using the scans they were then able to reproduce the videos—though they’re blurry. “This is a major leap,” the co-author says. He thinks the technique could eventually be used to reconstruct dreams or memories, if it turns out the brain processes those things similarly to how it processes movies. “It’s our next line of research.” (More details on how the experiment worked, along with a video, at PC Magazine.)

**Congressional Inaction and Sequestration Looms**

The US Congress is currently in a state of deliberate idleness, with House Republicans pursuing a strategy of inaction to avoid a tax increase and instead reap a 2.5% cut in federal spending. The sequestration, a set of automatic automatic cuts, is set to take effect on March 1, and will impact various government agencies, including NASA, with a $894 million cut from an already strapped budget.

In a move seen as ironic, the House of Representatives voted to rename the Dryden Flight Research Center as the Neil A. Armstrong Flight Research Center, honoring the first man to walk on the moon. The bill, sponsored by Majority Whip Kevin McCarthy (R-Calif.), passed the House in the last Congress by a vote of 404 to 0, but was not taken up by the Senate.

Meanwhile, politicians' attempts to frighten the public with the prospect of sequestration have fallen flat. Columnist Dana Milbank notes that Americans have survived past disasters, including Vietnam, Watergate, and Disco, and are not easily intimidated by the threat of sequestration. In fact, the salaries of senators and representatives will not be cut under sequestration, while their staffers face a 20% pay cut through furloughs.

The sequestration was designed to be so horrible that both parties would recoil from it, but it appears that Republicans are embracing it as a way to avoid a tax increase. The cuts will impact various government agencies, but will not stop members of Congress from ordering out and continuing to receive their six-figure salaries.

Overall, the current state of affairs in Washington is one of inaction and partisan posturing, with the sequestration looming large as a potential threat to the economy and government services.
details.ground_truth: We shouldn't be too worried about the sequester—after all, Congress doesn't exactly seem to be recoiling at the thought. And Roger Simon has a theory on why: Lawmakers' own salaries aren't getting cut, he explains at Politico. Sure, their staffers are facing furloughs that amount to a 20% pay decrease, but members of Congress will keep collecting six-figure salaries. Of course our lawmakers "might have to figure out how to put those plastic coffee pods in the machines themselves, but these people are not fools," he writes. "They will order out. If they can figure out how to work the telephones." In fact, House Republicans are positively excited about the sequester, writes Dana Milbank in the Washington Post, noting their agenda yesterday consisted only of renaming a California NASA research center. They're "pursuing a considered strategy of deliberate idleness," after finally realizing that doing nothing means they get "a 2.5% cut in all federal spending without coughing up a single dollar in tax increases. They have learned to stop worrying and love the sequester." Click for Milbank's full piece; Simon's humorous take is here.

**Headline:** Wells Fargo Scandal and Middle East Politics: A Week of Turbulent News

**Summary:**

This week, the US Senate witnessed a heated grilling of Wells Fargo CEO John Stumpf by Senator Elizabeth Warren over the bank's cross-selling scandal, which has resulted in the firing of over 5,300 employees and a $185 million penalty. Senator Warren called for a criminal investigation into the executives and demanded that Stumpf resign, return the money he made during the scam, and face criminal charges. She argued that the executives' actions were a clear case of fraud and that the only way to reform the industry is to hold executives accountable for their actions.

Meanwhile, in the Middle East, US President Donald Trump is expected to announce that he plans to recognize Jerusalem as Israel's capital, a decision that has been met with widespread criticism from the international community. The Pope, Palestinian leaders, and other world leaders have called on the US to respect the status quo in Jerusalem, which both Israelis and Palestinians claim as their capital. The decision is seen as a compromise, as Trump will still sign a six-month waiver to delay the move of the US embassy to Jerusalem. However, the move is likely to poison the well before peace talks have even begun and has sparked violent protests in the region.

The decision comes amidst a broader shift in Middle East politics, with Saudi Arabia and the US growing closer to Israel. The New York Times reported that Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman presented a peace plan to the Palestinian leader that would give the Palestinians a state but with limited sovereignty and noncontiguous parts of the West Bank. The plan would also leave Israeli settlements in the West Bank intact and deny the right of return for Palestinian refugees and their descendants.

details.ground_truth: Sen. Elizabeth Warren went after Wells Fargo CEO John Stumpf during a Senate hearing Tuesday with the kind of heat she usually reserves for Donald Trump tweetstorms. "You should resign ... and you should be criminally investigated," NPR quotes Warren as saying. Stumpf was in front of the Senate banking committee after Wells Fargo employees created millions of fake accounts for customers without their permission in order to charge them extra fees. Stumpf said he was "deeply sorry" for what happened, New Republic reports. But—according to CNBC—he maintained it wasn't a "scam"—Warren's word—but a "way of deepening relationships" with customers. Warren accused Stumpf of "gutless leadership" for passing responsibility for what happened away from himself and other senior executives while blaming "thousands of $12-an-hour employees who were just trying to meet cross-sell quotas that made you rich." The employees' cross-selling scheme was at least partially responsible for Wells Fargo stock going up, making Stumpf's shares alone gain $200 million in value. While more than 5,000 Wells Fargo employees were fired, none of the senior executives are losing their jobs. Warren said the only way Wall Street will be reformed is if CEOs like Stumpf start seeing jail time. She's one of only a few members of the Senate banking committee that hasn't gotten money from Wells Fargo's PAC.

**Netflix Hacker Hack Leaks "Orange Is the New Black" Season 5 Episodes**

A notorious hacker, known by the handle "thedarkoverlord," has released the first 10 episodes of the upcoming season 5 of Netflix's hit series "Orange Is the New Black" on the popular file-sharing site Pirate Bay. The leak is believed to have originated from a post-production studio, Larson Studios, which was attacked in late 2016. The hacker claims to have obtained unreleased shows from ABC, Fox, National Geographic, and IFC, and is demanding an unspecified ransom from Netflix to prevent the remaining three episodes from being released.

The leak is a significant blow to Netflix, which had set June 9 as the official release date for the new season. The company's stock price and subscriber growth may be affected by the piracy of one of its top shows. Netflix has stated that a production vendor used by several major TV studios had its security compromised, and the appropriate law enforcement authorities are involved.

The hacker, "thedarkoverlord," has a history of ransom demands and has claimed to have stolen series from other studios by breaking into a single company. In a message posted on Saturday, the hacker was arrogant and scolding, stating that Netflix would lose more money than the modest ransom demand. The company has not responded to a request for comment on the latest development.

The leak is the latest in a series of rumors and screenshots circulating online about a massive leak of Hollywood films and TV episodes. The hacker's renewed ransom demands are unclear, but Netflix is counting on "Orange" to help it add 3.2 million subscribers from April through June.
details.ground_truth: A hacker going by The Dark Overlord claims to have stolen and released 10 episodes of the new season of Orange Is the New Black more than a month before its official release on Netflix. Variety reports the hacker was demanding an unspecified ransom from Netflix, uploading the first episode of the upcoming fifth season to a file-sharing site Friday as proof. The next nine episodes were uploaded early Saturday morning after The Dark Overlord claims Netflix refused to pay them. The hacker says they're "quite ashamed to breathe the same air" as Netflix, as they were being entirely "reasonable and merciful" with their ransom offer. Neither Variety nor the AP have been able to—legally—confirm the authenticity of the episodes uploaded by The Dark Overlord. The new Orange Is the New Black episodes were apparently stolen months ago. Netflix says a production vendor "had its security compromised"; Variety identifies that vendor as Larson Studios, a post-production facility. The Dark Overlord says they weren't able to steal the final three episodes of the season because they weren't finished yet. The Dark Overlord told the AP in February they wouldn't release any of the stolen episodes; it's unclear why that changed. The hacker also claims to have stolen TV series from ABC, National Geographic, Fox, and IFC via the hack at Larson Studios. They're reportedly seeking a "modest" ransom for those as well. The leak of new Orange is the New Black episodes could hurt Neflix's subscriber numbers and stock price. The FBI is investigating.

**The Search for Malaysia Airlines Flight 370: A Statistical Approach**

The disappearance of Malaysia Airlines Flight 370 on March 8, 2014, remains one of the greatest aviation mysteries in history. Despite extensive searches, the plane's fate and location remain unknown. However, statistical tools, particularly Bayesian methods, can help refine and focus the search for the plane and provide a solution to the deepening mystery.

**Bayesian Methods in Search and Rescue**

Bayesian statisticians have a long history of helping in search and rescue operations. They use a method of updating probabilities based on new information, allowing them to assess various scenarios at once, even contradictory ones. This approach has been used in the search for U-boats in World War II, a U.S. submarine in the 1960s, and an Air France jet in 2011.

**The Air France Flight 447 Example**

In 2009, Air France Flight 447 went missing while flying from Rio de Janeiro to Paris. Debris from the plane was found five days later, but the mystery of the crash could only be answered by finding the black box and cockpit voice recorder. A team of statisticians from Metron Inc, led by Colleen Keller, was called in to help. They used Bayesian methods to assess the uncertainties of each piece of data and calculate the probability of the plane being found in each location. Their probability map gave a much tighter area to search, and a team was sent out to investigate. Although they were unable to find the plane initially, they were asked to make one last attempt to analyze the data. This time, they adapted their model to the possible scenario that neither the black box nor the voice recorder were sending a signal, and they were able to find the plane.

**The Challenges of the Malaysia Airlines Search**

The search for Malaysia Airlines Flight 370 is a complex and challenging operation. The area of the Indian Ocean where the plane is believed to have gone down is vast and unpredictable, with strong currents and deep waters. The search team has been using a variety of methods, including sonar and visual searches, but so far, no signs of the plane have been found. Colleen Keller, who led the team that found the Air France plane, is skeptical that the Malaysia Airlines plane will be found. "It's a big world out there," she says. "And I know people are saying - how could you possibly hide or not find a Boeing 777? But it's very likely if we don't get any breakthroughs
details.ground_truth: Forget satellite images and aerial searches—the best way to find Malaysia Airlines Flight MH370 may be with mathematical techniques dating back to the 18th century, the BBC reports. That's how Air France flight 447 was found in 2009, using "Bayesian statistics" to measure the probability of the plane being in one place or another. Named after Presbyterian minister and mathematician Thomas Bayes, the technique allowed experts to apply several factors to each point on a map: For example, what was the chance it crashed from mechanical failure? How far do planes tend to crash from their last known location? What was the chance that search teams missed debris in various locations? It's like picking a restaurant by balancing how full it is, what your favorite restaurant-review website says, and so on—except that experts hunting for Airbus A330 did that for each point where plane may have crashed in the Atlantic, Five Thirty-Eight notes. It was so hard that the US team of statisticians invited by France gave up, until they de-emphasized one statistic: that a plane's black box emits a signal after a crash 90% of the time. They changed their findings, and presto, the plane was found. Bayesian techniques have helped people find World War II U-boats, men overboard, and sunken treasure, but there's no evidence that Malaysia is employing them now. "I suspect that they just guess, like professional baseball managers used to do before Moneyball," says a biostatistician.

**Syrian Conflict Escalates: Twin Blasts Rock Damascus, Journalist Killed**

The Syrian civil war has intensified, with twin blasts targeting the army command headquarters in Damascus on Wednesday, sparking hours of sporadic gunbattles and a raging fire. The explosions, which were heard several miles away, shattered the windows of nearby buildings, including the Dama Rose hotel.

According to an army statement, the blasts were caused by a car bomb and an explosive device, with "terrorists" in the area opening fire randomly to terrorize people. The statement said a number of guards were wounded, but no military commanders or personnel were hurt.

However, Iranian Press TV reported that one of its correspondents, 33-year-old Maya Nasser, a Syrian national, died in an exchange of fire in the area following the blasts. Nasser was shot and killed by a sniper while reporting on air just hours ago.

The blasts are the latest in a series of attacks targeting security sites and symbols of regime power in a bid to turn the tide in the fighting. On July 18, rebels penetrated the heart of Syria's power elite, detonating a bomb inside a high-level crisis meeting in Damascus that killed three top regime officials.

The Syrian conflict has been the focus of attention at the U.N. General Assembly's annual meeting in New York this week, with U.N. Secretary-General Ban Ki-moon demanding international action to stop the war. However, a resolution aimed at pressuring Assad to end the violence and enter negotiations on a political transition has been vetoed by Russia and China.

In a stark contrast, President Barack Obama pledged U.S. support for Syrians trying to oust Assad, calling him a "dictator who massacres his own people."
details.ground_truth: Damascus has been rocked by bombings for the past two days, with at least two large blasts hitting Syria's military headquarters there this morning. The Free Syrian Army claimed responsibility for the attack, said to be the largest since July explosions killed President Bashar al-Assad's brother-in-law and other key aides; the BBC reports that Assad would have been able to hear today's explosions from his palace. Gunfire broke out afterward, and Maya Nasser, a TV correspondent for the Iranian Press TV, was shot dead by sniper fire during a live broadcast, report the New York Times and Press TV. Other government facilities were hit by bombs yesterday, proving the opposition can still get past government security, but the regime is downplaying the scope of the attacks while the opposition says many of Assad's forces have been killed. In other news from Syria: UN Secretary-General Ban Ki-moon called for international action at the General Assembly yesterday, calling the conflict "a regional calamity with global ramifications," the AP reports. Click for more from the General Assembly on Syria. Clashes have also been reported near Syria's border with Israel, leading the Israel Defense Forces to issue a statement declaring that "fire from Syria leaking into Israel will not be accepted."

**Breaking News: Decomposing Body Found in Lancaster Supermarket Column**

On August 11, 2018, a shocking and disturbing discovery was made at a WinCo Foods supermarket in Lancaster, California. A decomposing body was found stuck inside a brick pillar at the store, located in the 700 block of West Avenue K.

**Investigation and Discovery**

According to Los Angeles County Sheriff's Department officials, the manager of the store reported a strong smell from the column, which he initially thought was a sewage leak. A handyman and plumber were called to investigate, and they began removing bricks from the column. It was then that they noticed a shoe and leg inside the pillar.

**Autopsy and Identification**

The coroner's office later identified the body as Raymundo Rivera, a 35-year-old man. An autopsy was pending to determine the cause of death, but investigators believe it may be related to the suspect who escaped from a traffic stop on Monday night.

**Chase Suspect**

On Monday, a driver was pulled over for a fake license plate and crashed the car. The suspect then escaped onto the WinCo roof and disappeared. Investigators believe that the suspect may have fallen or gotten stuck in the pillar and was found five days later.

**Witness Accounts and Reaction**

Witnesses, including Henry Oliver, described the suspect's escape and disappearance. Customers were turned away from the store, and many were shocked and emotional by the discovery. Constance Fullwood, a regular customer, expressed her concern for the safety of others in the store.

**Investigation Ongoing**

The Los Angeles County Sheriff's Department's Homicide Bureau is leading the investigation and encourages anyone with information to contact them at (323) 890-5500. If you prefer to remain anonymous, you may call "Crime Stoppers" at (800) 222-TIPS (8477).

This disturbing and unusual discovery has left many in the community shaken and concerned for their safety. The investigation is ongoing, and we will provide updates as more information becomes available.
details.ground_truth: A strong smell outside a California supermarket turned out to be someone who had decomposed in one of the store's entrance columns, ABC 7 reports. Police were called Saturday to the WinCo Foods in Lancaster after a plumber, told it was likely a septic problem, helped knock brick from the column and saw a shoe and a leg inside. "It's been over 100 degrees up here every day," says LA County Sheriff's Lt. John Corina. "I can't imagine being inside that column and just baking. It's a strange development, and if he died that way, it's a horrible way to die." Police say the victim may be a man who was stopped for a fake license plate Monday night, NBC Los Angeles reports. He crashed the vehicle, ran away from deputies, and got into the supermarket. The man "ran up the steps where the managers [are]," an eyewitness tells CBS Los Angeles. "And somehow he got on the roof. Last I heard, he got away." From up there, he could have reached the store fascia and descended into the column. Whether he died from the fall or baked inside is unclear; he was found on his feet with an arm stuck behind his head. "I saw some gooey liquid and it smelled really foul ... it was oozing out of the pillar onto the pavement," says a witness. "It smelled like death."

**Sochi Winter Olympics Security Concerns: A Logistical and Diplomatic Challenge**

The upcoming Sochi Winter Olympics has raised concerns about security, with experts warning that a catastrophic terrorist strike could present a daunting challenge for the United States. The U.S. military has planned to use two warships in the Black Sea and planes on standby in Europe to evacuate American athletes, but any rescue operation would depend on the approval of Russian President Vladimir Putin, who sees the games as a matter of national pride.

The U.S. Olympic Committee has declined to discuss the details of security for American athletes, but some teams have taken matters into their own hands. The U.S. ski and snowboard team has hired a private security firm, Global Rescue, to provide additional protection in case of an emergency. The firm has six aircraft on standby and promises to provide communications help, "rally points" for athletes to shelter in place, and ways to get them around or out of Sochi.

Other countries' teams are also taking extra precautions. Britain has said it will send security staff to Sochi as part of its delegation, while Israel, which experienced a tragedy at the 1972 Summer Olympics, is confident that Russian security agencies will do their job properly. Germany and the Netherlands have also expressed trust in Russian authorities, but will be keeping a close eye on the situation.

Russia has introduced extensive safeguards to ensure security, including deploying 40,000 police officers and 70,000 soldiers from the 58th Army unit to patrol the nearby southern border with Georgia. President Putin has promised to protect the air and sea space, as well as the mountain cluster, and has vowed to ensure that the security measures do not "depress the participants in the Olympic Games."

The threat of terrorism is higher than at any previous Olympics, with several groups having threatened to attack the Sochi Olympics. The U.S. government has sent FBI agents to Sochi and Moscow for the duration of the games and has made air and naval assets available to help Russia prevent possible terror attacks.

As the games approach, athletes and their families are expressing concerns about the level of danger in Sochi. Some have told their families not to travel to Sochi to cheer them on, while others have purchased medical and evacuation insurance to be prepared in case of an emergency.

Ultimately, the security of the Sochi Winter Olympics will depend on the cooperation of the Russian government and the vigilance of athletes, teams, and security personnel.
details.ground_truth: The US has sent two warships to the Black Sea and put planes on standby in Europe as it prepares to evacuate its Olympians from Sochi in the event of an attack. But officials fear their preparations might not be enough, reports NBC News, which describes the effort as "logistically mind-boggling and diplomatically delicate." The big issue: The US can't do much without Russian permission, and it doesn't expect to get that. "Something that looks like the US cavalry riding to rescue Russia … well, it's just hard to imagine that happening," one Russian expert said. The US skiing and snowboarding teams have signed up their own private security company, Global Rescue, which claims it has six aircraft ready for an evacuation if necessary. But having planes isn't the only issue; it's having runways. Sochi's airport is fairly small, so deciding which of the 85 countries competing to use it would be a nightmare. Other teams are also taking special security precautions, the Moscow Times reports. Britain, for example, is sending extra security personnel with its delegation. A German spokesman, meanwhile, said the country trusted Russia, but would "look closely" at the situation. "We will have no aircraft, that is all that I can say."

**Robert Durst Sentenced to 7 Years in Prison, Faces California Murder Charge**

Real estate scion Robert Durst, 73, was sentenced to 7 years in prison for a federal gun crime in New Orleans, Louisiana. The judge, Kurt Engelhardt, agreed to recommend that Durst serve his time in California, where he faces a murder charge in connection with the 2000 death of his friend, Susan Berman.

Durst pleaded guilty to the gun charge in February and has been held in Louisiana since his March 2015 arrest. He is accused of killing Berman to keep her from talking to prosecutors about the disappearance of his first wife, Kathleen, in 1982.

Durst's attorneys have maintained that he is innocent and wants to prove it. They have requested that he be housed at a low-security federal prison facility in California, FCI Terminal Island, which has medical facilities near Los Angeles.

The deal with federal prosecutors, part of Durst's guilty plea, would have him back in California by August 18 for his arraignment in the murder case. However, Assistant U.S. Attorney Michael McMahon said it's likely Durst will leave within a few weeks for California.

Durst's sentence is part of an elaborate deal that resolves several pending charges against him in New York, Texas, and Louisiana state court. He has forfeited items found in his hotel room, including a flesh-colored latex mask, marijuana, and a loaded revolver.

The HBO series "The Jinx: The Life and Deaths of Robert Durst" documented investigations of Durst in connection with the dismemberment killing of a male neighbor in Texas in 2003, for which he was tried and acquitted of murder, and the 1982 disappearance in New York of his wife, Kathleen.

Durst's attorney, Dick DeGuerin, said the deal "cleared the decks, at a cost. It's a serious cost, but he's not facing any other prosecution except what's in California. That's what this is all about. We want to get to California and go to trial."

DeGuerin again insisted that Durst didn't kill Berman and doesn't know who did. He said Los Angeles prosecutors have not revealed any new evidence in the case but that he's confident Durst will beat the murder rap.

Renewed interest in Durst for Berman's killing stemmed from the conclusion of the HBO show. In the final episode, Durst made what some observers described as a confession while
details.ground_truth: A New Orleans judge officially approved Robert Durst's February plea deal on a gun charge and sentenced him to seven years in prison on Wednesday, Reuters reports. For more than a year, the real estate heir has languished in a New Orleans prison after being nabbed with a handgun in his hotel room. He was busted for that felony just one day before the series finale of HBO's The Jinx, in which he famously appeared to confess to three murders by saying, "What the hell did I do? Killed them all, of course." US District Judge Kurt Engelhardt also agreed that Durst can be transferred to a low-security prison with medical facilities in San Pedro, Calif., where the 73-year-old's attorneys say his "advanced age and poor health" can be better addressed, per the Advocate. In California, Durst will face a murder charge in the 2000 death of friend Susan Berman, the AP reports. (A Texas judge believes Durst once dumped a cat's head on her doorstep.)

**Benghazi Investigation Report Released, Criticizing Hillary Clinton's Handling of the Crisis**

The House Benghazi Select Committee has released a 800-page report on the 2012 attack in Benghazi, Libya, which killed four American personnel. The report criticizes former Secretary of State Hillary Clinton and other officials for not adequately heeding concerns about growing extremism in Libya. The report also accuses Clinton of falsely blaming the violence on an anti-Muslim video behind unrest elsewhere.

**Clinton's Private and Public Statements Contradict Each Other**

According to the report, Clinton's private and public statements on the attack contradict each other. In a private call with the President of Libya, Clinton made no mention of a protest or video, while in a public statement, she blamed the violence on the video. The report also notes that Clinton's public and private statements remained unchanged, despite changing intelligence information.

**State Department Officials Worried About Marine Uniforms During the Attack**

In a separate news story, it was revealed that State Department officials were worried about whether Marines that might have been deployed to Benghazi should wear uniforms, which could hurt diplomacy in the region. However, the military never deployed to Benghazi, and the clothing issue did not affect the speed or timeliness of any possible military response.

**Rep. Elijah Cummings Criticizes the Benghazi Investigation**

Rep. Elijah Cummings (D-Md.), the ranking Democrat on the House Benghazi Select Committee, criticized the investigation, calling it a "saddest exercise" he's ever engaged in. Cummings accused Chairman Trey Gowdy (R-S.C.) of trying to prevent accountability for the panel's GOP lawmakers and of hiding certain transcripts from the Democrats.

**The Internet Archive's Effort to Fix Broken Links**

In a related story, the Internet Archive is working to archive and preserve web pages, including those referenced by the Benghazi investigation report. The goal is to fix all broken links on the web by preserving the version of the page that was live when it was written.

Overall, the release of the Benghazi investigation report and the surrounding news stories highlight the controversy and criticism surrounding Hillary Clinton's handling of the crisis, as well as the challenges and criticisms faced by the investigation itself.
details.ground_truth: The Republican-led House Benghazi committee released its final report Tuesday after two years of investigations. The 800-page report lays out the committee's findings on the 2012 attacks in Libya that left four Americans dead. Here's what you need to know: Benghazi is "unlikely to be a potent tool for weakening Clinton" in the general election, as the committee and report have little credibility and aren't seen as serious by many, the Los Angeles Times reports. Easily distilling the massive report, the Washington Post lays out its five "most serious accusations," including that the CIA "missed warning signs" and "misread how dangerous Libya ... was at the time." New evidence of “culpability or wrongdoing” on Clinton's part is lacking in the report, despite it being “one of the longest, costliest, and most bitterly partisan congressional investigations in history," according to the New York Times. Given a lack of new evidence against Clinton, the Weekly Standard uses the report as a tool for blaming Obama. “The Obama administration knowingly provided the American people a false story about the Benghazi attack, its causes, and its consequences," the Standard states. How various government agencies responded to the Benghazi crisis is one of the more interesting aspects of the report. According to Politico, a conference call in the midst of the attack included a conversation about whether Marines should wear their uniforms if deployed. Marines changed in and out of their uniforms multiple times while waiting for an answer and were never deployed. According to the Hill, a Democratic lawmaker on the House Benghazi committee calls it "one of the saddest exercises I've ever engaged in" during 20 years in Congress. He accuses Republicans of hiding the final report from Democrats to avoid having it fact-checked. Zeroing in on what it calls the "most revealing paragraph" in the report, Vox concludes there's a pattern of spinning "non-damning facts as damning" for the Obama administration. The paragraph in question admits there were no US forces close enough to prevent the attack but insists that, in itself, was a failure on the part of the White House. "I think it's time to move on," the AP quotes Clinton as saying after the release of the report, which she says includes nothing not previously discovered by an accountability board.

**Hannah Graham Case: New Developments and Pretrial Hearing**

A pretrial hearing for Jesse LeRoy Matthew Jr., the man accused of killing University of Virginia student Hannah Graham, took place on Monday. The hearing saw the testimony of several witnesses, including former Louisa County Sheriff's Office Detective Buck Garner, who revealed new details about the investigation.

**Key Testimony and Evidence**

* Former Detective Buck Garner testified that a police bloodhound, named Shaker, detected traces of Hannah Graham's scent at an industrial site about a mile from downtown Charlottesville, near a mulch pile where the dog indicated Graham may have been attacked.
* The bloodhound also found Graham's scent on the passenger door of Matthew's car, in the doorway to his home, and near a dumpster at his apartment complex.
* Surveillance video from businesses showed Graham walking alongside Matthew, and her remains were found six weeks later on an abandoned property in Albemarle County.
* The bloodhound's work provided the basis for police to obtain a search warrant for Matthew's apartment and vehicle.

**Challenges to the Search Warrant**

Defense attorney Doug Ramseur argued that the search dog's findings were inconsistent with witness accounts and video surveillance evidence, and that detectives might have misled a magistrate to obtain the warrant. However, Albemarle County Circuit Judge Cheryl Higgins ruled that there were no "instances of deliberate misstatements and reckless disregard" of the truth in the affidavit supporting the search warrant.

**Additional Charges and Background**

Matthew faces capital murder charges in Graham's 2014 disappearance and death, as well as first-degree murder charges in the 2009 death of Virginia Tech student Morgan Harrington. He is serving a life prison term for a 2005 attempted murder and sexual assault in northern Virginia.

The pretrial hearing is a significant development in the case, and the new testimony and evidence may shed more light on the events surrounding Graham's disappearance and death.
details.ground_truth: A former police bloodhound handler told a court on Monday that during the search for UVa student Hannah Graham, the animal detected her scent outside the car and apartment of suspect Jesse Matthew—and the scent of "fear and adrenaline" where she was allegedly attacked. The handler was testifying during a pretrial hearing where lawyers for Matthew, who is already serving three life terms in prison for a 2005 sexual assault, unsuccessfully challenged the search warrants police obtained for Matthew's home and vehicle, the AP reports. He said the 7-year-old dog, Shaker, traced Graham's scent from downtown Charlottesville to a mulch pile at an industrial site, where the smell of fear was strongest. Matthew, 33, faces a capital murder charge in the 2014 death of Graham, whose body was found on an abandoned property around six weeks after she disappeared. He has also been charged with the 2009 murder of a Virginia Tech student. At Monday's hearing, another police witness testified that, contrary to what the defense claimed, the scent trail was consistent with surveillance videos that showed Graham wandering around, reports WSET. The officer shared text message records indicating that she was lost the night she disappeared.The Washington Post notes that Matthew is seen walking alongside Graham in some of the surveillance videos.

**Convicted White-Collar Criminal Sentenced to Additional Time for Insurance Scam and Child Support**

A 43-year-old convicted white-collar criminal, Shannon Egeland, was sentenced to an additional three years and 10 months in prison for his role in a staged insurance scam and for failing to pay child support. Egeland, who was already serving a 10-year federal prison term for his involvement in a mortgage fraud scheme, orchestrated through the housing development company where he worked as vice-president, raised millions from local banks through a series of bogus business plans and falsified loan applications.

**The Staged Insurance Scam**

In an attempt to delay his prison sentence, Egeland directed his 17-year-old son, Rylan Egeland, to shoot him in the leg with a 20-gauge shotgun on July 31, 2014. The shooting, which resulted in the amputation of Egeland's left leg, was part of a staged insurance scam to collect disability benefits. A week before the shooting, Egeland had applied for a disability insurance policy, falsely claiming he had not been arrested in the past decade.

**The Consequences of the Staged Insurance Scam**

Prosecutors described Egeland as a "menace to society" and a "self-absorbed, opportunistic narcissist" motivated by greed who recklessly manipulated his son. The assistant US attorney, Scott Bradford, wrote in a sentencing memo that the psychological and emotional destruction Egeland caused his minor child is "unimaginable." If Egeland had died in the stunt, the minor child would have been left to deal with the consequences, Bradford added.

**The Sentencing Hearing**

During the sentencing hearing, Egeland stood on a prosthetic leg and expressed remorse for his actions. He said that what bothers him the most is his son, the pain is on him, and that he wishes he could take it all back but cannot. Egeland was also ordered to pay the $90,011 he owes in child support.

The additional sentence of three years and 10 months was tacked on to Egeland's existing 10-year sentence, bringing his total total to 13 years and 10 months in prison.
details.ground_truth: A white-collar crook is facing extra prison time for having his son shoot him in the legs with a 20-gauge shotgun, the BBC reports. Shannon Egeland, 43, was already facing 10 years for taking part in a vast mortgage-fraud scheme when he had his 17-year-old son shoot him by a road in Caldwell, Idaho, in an attempt to avoid prison. "The psychological and emotional destruction defendant caused this minor child is unimaginable," US attorney Scott Bradford wrote in a sentencing memo. Back in 2009, Egeland was co-owner of an Oregon development company that defrauded banks out of $20 million in loans that he and a partner used to fund a lavish lifestyle. Facing prison, Egeland took out disability insurance and had his son aim for his legs. Egeland told police he'd been attacked after stopping to assist a pregnant driver, but investigators got suspicious when his pricey BMW, his wallet, and his cellphone weren't taken, the Idaho Statesman reports. Then they learned about his recent insurance, and the whole thing unravelled. Now Egeland is facing an extra 3 years and 10 months for his roadside scheme and had his leg amputated from the shotgun blast. Bradford called him a "self-absorbed, opportunistic narcissist," the Oregonian reports, but Egeland—who is now on three medications for mental health—has cast his own judgment. "What bothers me the most is my son—the pain is on him,'' he says. "If I could take it all back, I would, but I can't. That will haunt me the rest of my life.''

**Tesla's Autopilot System Under Scrutiny After Fatal Crash**

A recent fatal crash involving a Tesla vehicle has raised concerns about the safety and reliability of the company's Autopilot system. The crash, which occurred on March 23rd, killed the driver, Walter Huang, a 38-year-old Apple engineer. Huang's family has reported that he had complained to the dealership about the Autopilot system veering towards the same barrier on multiple occasions before the accident.

According to Tesla, the Autopilot system was engaged with the adaptive cruise control follow-distance set to minimum at the time of the collision. The driver had received several visual and one audible hands-on warning earlier in the drive, but the vehicle logs show that no action was taken. The company has stated that the reason for the severity of the crash was the crushed crash attenuator, a highway safety barrier designed to reduce the impact into a concrete lane divider, which had not been replaced after a previous accident.

The National Transportation Safety Board (NTSB) is still investigating the accident, and the California Department of Transportation (CalTrans) has released a statement stating that replacement work was scheduled for the damaged barrier, but was delayed due to storms.

The incident has sparked renewed debate about the safety and reliability of autonomous vehicles and Autopilot systems. Tesla has argued that its Autopilot system makes the world safer for vehicle occupants, pedestrians, and cyclists, and that the company's data shows that Tesla owners have driven the same stretch of highway with Autopilot engaged over 85,000 times since its introduction in 2015.

However, critics have pointed out that the company's data is not comprehensive and that the Autopilot system is not foolproof. The incident has also raised questions about the responsibility of manufacturers and regulators in ensuring the safety of autonomous vehicles.

* 1.25 million automotive deaths worldwide worldwide
* 1 fatality every 86 million miles across all vehicles from all manufacturers in the US
* 1 fatality every 320 million miles in vehicles equipped with Autopilot hardware
* 3.7 times less likely to be involved in a fatal accident with a Tesla equipped with Autopilot hardware

**Key Quotes:**

* "We care deeply for and feel indebted to those who chose to put their trust in us." - Tesla
* "Walter was just a straight up, caring guy." - Shawn Price, friend of Walter Huang
* "The reason this crash was so severe is because the
details.ground_truth: The Tesla Model X that slammed into a highway barrier in California last week and killed the 38-year-old Apple engineer at the wheel had been on autopilot at the time of the crash, Tesla confirms in a blog post. But the company says driver Walter Huang "received several visual and one audible hands-on warning earlier in the drive," adding that "the driver’s hands were not detected on the wheel for six seconds prior to the collision." Tesla also laid some blame on state highway officials, asserting that "the reason this crash was so severe is because the crash attenuator, a highway safety barrier which is designed to reduce the impact into a concrete lane divider, had been crushed in a prior accident without being replaced." The fact that the vehicle was on autopilot is likely to be controversial because, as ABC News notes, driver Huang had reportedly been concerned about it. His brother told KGO that Huang had brought his Model X back to the dealership complaining that when he used the feature, the car kept swerving toward the very same barrier that it eventually struck in the fatal collision. The NTSB is investigating the crash, including the fire that broke out after the collision. The accident follows another in Arizona in which a self-driving Uber struck and killed a pedestrian.

**Robin Williams: A Legacy of Joy and Genius**

Renowned actor and comedian Robin Williams passed away at the age of 63, leaving behind a legacy of unforgettable performances and a profound impact on the entertainment industry. Williams was widely admired by his peers and was known for his generosity, humility, and willingness to take on challenging roles.

In the 1990s, Williams shifted his focus to family-friendly entertainment, starring in films like "Hook," "Aladdin," and "Mrs. Doubtfire." These movies not only became VHS classics but also left an indelible mark on the childhoods of those who grew up during that time. Williams' ability to erase his recognizable face and body, yet still convey a sense of fun and comfort, was a testament to his skill as a performer.

Throughout his career, Williams tackled a wide range of themes, from the complexities of adulthood in "Mrs. Doubtfire" to the struggles of growing up in "Jumanji" and "Jack." His performances were often marked by a sense of innocence and unselfishness, as he lowered himself to the level of his audience and made them laugh with what he found funny.

As the century turned, Williams returned to more serious projects, but the mid-budget adult drama was beginning to disappear. Despite this, he continued to reboot his children's movie career as Theodore Roosevelt in the "Night at the Museum" movies.

Williams' impact on the entertainment industry was not limited to his film work. As a stand-up comedian, he was a dazzling, unknown force in 1978, when a freelance journalist met him at the Comedy Store on Sunset Boulevard. Williams' routine was a masterclass in character work, accents, and lightning-fast comic connections. The journalist's encounter with Williams was a harbinger of the tsunami of fame that was headed his way.

In the years that followed, Williams became a household name, starring in films like "Good Morning, Vietnam," "Dead Poets Society," and "The Fisher King." His Oscar-nominated performances and eventual win for "Good Will Hunting" cemented his status as a formidable actor.

Despite his success, Williams' struggles with his fame and the pressures of the industry were evident. In a stage whisper, he once pleaded for help, echoing the doomed fly voice from the 1950s science fiction classic "The Fly." This moment, and others like it, revealed a restlessness and search for experience that characterized his comedy and physical presence.

As we remember Robin Williams, we are reminded
details.ground_truth: After Robin Williams' sudden death, the tributes are rolling in, with many recalling a comic genius grappling with private challenges. A sampling: When it comes to comedy, "there wasn't a faster brain on the planet," writes Tony Hicks in the Contra Costa Times. We'll be hearing "a litany of tributes about one of the great comedians who ever lived. Much of it will be some of the same adjectives used when any great performer dies—especially before their time. But in Williams' case, most of it will be true." Early in his career, "it was clear that Mr. Williams was one of the most explosively, exhaustingly, prodigiously verbal comedians who ever lived," AO Scott writes in the New York Times. And beyond that, "his essential persona as an entertainer combined neediness and generosity, intelligence and kindness, in ways that were charming and often unexpectedly moving." Williams may have gotten his start long before Aladdin, but he gave a "generous and humble" gift to the millennial generation, writes Daniel D'Addario at Salon. "Williams will be remembered for his edgy comedy and for dramatic turns, but for people my age, Williams was our childhood," from Mrs. Doubtfire to Jumanji to Flubber. In the Los Angeles Times, Kenneth Turan recalls a moment when, facing an onslaught of photographers, Williams asked him—in the voice of a fly from a 1950s sci-fi flick—to "help me, Ken. Help me." The experience stuck with him and seems particularly resonant today, he writes.

**Vermont Murder Suspect Remains on the Run**

A murder suspect, Jayveon Caballero, 29, of Barre, is still at large after a weekend shooting in Montpelier that left a former Vermont Frost Heaves basketball player, Markus Austin, 33, dead. The shooting occurred outside Austin's apartment at 191 Barre Street in Montpelier around 4:30 a.m. Sunday morning.

According to police, Caballero and Austin were involved in an altercation outside Gusto's bar in Barre earlier Sunday morning. During the altercation, Austin allegedly assaulted Caballero's girlfriend, Desiree Cary, 22. Caballero later confronted Austin outside the Montpelier apartment building and shot him.

Cary was taken into custody on Sunday evening in Barre on charges unrelated to the shooting, including selling crack cocaine and heroin. She was held in custody for lack of $10,000 bail and is scheduled to be arraigned in Washington County Superior Court Criminal Division.

In an attempt to locate Caballero, police raided his apartment early Monday morning but he was not there. Neighbors have reported dealing with constant problems from the property in the past, including drag racing and cars constantly coming and going.

Police believe Caballero may have boarded a bus on Sunday in White River Junction headed for New York City, but it is unclear if he got off at a stop before that. They are reaching out to other states to help locate him and are asking anyone with information about his location or the shooting to contact the Vermont State Police.

The shooting death of Austin is the first murder in Montpelier in almost a century, with the last recorded murder being in the 1920s when a woman shot her husband.

**Key Developments:**

* Arrest warrant issued for Jayveon Caballero on a murder charge
* Caballero may have fled the state, according to police
* Desiree Cary, Caballero's girlfriend, taken into custody on charges unrelated to the shooting
* Bail set at $500,000 for Caballero
* Police believe Caballero may have boarded a bus on Sunday in White River Junction headed for New York City
* The shooting death of Markus Austin is the first murder in Montpelier in almost a century
details.ground_truth: Police in the country's smallest state capital are investigating something that hasn't happened there in living memory: a murder. Police in Vermont say they have issued an arrest warrant for 29-year-old Jayveon Caballero, who is accused of fatally shooting Markus Austin in Montpelier early Sunday after an altercation, the Times Argus reports. Witnesses told investigators that Austin, 33, assaulted Caballero’s girlfriend, Desiree Cary, during the altercation outside a bar in the nearby city of Barre early Sunday. Police say Caballero later confronted Austin outside his apartment and shot him dead. The body was found in the parking lot of Austin's apartment complex. A Vermont State Police spokesman says Caballero, who knew Austin before the shooting, fled the town and probably the state after the shooting, WCAX reports. The police spokesman says Caballero is believed to have boarded a Greyhound bus bound for New York City on Sunday morning, which made numerous stops in other states along the way. Later Sunday, Cary, 22, was arrested on apparently unrelated charges of selling heroin and crack cocaine. With around 7,500 residents, Montpelier has the smallest population of any state capital, the AP notes. Police Chief Anthony Facos says this is the city's first murder since a woman shot her husband in the 1920s.

**Rare Celestial Event: Super Blue Blood Moon Total Eclipse on January 31, 2018**

On January 31, 2018, a rare and spectacular celestial event will occur: a Super Blue Blood Moon Total Eclipse. This event will be visible from eastern Asia, across the Pacific to western North America, and will be the first total eclipse of a Blue Moon in nearly 152 years.

**What is a Super Blue Blood Moon Total Eclipse?**

A Super Blue Blood Moon Total Eclipse occurs when the Moon is at its closest point to Earth (perigee), making it appear 14% bigger and 30% brighter than a regular full moon. The Moon will also be in its second full moon of the month, known as a Blue Moon. During the eclipse, the Moon will slowly lose its brightness and take on a reddish hue, often referred to as a "blood moon."

**When and Where to See the Super Blue Blood Moon Total Eclipse**

The best time to see the Super Blue Blood Moon Total Eclipse is right after moonrise and before sunrise, when the Moon is sitting on the horizon. Check your local moonrise time here. The eclipse will be visible from eastern Asia, across the Pacific to western North America, and will reach its maximum at 1:30pm UTC UTC.

**Livestream Options**

If you can't make it outside, you can still experience the Super Blue Blood Moon Total Eclipse with a livestream. The Virtual Telescope Project will provide a livestream of the eclipse from Australia, beginning at 11:30am UTC UTC.

**Photographing the Super Blue Blood Moon Total Eclipse**

To take a great picture of the Super Blue Blood Moon Total Eclipse, use the daylight white balance on your camera or smartphone to adjust for the sunlight being reflected off the Moon. Keep in mind that the Moon is a moving object, so you'll need to adjust your shutter speed and exposure accordingly. Consider putting the Moon in context with a local landmark to give your photo a sense of place.

**No Special Glasses Needed**

Unlike a solar eclipse, the Super Blue Blood Moon Total Eclipse is safe to view with the naked eye. So, grab a pair of binoculars or a telescope, and get ready to witness this rare and spectacular celestial event!
details.ground_truth: Look up at the sky on Jan. 31 and you may witness a sight unseen for 150 years. For the first time since 1866, a total lunar eclipse will occur with the blue moon, or second full moon of the month, which—like the one visible on New Year's Day—will also be a supermoon. Got all that? When the moon is at or near the closest point to Earth in its orbit (aka, a supermoon), it appears 14% bigger and 30% brighter than full moons that occur at the farthest point in the moon's orbit, though the moon will lose brightness as it enters Earth's shadow, according to NASA. Because of the way Earth's atmosphere bends light, the eclipsed moon will take on a reddish hue, something often referred to as a blood moon, making this rare celestial event "a super blue blood moon eclipse," according to the Miami Herald. The next blue moon eclipse won't occur until 2028. The eclipse will last for 3.5 hours, with the total eclipse stretching for 77 minutes, reports Sky & Telescope. Those along the Pacific Rim from Alaska and northwestern Canada to central and eastern Asia, Indonesia, New Zealand, and Australia will get the best view of totality during the middle of the night. However, partial phases of the eclipse should be visible under a clear sky in central North America, western Asia, India, the Middle East and eastern Europe, per Space.com. North American viewers should look up as the moon sets on the morning of Jan. 31, while those on the opposite side of the world should see a partial eclipse as the moon rises. Not in an ideal viewing area? The Virtual Telescope plans to livestream the total eclipse from Australia, which you can watch here, per Quartz. (Bogus glasses damaged a solar eclipse viewer's eyes.)
